title: feat(linux): new domain for parsed /proc and /sys system state
description:
Epic for a new sak domain emitting structured TSV/JSON for well-known `/proc` and `/sys` files. `sak fs read /proc/...` already covers the byte-level case, so this isn't a safety guardrail — it's a *parsing convenience*. The value-add is sparing LLMs from fragile `awk` against kernel-version-varying layouts.

Scope is a *curated set of parsers*, not a generic `/proc` walker. Anything that's a multi-column or key:value blob (`/proc/cpuinfo`, `/proc/meminfo`, `/proc/mounts`, `/proc/<pid>/status`) is in scope.

## Foundation

- **No new deps**, no feature flag, no chokepoint. All commands read from `/proc` and `/sys` via `std::fs`; there is no mutation surface to guard (the kernel rejects writes to these interfaces by file permission, and the writable `sysctl` knobs are written via the `sysctl(2)` syscall, not via writing a file from userspace).
- Linux-only: gate the entire `src/linux/` module with `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` at the `mod linux` site in `src/main.rs`. The dispatch layer's stub on other platforms emits a clear error.
- One file per command, dispatch in `src/linux/mod.rs`.
- All parsers are pure functions on `&str` and unit-testable on hand-built fixtures — mirror the `src/k8s/containers.rs` pattern. Test fixtures should include a couple kernel versions' worth of cpuinfo / meminfo so the parsers stay resilient to layout drift.

## Track of child issues

The foundation child must land first. Per-command children all depend on it.

Naming nit: `linux` leaves room to grow beyond `/proc` into journal/dmesg/`/sys/class` later without renaming.

## Out of scope (file separately)

- `dmesg`, `journalctl` — separate tools.
- `/sys/class/*` device-tree parsers — per-class follow-ups (block I/O, network counters, thermal zones).
- `/proc/diskstats`, `/proc/stat`, `/proc/interrupts`, `/proc/pressure/*` — useful but per-parser follow-ups inside this domain.
- BPF, perf, ftrace.
- Cross-platform abstraction (macOS `vm_stat`, etc.) — explicit Linux focus, hence the domain name.
close_reason: Epic complete. All child issues closed: foundation (ev4), cpuinfo (320), meminfo (q2p), mounts (a8w), loadavg (ojt), uptime (m42), sysctl (7do, incl. the carried-over check_sysctl hook redirect), process (ltr), network (d4d). The linux domain ships a curated set of pure-function /proc parsers (no new deps, no cargo feature, Linux-gated at run()), each emitting fixed-column TSV / NDJSON with missing fields as '-'. Out-of-scope items (dmesg, journalctl, /sys/class/*, /proc/diskstats|stat|interrupts|pressure, BPF/perf/ftrace, cross-platform) remain for separate follow-ups.

title: feat(cert): new domain for inspecting PEM/DER certificates
description:
Add a new `cert` domain to `sak` for read-only inspection of TLS/X.509 certificates. Today, certificate triage in `sak`-friendly contexts (Kubernetes, kubeconfigs, secrets, on-disk PKI) requires hand-rolled pipelines of `grep | awk | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates`. A first-class `cert` domain would collapse that to a single command and unlock structured/composable output.

## Real-world motivation

During a recent home-ops cert-expiry alert investigation, the same `openssl`-pipe pattern was written **4+ times** to inspect:

- The admin client cert embedded in a kubeconfig file
- The `adminKubeconfig` field nested inside a Talos `KubernetesSecrets` resource (base64 inside YAML)
- The kubelet client cert at `/var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem` on every node
- The static-pod client certs under `/system/secrets/kubernetes/kube-{apiserver,controller-manager,scheduler}/` on every node

Each invocation looked like:

```sh
grep client-certificate-data kubeconfig | awk '{print \$2}' | base64 -d \
  | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates -issuer
```

## Commands

### `sak cert inspect [files...]`
Print structured details of one or more certificates.

- Accepts: PEM, DER (auto-detect), base64-wrapped PEM (auto-strip), and multi-cert bundles
- Reads stdin if no files given
- Output fields: subject, issuer, serial, NotBefore, NotAfter, days remaining, SANs, key usage, SHA256 fingerprint
- Output formats: human table (default), `--json`, `--tsv`
- Flags:
  - `--field <name>` (e.g. `--field not_after`) — print only that field, useful in shell pipelines
  - `--chain` — treat input as a chain and print each cert with depth index

### `sak cert expiring [files...]`
Filter to certs expiring within a window.

- `--days <N>` (default 30) — show only certs with <N days until `notAfter`
- Exit 1 if any cert matches (so it can be used in shell conditions / CI)
- Same input handling as `inspect`

### `sak cert from-kubeconfig <file> [--user <name>]`
Extract the client cert from a kubeconfig and pipe it to `inspect` semantics.

- Default: print expiry of every `user` entry's `client-certificate-data`
- `--user <name>` to filter
- Also extracts the cluster `certificate-authority-data` with `--ca`

### `sak cert from-yaml <yaml-file> --path <jq-style-path>`
Extract a base64-encoded cert/kubeconfig from inside a YAML document (e.g. Talos resources, Secrets, ConfigMaps) and inspect it.

- `--path .spec.adminKubeconfig` — extract that string, then auto-detect if it's a kubeconfig or raw cert
- Composes with `kubectl get … -o yaml` and `talosctl get … -o yaml`

## Why now / leverage

- Dual-use far beyond Kubernetes: cloudflared certs, internal CAs, mTLS in app secrets, `ingress-nginx` TLS configs
- Read-only by definition — fits the `sak` charter cleanly
- A single command unlocks `for f in /etc/pki/**/*.pem; do sak cert expiring --days 30 \"\$f\"; done` style sweeps

## Implementation notes

- Use the `x509-parser` or `rustls-pki-types` + `x509-cert` crates (pure-Rust, no OpenSSL dep)
- Auto-detect input: try PEM headers → fall back to base64-decode-then-PEM → fall back to DER
- For `from-yaml` / `from-kubeconfig`, reuse the YAML parser already in `sak config`
- Strictly read-only; never writes or modifies certs
close_reason: Implemented sak cert domain with inspect/expiring/from-kubeconfig/from-yaml. Auto-detects PEM bundles, raw DER, and base64-wrapped PEM via shared cert::extract_ders chokepoint. Reuses dot-path / JSON-Pointer parser from src/value.rs for from-yaml. expiring inverts the standard sak exit-code convention (exit 1 = matches found) for grep-style shell ergonomics. Pure parsing, no chokepoint grep test (no mutation surface to guard). Unblocked sak-llm-gxu and sak-llm-3sm.

title: feat(gh): run-list command
description:
Wraps `gh run list --json <fields>`. Output: TSV one row per workflow run. This is the killer command for CI triage.

## Command

### `sak gh run-list`

Flags:
- `--repo <owner/name>`
- `--workflow <name-or-id>` — filter by workflow
- `--branch <name>` — filter by branch
- `--event <push|pull_request|...>` — filter by trigger event
- `--status <queued|in_progress|completed|...>` — filter by run status
- `--user <user>` — filter by actor
- `--limit <n>` (default 30; passes through)
- `--fields <a,b,c>` — default `databaseId,workflowName,headBranch,event,status,conclusion,createdAt,startedAt,updatedAt,displayTitle`
- `--format <tsv|json>` (default tsv)

Output: TSV with the columns named in `--fields`. The `databaseId` field is the run ID needed by `gh run-view`.

Hook: redirect `gh run list` → sak.
close_reason: Landed sak gh run-list (the CI-triage workhorse). src/gh/run_list.rs: --repo, --workflow, --branch, --event, --status, --user (all free-form Option<String> since gh's status/event value sets are open-ended — no clap enums), --limit (usize, default 30 per spec, always forwarded to gh --limit and bounds output), --fields (default databaseId,workflowName,headBranch,event,status,conclusion,createdAt,startedAt,updatedAt,displayTitle — databaseId leads because it's the run ID gh run-view consumes; forwarded verbatim to gh run list --json), --format tsv|json (default tsv). Runs via chokepoint invoke_ok('run', Some('list'), ...) and reuses shared src/gh/render.rs (no new rendering code). build_argv split out for testing; 3 argv tests. Hook: extended check_gh to redirect 'gh run list' -> 'sak gh run-list'; 'gh run view'/'rerun'/'cancel' and other unshadowed verbs pass. Tests gh_run_list_blocks + updated gh_mutations_and_unshadowed_allow (added run rerun/cancel/view cases). Docs: main.rs QUICK_START example, README + CLAUDE.md dogfood bullets. Version 0.14.3 -> 0.14.4 (patch). cargo fmt / clippy (default + --no-default-features) / test (default + --no-default-features) all clean. All three list-style gh commands (pr-list/issue-list/run-list) now share render.rs. Hook note: now redirects 'gh run list', upgrade older sak installs.

title: json grep: find paths whose key or value matches a regex
description: Add `sak json grep <pattern> [files...]` that searches a JSON document for paths whose key or value matches a regex. Options: --key (match keys only), --value (match values only), --type <t> (filter by JSON type). Output: 'path<TAB>value' lines. Useful for finding all api_* keys, all string values containing an email, etc.

Output is path<TAB>value with leading-dot dot paths matching json query/diff/schema; (root) for root scalar. title: Add config grep command (structural search)
description:
Add `sak config grep <pattern> [files...]` — search a config document for keys or values matching a regex, returning the matching paths and (optionally) values. Structure-aware, unlike `fs grep`.

## Motivation

LLMs often need to answer "where is X used in this config?" — e.g., "find every place 'localhost' appears", "find every key starting with 'aws_'". Text grep returns false positives (matches in comments, in keys when you wanted values, etc.); a structural search returns dot-paths so the LLM can navigate from the answer.

## Scope

- `--key <regex>` matches against keys
- `--value <regex>` matches against scalar values (strings, numbers, bools)
- Default behavior with no flag: match keys
- Output: `path<TAB>value` lines, sorted by path
- `--paths-only` to suppress values
- Standard flags: `--format`, `--limit`

## Relationship to existing work

A `json grep` equivalent already exists as **sak-llm-2us**. Both should be implemented together since the walking logic belongs in `src/value.rs`.

## Implementation notes

- Add `value::grep(value, key_re, value_re) -> Vec<(String, &Value)>` walker
- Inline tests across all three formats with both key and value patterns
close_reason: Added sak config grep: structural regex search over TOML, YAML, and plist via the shared value::grep walker. Same flag surface as sak json grep plus --format. Cross-format because every input normalizes through serde_json::Value. Inline tests across all three formats with both key and value patterns.

title: Add git domain enhancements: ref-resolve, merge-base, changed-files, shortlog, ls-files, cat-file
description:
Follow-up enhancements for the git domain after the core commands (status, diff, log, show, blame, branch, tags, stash-list, remote, contributors) are implemented.

## Moderate Value

### sak git ref-resolve <ref>
Resolve a ref (branch, tag, HEAD~3) to a commit SHA + metadata.
- Quick lookup without full show output
- Uses repo.revparse_single(ref) -> peel to commit
- Output: <sha> <type> <summary>

### sak git merge-base <ref1> <ref2>
Find common ancestor of two refs.
- Uses repo.merge_base(oid1, oid2)
- Useful for LLMs reasoning about branch relationships
- Output: <sha> <summary>

### sak git changed-files <ref1> [ref2]
List files changed between two refs (simpler than diff --name-only).
- Default ref2 is HEAD
- Uses diff_tree_to_tree() and iterates deltas
- Output: <status>\t<path> (A/M/D/R)
- Sorted by path

## Lower Value (covered by combinations of existing commands)

### sak git shortlog
Author summary with commit counts.
- Derivable from log output but convenient as a dedicated command
- Similar to contributors but grouped differently

### sak git ls-files
List tracked files.
- Derivable from status + fs glob but convenient for 'what does git track?'
- Uses repo.index() -> iterate entries

### sak git cat-file <ref>
Raw object access (blob, tree, commit, tag content).
- Show mostly covers commit use cases
- Useful for inspecting blobs at specific revisions

## Implementation Notes

- All commands follow the same pattern as core git commands
- Use git2 crate APIs (no shelling out)
- Output through BoundedWriter with --limit support
- All operations strictly read-only

title: feat(k8s): describe command
description:
## Context

The "one command to look at a broken thing" command. Pulls together everything an LLM needs to understand why a single resource is in the state it is, in one call.

**Not kubectl describe.** kubectl describe is human-prose oriented (paragraphs, blank lines, mixed indentation) and is hostile to LLM parsing. `sak k8s describe` outputs structured tab-separated sections with section headers, the same way `sak git log` and `sak fs grep` already do.

## Command

### `sak k8s describe <kind> <name>`

Args:
- `<kind>`, `<name>` — resolved via `discovery::resolve` (same as `get`)
- `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output sections, each prefixed with a `# Section` header line written as a `BoundedWriter` decoration (does not count toward `--limit`):

1. `# Object` — `kind/name`, namespace, age, labels (sorted), annotations (sorted, one per line)
2. `# Status` — top-level `status.phase` / `status.conditions[*]` as `type<TAB>status<TAB>reason<TAB>message`
3. `# Containers` — for each container in the resolved pod-template (using the existing `containers::walk_containers`): `name<TAB>image<TAB>ready<TAB>restarts<TAB>last-state<TAB>last-reason`
4. `# Owners` — walk `metadata.ownerReferences` recursively up to the root: `kind/name` per ancestor
5. `# Events` — last 10 events whose `involvedObject` matches this resource, formatted the same way as `sak k8s events`

## Implementation notes

- Resource fetch: `client::get_dyn`. 404 → exit 1.
- Container statuses live on `status.containerStatuses` (Pod) — for non-Pod kinds we walk `containers::walk_containers` for declared containers and skip the `restarts/last-state/last-reason` columns (or render them as `-`).
- Owner walk is bounded: cap at 5 hops to defend against pathological loops.
- Events fetch shares the same Event-list logic as `sak k8s events --for kind/name`. **Depends on sak k8s events landing first** so the events helper can be factored into a small shared module (or duplicate the helper if events is not yet in flight — flag in the PR description either way).
- All section headers via `BoundedWriter::write_decoration` so they do not get truncated mid-section.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/describe.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Describe` variant.
- (Possibly) `src/k8s/events.rs` — extract the events-for-object fetch into a `pub fn events_for(client, ar, namespace, name) -> ...` so describe can call it.

## Dependencies

Depends on `sak k8s events` (separate issue) for the events fetch helper. Can be implemented in parallel up to that point, but the final version should reuse the shared helper rather than duplicating it.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual: `cargo run -- k8s describe deployment <name> -n <ns>` shows all five sections, with events at the bottom.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented src/k8s/describe.rs with five LLM-friendly tab-separated sections: # Object (kind/name, namespace, age, sorted labels, sorted annotations), # Status (phase + conditions), # Containers (declared containers from walk_containers joined with status.containerStatuses for ready/restarts/last-state/last-reason; non-pods get '-' for runtime columns), # Owners (ownerReferences chain capped at 5 hops, prefers controller=true), # Events (top 10 via events::fetch_events_for). Section headers via write_decoration so they don't count toward --limit. Includes tiny RFC3339-Z parser for human-friendly age strings to avoid pulling in chrono. README hook regex extended to redirect kubectl describe + kubectl events; CLAUDE.md no further changes needed.

title: feat(talos): version command for per-node version drift detection
description:
Across-node version drift detection. Surfaces stale nodes after a partial upgrade — currently requires manual fan-out.

## Command

### `sak talos version`

Fans out across every node, reports Talos / kernel / Kubernetes versions.

Output:

```
node<TAB>talos<TAB>kernel<TAB>kubernetes
10.0.0.1<TAB>v1.7.5<TAB>6.6.30-talos<TAB>v1.30.1
10.0.0.2<TAB>v1.7.5<TAB>6.6.30-talos<TAB>v1.30.1
10.0.0.3<TAB>v1.7.4<TAB>6.6.30-talos<TAB>v1.30.1
```

Flags:
- `--nodes <ip1,ip2>` — override the context node set
- `--drift` — only show nodes that diverge from the cluster-mode version (exit 1 if drift found, matching the grep convention)
- `--format json`

## Implementation notes

- Shell out via the chokepoint (`talosctl version`; verb is already in `READ_ONLY_VERBS`).
- Talos version comes from `talosctl version --short` or `--json`.
- Kubernetes version: `talosctl get kubernetesversion` (or read `MachineConfig.cluster.kubernetes.version` if that's cleaner).
- Kernel version: `talosctl get kernelversion`, or `uname` via `talosctl read /proc/version` as a fallback.
- Same fan-out helper as `talos certs` / `talos services`.
- `--drift` mode: compute the modal value per column, mark rows that diverge. Exit 1 if any drift detected.

## Test coverage

Unit-test the mode-vs-drift comparator and the per-node JSON parser on hand-built fixtures.

title: feat(k8s): kubeconfig command to inspect users, clusters, and embedded certs
description:
Add `sak k8s kubeconfig` subcommands for read-only inspection of kubeconfig files — including the certs embedded inside them. A kubeconfig is a structured object that today requires `yq` + `base64` + `openssl` to interrogate; a dedicated command would handle the common questions (\"who's in this kubeconfig?\", \"when does the admin cert expire?\", \"what server does it point to?\") in one call.

## Real-world motivation

During home-ops cert-expiry triage, the same chain was needed repeatedly:

```sh
grep client-certificate-data ~/.kube/config | awk '{print \$2}' | base64 -d \
  | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates
```

…to discover that a local admin kubeconfig had a cert expiring in 7 days. Then again for each user/cluster entry in larger kubeconfigs.

## Commands

### `sak k8s kubeconfig users [path]`
List user entries with auth type (cert / token / exec) and, for certs, subject + NotAfter + days remaining.

### `sak k8s kubeconfig clusters [path]`
List clusters with server URL and CA cert NotAfter.

### `sak k8s kubeconfig contexts [path]`
List contexts and the current-context marker.

### `sak k8s kubeconfig certs [path] [--user <name>]`
Extract every embedded `client-certificate-data` / `certificate-authority-data` and print structured cert details. Equivalent to piping into `sak cert inspect` (see companion issue) but with the user/cluster names attached as columns.

### `sak k8s kubeconfig validate [path]`
Sanity checks: every context references an existing user/cluster, no expired CAs, no expired client certs.

## Defaults

- Reads `\$KUBECONFIG`, then `~/.kube/config`, if no path given
- Honors `KUBECONFIG` colon-separated list (same precedence as `kubectl`)
- `--json` output on every command for `sak json` piping

## Why now / leverage

- Composes naturally with the proposed `sak cert` domain
- Surfaces stale/about-to-expire admin kubeconfigs in seconds — a recurring problem in any Talos/kubeadm cluster where the admin cert has a 1-year default lifetime and isn't auto-rotated
- Single-purpose; small implementation; doesn't need its own crate

## Implementation notes

- Reuse `sak config` YAML parser to load the kubeconfig
- Decode `*-data` fields (base64) and either inline or delegate to `sak cert inspect`
- Strictly read-only

title: feat(fs): triage commands — duplicates, largest, find with predicates
description:
Three small fs additions for disk triage. None of them are covered by `glob` (name-only) or by the open beads sak-llm-ltn (tree/stat/head/tail/wc).

## Commands

### `sak fs largest [path]`

Walk and print top N largest files. Output: `size<TAB>path` sorted by size descending.

Flags:
- `-n, --top <N>` (default 25)
- `--human` — human-readable sizes (1.2K, 4.5M, 1.1G)
- `--min-size <N>` — exclude files below threshold
- `--hidden`
- `--limit`

### `sak fs duplicates [path]`

Find files with identical contents. Two-pass: group by size, then hash same-size groups (sha256 streamed in 64KB chunks — share the same digest helper as `sak hash` if/when that lands; otherwise inline `sha2`). Output: groups separated by blank lines, each group with `size<TAB>hash<TAB>path` lines.

Flags:
- `--min-size <N>` — skip tiny files (default 1KB; tiny dupes are typically noise like empty `__init__.py`)
- `--hidden`
- `--limit`

### `sak fs find <path>` with predicates

`glob` searches by name. `find` searches by *metadata*:
- `--size +1M`, `--size -10K`, `--size 4K-1M`
- `--mtime +7d`, `--mtime -1h`, `--mtime 2026-01-01..2026-02-01`
- `--type f|d|l` — file, directory, symlink
- `--name <glob>` — combine with name pattern (uses `globset`, same as `fs glob`)
- `--hidden`
- `--limit`

Output: paths sorted alphabetically.

## Implementation notes

- All three reuse the existing `walkdir`-with-`filter_entry` directory pruning from `glob`/`grep`. Always check `e.depth() > 0` to avoid filtering the root.
- Duplicates: hash via `sha2` (already unconditional dep from `cert`); switch to `blake3` if/when the hash domain lands and perf matters.
- The size/mtime expression parser (`+1M`, `2026-01-01..2026-02-01`) is small but worth dedicated unit tests.
- Output via `BoundedWriter`.
- Sort deterministically (alphabetical for `find`, size-desc for `largest`, size-then-hash-then-alpha for `duplicates`).
close_reason: Implemented fs largest/duplicates/find. largest: top-N by size (size<TAB>path, --human, --min-size, -n). duplicates: size-bucketed then SHA-256-confirmed groups (size<TAB>hash<TAB>path, --min-size default 1K). find: metadata predicates --size (+N/-N/A-B/N), --mtime (+D/-D/DATE..DATE), --type f|d|l, --name <glob>. Shared walk pruning extracted to fs/mod.rs (pruned_walk/should_skip/is_hidden_file), shared size parsing+human formatting in fs/size.rs. Hook check_find now steers metadata searches to 'sak fs find'. Docs (README, main.rs quick-start, --help) updated; version 0.17.1->0.17.2.

title: feat(prom): new domain for read-only Prometheus + Alertmanager queries
description:
Add a new `prom` domain to `sak` for read-only Prometheus and Alertmanager inspection. In-cluster Prometheus is the canonical source of truth for triage (\"is the alert firing?\", \"what's the current rate?\", \"which target is down?\"), but reaching it from a workstation today requires `kubectl port-forward` + `curl` + a JSON parser. That's a lot of glue for every question.

## Real-world motivation

During a recent home-ops cert-expiry alert investigation, the following sequence was needed *just to see which apiserver was alerting and what value*:

```sh
kubectl -n observability port-forward svc/kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus 19090:9090 &
sleep 2
curl -s http://localhost:19090/api/v1/alerts \
  | python3 -c \"import json,sys; …filter and pretty-print…\"
```

Followed by another bespoke `curl + python` block for the histogram_quantile query, then another for the raw bucket counts, then another to compute rate deltas. Every single one was \"escape PromQL into a URL, fetch JSON, parse with Python.\"

## Commands

### `sak prom alerts [--firing|--pending|--all] [--name <regex>]`
List alerts from Prometheus's `/api/v1/alerts`. Columns: alertname, severity, instance, value, state, activeAt, summary. Default = firing+pending.

### `sak prom query <promql> [--at <time>]`
Instant query. Pretty-prints labels + value; `--json` for machine output. `--at` accepts RFC3339 or relative (`-5m`).

### `sak prom query-range <promql> --since <duration> [--step <duration>]`
Range query. Tabular output (time, labels, value) or `--json`.

### `sak prom histogram <metric> [--labels k=v,...]`
Pretty-print a `_bucket` histogram with cumulative count, delta, and 5m rate per `le` bucket side-by-side. This is the single highest-leverage command for cert-expiration, latency, and SLI debugging.

Example output:
```
le=<7.00d     cum=1539     delta=1529   rate=0.0889/s
le=<30.00d    cum=2517     delta=978    rate=0.0889/s
le=<360.00d   cum=2300091  delta=2295049 rate=5.4889/s
```

### `sak prom targets [--down] [--job <regex>]`
List scrape targets and their health, lastScrape, lastError. `--down` filters to unhealthy.

### `sak prom rules [--firing]`
List recording + alerting rules and their state.

### `sak am alerts` / `sak am silences`
(Alertmanager subdomain — or fold under `sak prom am alerts` if a sibling domain feels heavy.) Same idea: list active alerts/silences without writing the curl glue.

## Connectivity

Two modes, auto-detected:

1. `--url <http://…>` for explicit endpoint
2. Default: auto-discover via Kubernetes Service selector (`app.kubernetes.io/name=prometheus` and `=alertmanager`), and transparently `port-forward` for the duration of the command. The user already gives `sak k8s` cluster credentials — reuse them.

A `--no-port-forward` opt-out for users who run Prometheus exposed via Ingress/Gateway.

## Why now / leverage

- Every k8s-shaped session inevitably hits \"is this alert firing?\" — this is the moral equivalent of `sak k8s events` for the metrics plane
- Histogram inspection alone justifies the domain — it's the most opaque part of the Prometheus data model and the existing UI buries it
- Read-only by definition; aligns with `sak`'s charter

## Implementation notes

- Use a minimal HTTP client (`ureq` or `reqwest` blocking) — no need for async
- `--url` flag + `PROMETHEUS_URL` / `ALERTMANAGER_URL` env vars
- Port-forward via the same Kubernetes client `sak k8s` uses; pick a free local port
- Format durations human-readably (`<7.00d`, not `<604800s`) in the histogram view
- `--json` output mode on every command for further `sak json` piping
close_reason: Tier 1 shipped: prom domain with alerts, query, query-range, histogram, targets, rules, and am alerts | am silences subcommands. Connection via --url or PROMETHEUS_URL / ALERTMANAGER_URL env vars. Read-only enforcement via chokepoint + grep test mirroring k8s/lxc/docker. Smoke-tested end-to-end against a live cluster (prometheus.chezlawrence.com + alertmanager.chezlawrence.com). Tier 2 (Kubernetes service auto-discovery + transparent port-forward) deferred — file a follow-up issue if/when needed.

title: feat(sqlite): count command
description:
## Context

Row count for a table, with an optional WHERE clause. The "how big is this" command. Small but worth its own command so LLMs do not have to construct \`SELECT COUNT(*)\` themselves and risk picking up a \`sqlite query\` typo.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite count <db> <table>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`<table>\` — table name
- \`--where <expr>\` — optional SQL boolean expression (no leading \`WHERE\`)

Source:

\`\`\`sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "<table>"
\`\`\`

or, with \`--where\`:

\`\`\`sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "<table>" WHERE <expr>
\`\`\`

## Validation

- Quote the table name by wrapping in double quotes and doubling any internal double quotes.
- Reject \`--where\` containing \`;\` (defense in depth — the connection is read-only and \`query_only=ON\`, but a stray \`;\` indicates the user is trying to chain statements and should get a clear error).
- Reject \`<table>\` containing a NUL byte.

## Output

A single line with the count. Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

## Exit codes

- 0 if the query succeeded (any non-negative count, including 0 — \`count\` is a metadata command, an empty table is a valid answer)
- 2 on error (table missing, malformed where clause, etc.)

Note: \`count\` deviates from the usual sak convention (1 = no results) because the count itself is the result, and zero is meaningful.

## Implementation notes

- Open via \`client::open_readonly\`, run via the existing query helper from the foundation issue.
- Pure \`u64\` extraction from a one-row, one-column result.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/count.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Count\` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit tests over a temp database covering: empty table → \`0\`; non-empty table; \`--where\` filter; missing table → exit 2; \`--where\` with \`;\` → exit 2 with clear error; table name containing a quoted-double-quote.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite count some.db users --where "active = 1"\`.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented sak sqlite count: quoted identifier, optional --where (rejects ;), exit 0 even on count=0 per issue spec.

title: feat(gh): new domain for read-only GitHub CLI inspection
description:
Epic for a new sak domain wrapping read-only `gh` (GitHub CLI) invocations. LLM-driven CI/PR/issue triage currently bottoms out at shelling to `gh` directly or `curl`ing the GitHub API. This formalizes the read-only subset behind a verb allowlist and gives sak's normal TSV/JSON output discipline.

Mirrors the talos and nix epics: shell out to the system `gh` binary, no Rust deps, no cargo feature (CLAUDE.md rule: domains that bring no new Rust deps are always-on). Read-only enforcement is convention + a `READ_ONLY_VERBS` allowlist (noun, verb) + a grep-test chokepoint.

## Verb allowlist

`gh` is hierarchical noun-verb: `gh pr list`, `gh issue view`, `gh run view`. The chokepoint allowlists `(noun, Option<verb>)` pairs, same shape as nix:

```rust
pub const READ_ONLY_VERBS: &[(&str, Option<&str>)] = &[
    ("pr",       Some("list")),
    ("pr",       Some("view")),
    ("pr",       Some("status")),
    ("pr",       Some("diff")),
    ("pr",       Some("checks")),
    ("issue",    Some("list")),
    ("issue",    Some("view")),
    ("issue",    Some("status")),
    ("run",      Some("list")),
    ("run",      Some("view")),
    ("release",  Some("list")),
    ("release",  Some("view")),
    ("repo",     Some("list")),
    ("repo",     Some("view")),
    ("workflow", Some("list")),
    ("workflow", Some("view")),
    ("search",   None),       // all search subcommands are read
    ("status",   None),
    ("auth",     Some("status")),
    ("api",      None),       // GET-only enforced separately (see below)
];
```

## The `gh api` escape hatch

`gh api <endpoint>` is the catch-all REST/GraphQL caller. It defaults to GET but takes `-X / --method <METHOD>` to switch verbs. The chokepoint **rejects** any invocation containing `-X` or `--method` whose value is not `GET` (case-insensitive). This is stronger than the noun-verb allowlist because `gh api` is otherwise wide open. Document the rejection clearly in `long_about` — operators sometimes pass `-X GET` redundantly and that should be accepted, not rejected.

Notably **excluded** (mutation surface — hard error at the chokepoint):
- `pr create / merge / close / reopen / edit / ready / review`
- `issue create / close / reopen / edit / develop`
- `repo create / clone / delete / fork / archive / rename / sync`
- `release create / delete / upload / edit`
- `workflow run / disable / enable`
- `run rerun / cancel / delete / watch`
- `secret set / delete` / `variable set / delete` / `ssh-key add / delete`
- `auth login / logout / refresh / setup-git`
- `extension install / upgrade / remove`
- `gist create / delete / edit`
- `label create / delete / edit / clone`

## Auth model

`gh` reads its token from `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` (populated by `gh auth login`) or `GH_TOKEN` / `GITHUB_TOKEN` env vars. sak inherits the env unchanged — no plumbing needed. The `auth-status` command exposes the current state for triage.

## Track of child issues

Each command is a child issue and gates this epic. The foundation child must land first (it builds `src/gh/client.rs`, the dispatch module, the chokepoint grep test, and the `-X` flag inspector); every command depends on it.

## Out of scope (file separately if needed)

- `gh codespace` subcommands — separate noun, separate follow-up if anyone cares.
- `gh extension list` — minor; could be a follow-up.
- `gh gist list / view` — read-only and trivial; deferred until usage demands.
- GraphQL convenience helpers — `gh api graphql -f query=...` already covered by the `api` child; richer GraphQL helpers are out of scope.
- Multi-repo aggregation (e.g., "list PRs across N repos") — caller composes with shell loops.
close_reason: gh domain complete — all 12 child issues landed. Foundation (sak-llm-yln): src/gh/client.rs chokepoint with READ_ONLY_VERBS (noun, Option<verb>) allowlist, gh-api GET-only method guard, grep test confining Command::new("gh") to client.rs. Commands: api (GET-only REST/GraphQL escape hatch), pr-list, pr-view, issue-list, issue-view, run-list, run-view (metadata + --log/--log-failed streaming), release-list, release-view, workflow-list, repo-view. Shared src/gh/render.rs: Format enum, parse_fields, render_cell (scalar/login/name/comma-joined-array/compact-JSON, TSV-safe), emit (array TSV/JSON) + emit_single (single-object key<TAB>value/JSON), exit-code wrapper. All list/view commands forward --fields verbatim to gh (sak invents no field set) and default field sets are validated against the live 'gh <noun> <verb> --json' field lists — caught and fixed three stale spec field names (repo-view topics->repositoryTopics, release-list dropped invalid author, workflow-list dropped invalid createdAt/updatedAt). Hook: check_gh in src/hook/claude_code.rs redirects every read form (gh api GET, pr/issue/run/release/workflow list, pr/issue/run/release view, repo view) to its sak equivalent while letting mutating/unshadowed verbs pass; full block/allow test coverage. Read-only enforcement: noun/verb allowlist + gh-api method guard + chokepoint grep test (mirrors talos pattern; no cargo feature, no new Rust deps). Version 0.13.x -> 0.14.11. All commands live-smoke-tested against cli/cli with an authenticated gh. cargo fmt / clippy / test green on both default and --no-default-features throughout. Out-of-scope items from the epic body (codespace, extension list, gist, GraphQL helpers, multi-repo aggregation) remain unfiled — open follow-ups if demanded.

title: json schema: infer structural schema from a document
description: Add `sak json schema [files...]` that walks a JSON document and emits an inferred schema -- key names + types, recursively. Output could be JSON Schema or a simpler 'key: type' tree (decide during design). LLMs constantly need to understand shape before writing a query, and currently waste tokens reading whole files. Highest-value addition to the json domain.
close_reason: Implemented sak json schema. Walks a JSON document and emits flat dot-path lines of form 'path: type'. Arrays use [] in the path; element schemas are merged into unions when heterogeneous (e.g. '[]: boolean|number|string'). Root type recorded as '(root): <type>'. Supports --depth to cap recursion. Uses BTreeMap+BTreeSet for sorted, deduplicated output. Reuses read_json_inputs() and type_name() from json/mod.rs. Version bumped to 0.3.1.

title: feat(gh): run-view command
description:
Wraps `gh run view <run-id>`. Inspect a single workflow run — jobs, steps, conclusion, log retrieval.

## Command

### `sak gh run-view <run-id>`

Flags:
- `--repo <owner/name>`
- `--log` — emit the run's logs (text stream)
- `--log-failed` — only failed-step logs
- `--job <job-id>` — drill into a specific job's logs (passes through)
- `--fields <a,b,c>` — for non-log mode; default `databaseId,workflowName,headBranch,event,status,conclusion,jobs,createdAt,updatedAt`
- `--format <tsv|json>` (default `json` — nested jobs array doesn't TSV)
- `--limit`

Output: JSON passthrough by default. With `--log` / `--log-failed`, output is the raw log text streamed through `BoundedWriter` so `--limit` truncates cleanly.

Implementation: `gh run view` and `gh run view --log` are different code paths — the chokepoint allows both since both fall under `(run, view)`. Decide which based on the flags.

Hook: redirect `gh run view` and `gh run view --log` → sak.
close_reason: Landed sak gh run-view with two modes. src/gh/run_view.rs: required positional <run-id> (the databaseId from run-list), --repo, --log (full logs), --log-failed (failed-step logs only), --job <id> (narrows either mode), --fields (default databaseId,workflowName,headBranch,event,status,conclusion,jobs,createdAt,updatedAt — validated against 'gh run view --json'; metadata mode only), --format tsv|json (default JSON; metadata only), --limit. Mode split: --log/--log-failed (log_mode()) stream raw log text via a local emit_log (BoundedWriter, line-split, empty->exit 1, --fields/--format ignored); otherwise metadata via render::emit_single. build_argv enforces gh's --json-vs-log mutual exclusivity (only one emitted; run-id leads, then --repo/--job, then mode flag). Runs via chokepoint invoke_ok('run', Some('view'), ...) — both code paths fall under (run, view) as the spec noted. 6 build_argv tests. Hook: extended check_gh to redirect 'gh run view' -> 'sak gh run-view'; 'gh run rerun'/'cancel'/'watch'/'delete' and other unshadowed verbs pass. Tests gh_run_view_blocks + updated gh_mutations_and_unshadowed_allow (moved 'gh run view' from allow to block). Docs: main.rs QUICK_START example, README + CLAUDE.md dogfood bullets. Version 0.14.9 -> 0.14.10 (patch). cargo fmt / clippy (default + --no-default-features) / test (default + --no-default-features) all clean. Live-smoke-tested against cli/cli run 26189297400: metadata tsv/json clean, --log streams raw text with --limit truncation (exit 0 + stderr notice), --log-failed on a successful run correctly emits nothing (exit 1), hook block/allow correct. Only release-view (sak-llm-f00) remains before the gh epic can close. Hook note: now redirects 'gh run view', upgrade older sak installs.

title: feat(k8s): audit command to summarize kube-apiserver audit logs
description:
Add `sak k8s audit` for read-only analysis of kube-apiserver audit logs. Audit logs are JSON-Lines and contain everything needed to answer \"who is hitting my apiserver?\" — but the friction of writing the JSONL aggregator from scratch every time means most operators don't bother.

## Real-world motivation

During home-ops cert-expiry triage, identifying *which* client was using an about-to-expire admin cert required reading audit logs off a Talos node:

```sh
talosctl -n 192.168.1.11 read /var/log/audit/kube/kube-apiserver-….log \\
  | python3 -c '
    import json,sys,collections
    by_user=collections.Counter()
    by_ip=collections.Counter()
    by_uri=collections.Counter()
    for line in sys.stdin:
        try: ev=json.loads(line)
        except: continue
        by_user[ev.get(\"user\",{}).get(\"username\",\"?\")] += 1
        # … etc, repeat with different filters …
'
```

Three nearly-identical Python blocks were written in one session, each computing slightly different aggregations over the same JSONL.

## Commands

### `sak k8s audit summary [files...] [--by user|ip|verb|uri|userAgent] [--top N]`
Aggregate counts grouped by one or more dimensions. Default: top 20 by user. `--by user,ip` for composite grouping. Reads stdin or one-or-more files, globs supported (Talos rotates per-file).

### `sak k8s audit filter [files...] [predicates...]`
Stream-filter events with structured predicates that don't need PromQL/JQ:

- `--user <name>` / `--user-not <name>` (also `--user-regex`)
- `--ip <cidr>` / `--ip-not <cidr>` — supports CIDR so you can `--ip-not 10.192.0.0/16` to exclude pod traffic
- `--verb get,list,watch`
- `--uri-regex <regex>`
- `--namespace <name>`
- `--since <duration>` / `--until <duration>`
- `--source-chain` — print the full `sourceIPs` array (X-Forwarded-For chain) which the apiserver records but most viewers hide

Output: pretty (default) or `--json` for re-piping.

### `sak k8s audit clients [files...]`
A purpose-built shortcut equivalent to `summary --by user,sourceIPs[0],userAgent --top 30`. Answers \"who is connecting to this apiserver?\" in one command.

### `sak k8s audit cert-auth [files...]`
Filter to events authenticated via a client certificate (i.e. `user.extra.\"authentication.kubernetes.io/credential-id\"` contains `X509SHA256=…`), grouped by cert fingerprint + user + sourceIP. The killer feature for cert-expiry triage — \"which X.509 fingerprint is making these calls, and where is it coming from?\"

## Connectivity / input handling

- Default: stdin
- Filenames: accept paths or globs (Talos: `/var/log/audit/kube/kube-apiserver-*.log`)
- `--talos-node <ip>` convenience flag that shells out to `talosctl read` for the appropriate path (or document the pipeline if `sak` shouldn't wrap `talosctl`)
- Streaming (constant memory) — these files can be huge

## Why now / leverage

- Audit logs are the only source of \"which client did this?\" — opaque without tooling
- Highlighting the `sourceIPs` chain is non-obvious but critical: it revealed the X-Forwarded-For pattern that pinpointed Lens on a remote machine in the home-ops case
- One-shot per-session work that's currently done by writing the same Counter-based Python aggregator
- Pure read-only

## Implementation notes

- Streaming JSONL parser; never buffer the whole file
- Use `cidr` crate for CIDR matching
- `--json` output everywhere for further `sak json` filtering

title: feat(helm): dependency-list command
description:
Wraps `helm dependency list <chart>`. Lists declared dependencies of a chart from its `Chart.yaml` / `requirements.yaml`.

## Command

### `sak helm dependency-list <chart>`

Flags:
- `--format <tsv|json>` (default tsv)
- `--limit`

Output: TSV `name<TAB>version<TAB>repository<TAB>status` where `status` is `ok` / `missing` / `wrong version` per helm's check. `<chart>` is a path to a local chart directory or a `.tgz`.

Implementation: chokepoint subverb is `Some("list")` for `("dependency", Some("list"))`. Parse the human-shaped output (helm doesn't emit `-o json` for this verb) — the table format is stable enough to parse with a hand-rolled splitter, testable on hand-built fixtures.

Hook: redirect `helm dependency list` → sak.
close_reason: Implemented sak helm dependency-list <chart>: lists a chart deps via helm dependency list. TSV name/version/repository/status or --format json (sak-built array, since helm has no -o json for this verb). Key finding: helm dependency list output is TAB-separated (tabwriter emits literal \t between space-padded cells), NOT space-aligned — parse splits on \t and trims, robust to two-word wrong-version status and empty repository cells. Second pinned-subverb path invoke_ok(dependency, Some(list)). No cluster (reads local chart files). No deps -> exit 1; bad path -> exit 2 (helm error surfaced). Pure parse(&str)->Vec<Dep>, 7 fixture tests incl real-shape tab table. Hook: redirects helm dependency/dep/dependencies list (update/build pass through); tests. README/quick-start/version 0.16.6. Live-verified against a throwaway chart: TSV, json, no-deps=exit1, bad-path=exit2. fmt+clippy clean default+lean; 881/574 tests.

title: Add git domain with status, diff, log, show, blame, branch commands
description:
Add a new `git` domain to sak following the same pattern as the `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak git status`
Show working tree status — staged, unstaged, and untracked files.
Wraps `git status --porcelain=v2` for machine-parseable output.

### `sak git diff`
Show file diffs. Supports staged vs unstaged, commit ranges, and path filtering.
Wraps `git diff` with options for `--staged`, `--name-only`, commit refs, and path specs.

### `sak git log`
View commit history with flexible formatting.
Wraps `git log` with options for `--oneline`, `-n` count, `--since`/`--until`, `--author`, `--grep`, path filtering, and `--limit` (via BoundedWriter).

### `sak git show`
Display a specific commit's metadata and diff.
Wraps `git show <ref>` with options for `--stat`, `--name-only`, and `--format`.

### `sak git blame`
Show line-by-line authorship for a file.
Wraps `git blame --porcelain` with options for line ranges (`-L`), `--since`, and formatted output with right-aligned line numbers (reuse `format_line_number()`).

### `sak git branch`
List branches and show current branch.
Wraps `git branch` with options for `--all`, `--remote`, `--sort`, and `--contains`.

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `GitCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/git/`
- All operations are strictly read-only — no checkout, no commit, no push
- Use `std::process::Command` to shell out to `git`
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Reuse `output.rs` utilities (`format_line_number`, `relative_path`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results, 2 = error
- Errors to stderr prefixed with `sak: error:`
- Deterministic/sorted output where applicable
close_reason: Implemented git domain with 10 commands (status, diff, log, show, blame, branch, tags, stash-list, remote, contributors) using git2 crate. All 69 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: feat(linux): sysctl command
description:
Walk `/proc/sys/**` and emit `key<TAB>value` lines (dotted-key form matching `sysctl -a`: `net/ipv4/tcp_syncookies` → `net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies`).

`pattern` is an optional positional regex filter on the dotted key name. Default with no pattern: emit everything.

Flags:
- `--format <tsv|json>`
- `--limit`

Implementation: use the existing `walkdir` pattern with `filter_entry` restricted to `/proc/sys`. Always check `e.depth() > 0` to avoid filtering the root (matches existing fs convention). Multi-line values get newline/tab-replaced with spaces — same defense `sak sqlite info` applies to PRAGMA values — to keep the `key<TAB>value` contract.

The hook redirect for bare `sysctl` lives in the foundation issue, so no hook change here.
notes: Hook update moved here from the foundation (sak-llm-ev4): the foundation deliberately did NOT add the check_sysctl redirect, because it would have pointed at this not-yet-existing command. So THIS issue must also add check_sysctl to src/hook/claude_code.rs (redirect bare 'sysctl' / 'sysctl -a' / 'sysctl <key>' reads to 'sak linux sysctl'; leave mutating 'sysctl -w'/'sysctl -p' alone), route it from check(), and add block/allow tests. Disregard the description line saying the hook lives in the foundation issue.
close_reason: Implemented in src/linux/sysctl.rs: walks /proc/sys with WalkDir (filter_entry skips symlinked dirs via depth()>0 check), renders each regular file's path as a dotted key (net/ipv4/x -> net.ipv4.x via pure path_to_key), and emits key<TAB>value sorted by key. Values run through super::sanitize so tab/multi-line sysctls collapse to a space. Optional positional regex filters keys; write-only/permission-denied knobs silently skipped (like sysctl -a). --format tsv|json (NDJSON), --limit. HOOK: added check_sysctl to src/hook/claude_code.rs (carried over from foundation per the note) — blocks bare sysctl / -a / <key> reads, allows writes (key=value, -w, -p/--load, --system); 5 block/allow tests in src/hook/tests.rs. Updated CLAUDE.md hook-coverage list. Verified against real /proc/sys + hook --check.

title: feat(sqlite): explain command for query plan inspection
description:
`sak sqlite query` lets you run SELECTs but doesn't tell you *how* SQLite is executing them. `explain` surfaces the query plan, which is often what you actually want when investigating a slow query.

## Command

### `sak sqlite explain <db> <sql>`

Runs `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN <sql>` against a read-only connection.

Output:

```
id<TAB>parent<TAB>notused<TAB>detail
2<TAB>0<TAB>0<TAB>SEARCH users USING INDEX idx_users_email (email=?)
3<TAB>0<TAB>0<TAB>SCAN orders
```

Flags:
- `--bytecode` — run plain `EXPLAIN <sql>` instead (SQLite VDBE bytecode, lower-level — opcode/p1/p2/p3/p4/p5/comment columns)
- `--format json` — JSON output instead of TSV
- `--limit`

## Implementation notes

- Reuse `client::query_rows_typed` — `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` is already on the SELECT/WITH/EXPLAIN/PRAGMA allowlist in `is_read_statement` from the `sqlite query` work.
- The chokepoint already permits EXPLAIN, so no enforcement change needed.
- The SQL passed to explain still goes through `is_read_statement` — defense in depth.
- Output via `BoundedWriter`.
- No new deps.

## Why separate from `sqlite query`

- Different output shape (4-column metadata, not row data).
- Different intent (inspecting *the plan*, not the rows).
- Avoids putting an `--explain` flag on `query` that changes the entire output contract.

## Test coverage

Unit-test against a temp fixture DB with a known schema and known query plan (e.g. `SEARCH` vs `SCAN` depending on whether an index is hit).

title: feat(cert): verify-chain command for bundle integrity
description:
`sak cert inspect` reads a bundle but doesn't tell you whether the bundle is a *valid chain* — issuer/subject links match, validity windows overlap, signatures verify, order is leaf → intermediates → root.

## Command

### `sak cert verify-chain [files...]`

Reads one or more PEM/DER inputs (same auto-detection as `inspect` via the shared `cert::extract_ders` chokepoint), groups them into chains, and verifies each:

1. Order: leaf first, then issuers, then root (or self-signed) last.
2. Subject/issuer linkage: each cert's `Issuer` matches the next cert's `Subject`.
3. Signature verification: each cert is signed by the next cert's public key.
4. Validity overlap: every cert is currently within its `NotBefore`/`NotAfter` (or, with `--at <RFC3339>`, at a given instant).
5. Path length / basic constraints sanity (intermediate certs must have `CA:TRUE`).

## Output

Per chain:

```
chain<TAB>1<TAB>length=3<TAB>valid|invalid
  leaf<TAB><subject><TAB><not_after>
  inter<TAB><subject><TAB><not_after>
  root<TAB><subject><TAB><not_after>
  issue<TAB>cert 2 NotAfter (2026-01-01T...) is before cert 1 NotBefore (2026-06-01T...)
```

Exit codes:
- 0 — every chain valid
- 1 — at least one chain invalid (matches the `expiring` exit-code inversion for grep-style shell ergonomics)
- 2 — parsing error

## Implementation notes

- `x509-parser` already in deps (from cert domain) — exposes signature verification via `verify_signature` against an issuer's public key.
- No system CA bundle lookup — verification is **structural**, not "trusted by the system." (Cross-checking against the system trust store is a different command if it's ever needed; defer.)
- Reuse `cert::extract_ders` for input handling.
- Doesn't need a chokepoint test — pure parsing, no mutation surface (same rationale as the rest of the cert domain).
- Common signature algorithms to support: RSA-PKCS1, RSA-PSS, ECDSA P-256/P-384/P-521, Ed25519. `x509-parser` covers these.

## Flags

- `--at <RFC3339>` — verify validity at a specific instant (default: now)
- `--allow-self-signed` — accept a single self-signed cert as a valid one-cert chain
- `--format json` — structured output for piping

title: feat(base64): new domain for encode/decode
description:
`cert from-yaml` already decodes base64-wrapped PEM inside YAML; `sqlite query` base64-encodes BLOB columns. Promote to a first-class domain so poking at k8s Secrets and JWT payloads doesn't require shelling to `base64 -d` (or worse, `printf %s ... | base64 -d` with newline quirks).

`base64` is already an unconditional dep — no new dependencies needed.

## Commands

### `sak base64 decode [files...]`

Read base64 from files or stdin, emit raw bytes to stdout.

Flags:
- `--url-safe` — use URL-safe alphabet (for JWTs)
- `--no-pad` — allow unpadded input
- `--strict` — reject whitespace/newlines (default: tolerant, strip them — k8s Secret data fields often have embedded newlines)

### `sak base64 encode [files...]`

Read raw bytes, emit base64.

Flags:
- `--url-safe`, `--no-pad`
- `--wrap <N>` — wrap output at N characters (default: no wrap; pass `76` for MIME)

## Implementation notes

- Reuse the `base64` crate already in `Cargo.toml`.
- Output is binary on decode — write through `BoundedWriter`, but the limit semantics should be in bytes not lines. Check current `BoundedWriter` behavior; if it enforces line-level only, either raise a sibling issue or special-case the `decode` path.
- No feature gate.
- No chokepoint test needed — pure parsing, no mutation surface.

## Composes with other domains

```
sak k8s get secret foo -o json \
  | sak json query '.data."tls.crt"' \
  | sak base64 decode \
  | sak cert inspect
```

This pipeline is the motivation — currently requires `| jq -r '.data."tls.crt"' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout ...`.

title: json diff: structural diff of two JSON documents
description: Add `sak json diff <a.json> <b.json>` that performs a structural diff of two JSON documents and reports added, removed, and changed paths. Output format: '+ path<TAB>value', '- path<TAB>value', '~ path<TAB>old -> new'. Read-only, naturally fits the sak model. Useful for comparing config files or API responses.
close_reason: Implemented as 'sak json diff <a> <b>'. Output: '+ path<TAB>value', '- path<TAB>value', '~ path<TAB>old -> new'. Shared value::diff helper in src/value.rs powers both this and sak config diff (sak-llm-oze). Exit codes follow diff(1): 0=identical, 1=differences, 2=error.

title: Add config merge command (preview a layered merge)
description:
Add `sak config merge <base> <override...>` — show the result of layering one or more override configs onto a base config, written to stdout. Read-only because no files are modified.

## Motivation

Configs are commonly layered (base + environment override + local override). When debugging "what does this config actually look like in production?", an LLM needs to apply the layering manually, which is error-prone. A `merge` command makes the result inspectable in one call.

## Scope and open questions

This is the riskiest command on the list because merge semantics are opinionated. Decisions to make before implementing:

- **Deep merge vs shallow override?** Deep merge is more useful but harder to predict.
- **Array semantics**: replace (last wins) or append? This is the biggest fork — different ecosystems disagree.
- **Type mismatches**: error, or override wins?
- **Output format**: same as the base file's format, or settable via `--format`?

## Suggested defaults

- Deep merge for objects (later overrides win at leaves)
- Replace for arrays (avoids surprises; users who want concat can ask for `--arrays append`)
- Error on type mismatch by default; `--lenient` to let later wins
- Output format defaults to base file's format; `--format` overrides

## Implementation notes

- New `value::merge(base, overrides, opts) -> Value` helper in `src/value.rs`
- Reuse `read_config_inputs` for input
- Reuse the converter from sak-llm-hwc (config convert) for the output side
- Inline tests covering all the open-question scenarios above

## Lower priority because

The semantics are opinionated and risk shipping something users find surprising. Worth doing only after `convert` and `diff` land, since both are dependencies (output formatting reuses convert; verifying merge correctness benefits from diff).

title: gh issue-list: add free-text search filter
description:
## Problem

`sak gh issue-list` supports structured filters (`--author`, `--label`, `--milestone`, etc.) but has no way to do free-text search across issue titles and bodies. This forces users to fall back to raw `gh search issues` for queries like "find all open issues mentioning 1password hash mismatch."

## Proposed solution

Add a `--search <QUERY>` flag that forwards a free-text search term to `gh`. This could either:

1. Pass the query to `gh issue list --search <QUERY>` (if supported), or
2. Delegate to `gh search issues --repo <repo> <QUERY>` under the hood and normalize the output to match `issue-list`'s TSV/JSON format.

## Context

Discovered while trying to find an existing nixpkgs issue for a 1Password build failure using `sak gh issue-list --repo NixOS/nixpkgs --search "1password hash mismatch"`.
close_reason: Added --search <QUERY> to sak gh issue-list, forwarded verbatim to 'gh issue list --search' (option 1 from the issue). Accepts free-text and GitHub search qualifiers; combinable with existing structured filters. Live-tested against cli/cli. Hook already redirects 'gh issue list', so no hook change needed.

title: feat(k8s): owners and children commands
description:
## Context

Two commands that walk `metadata.ownerReferences` to resolve provenance ("where does this pod come from") and inverse provenance ("what does this Deployment own"). LLMs constantly chain three or four `get` calls to figure this out today; one command per direction is the obvious shortcut.

## Commands

### `sak k8s owners <kind> <name>`

Walks `metadata.ownerReferences` *up* to the root, one hop at a time.

Args:
- `<kind>`, `<name>`
- `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output: one line per ancestor, in order from immediate parent to root: `kind/name`. Stops at the first resource with no `ownerReferences`. Cap recursion depth at 8 hops to defend against pathological loops.

Empty output (no owners at all) → exit 1.

### `sak k8s children <kind> <name>`

Walks one level *down*: finds resources whose `ownerReferences` include this object.

Args: same as `owners`.

Output: one line per immediate child: `kind/name`, sorted by `(kind, name)`. Only one level deep — descending recursively explodes badly on Deployments with many ReplicaSet revisions.

Implementation maps each parent kind to its likely-children kinds (the `OWNERSHIP_MAP` table below), lists each child kind in the same namespace, and filters by `ownerReferences[].uid == parent.metadata.uid`. The likely-children list is hardcoded:

| Parent kind   | Child kinds searched              |
|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| Deployment    | ReplicaSet                        |
| StatefulSet   | Pod                               |
| DaemonSet     | Pod                               |
| ReplicaSet    | Pod                               |
| Job           | Pod                               |
| CronJob       | Job                               |
| Service       | EndpointSlice                     |

Anything else → "no known child kinds for {kind}" error, exit 2.

## Implementation notes

- Both commands fetch the parent resource via `client::get_dyn` first to read its `metadata.uid` (children) or `metadata.ownerReferences` (owners). 404 on the parent → exit 1.
- `owners` only needs `get_dyn` calls — one per hop. Cheap.
- `children` does one `list_dyn` call per child kind in the table, then filters in-memory. The table is small enough (max ~2 kinds per parent) to keep this bounded.
- Owner walk uses `metadata.ownerReferences[*].kind` + `.name` to construct the next `get_dyn` call. The `apiVersion` field on the owner reference is used to resolve the right `ApiResource` via `discovery::lookup_builtin` (fast path) or `discovery::resolve` (slow path) — the kind alone is ambiguous if you happen to have a CRD with the same name.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/owners.rs`
- `src/k8s/children.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Owners` and `Children` variants.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s owners pod <some-pod> -n <ns>` — should walk Pod → ReplicaSet → Deployment.
  - `cargo run -- k8s children deployment <some-deploy> -n <ns>` — should list ReplicaSets.
  - `cargo run -- k8s children pod <some-pod> -n <ns>` — should error cleanly.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add config paths command
description:
Add `sak config paths [files...]` — list every leaf path in a config document, sorted, without values. Output is one path per line.

## Motivation

`flatten` already produces `path<TAB>value` lines, so `sak config flatten file.toml | cut -f1` gets you most of the way. A dedicated `paths` command is more discoverable and avoids the pipeline. Useful when an LLM only needs the structure (e.g., to write code that consumes the config) and not the values.

## Scope

- Same flags as `flatten` for consistency: `--separator`, `--max-depth`, `--arrays`, `--format`, `--limit`
- Output sorted lexicographically

## Relationship to existing work

A `json paths` equivalent already exists as **sak-llm-6tn**. Since `value::flatten_value` is shared, both domains should be implemented together — possibly as a flag on `flatten` (e.g., `flatten --paths-only`) rather than a separate command. Worth deciding before implementing either issue.

## Open question

Should this exist as a separate command or just a flag on `flatten`? A flag is simpler but less discoverable in `--help`. Lean toward separate command for LLM discoverability.

## Implementation notes

- Trivially small — reuse `flatten_value` and discard the value column
- Or add `--paths-only` flag to existing `flatten` command and close this in favor of that
close_reason: Added sak config paths as a separate command (per the open question, leaned toward separate command for LLM discoverability). Same flags as config flatten plus --format. Reuses value::flatten_value via the shared helper, so TOML/YAML/plist all work.

title: fix(fs): -n -N (last N lines) rejected by clap arg parser
description:
The `sak fs read --lines/-n` flag advertises a "last N lines" form (`-n -20`) in both the per-command help/examples and in the `prefer-sak.py` agent hook tip, but clap rejects the leading hyphen and treats `-N` as an unknown flag:

```
$ sak fs read /tmp/foo.log -n -20
error: unexpected argument -2 found
  tip: to pass -2 as a value, use -- -2
```

This is a contract violation in a frequently-cited workflow — agents reading the hook tip or the `--help` examples will hit it on their first attempt and have to fall back to `tail -N` (or `sed -n`) via the bypass env var.

## Reproduction
```
sak fs read Cargo.toml -n -20    # fails
sak fs read Cargo.toml -n -5     # fails (any negative N)
```

## Suggested fix
- Cheapest: add `#[arg(allow_hyphen_values = true)]` to the `--lines` field so clap accepts `-N` as a value. Verify it does not break parsing of `-n 1-50` / `-n 100-` forms.
- Alternative: switch the syntax to `-n ..20` (range-style) and update the help/examples + `prefer-sak.py` hint accordingly. Heavier — touches downstream agent settings — but unambiguous.

## Test
Add an inline test under `src/fs/read.rs` that round-trips `-n -20` through clap and asserts the parsed range is "last 20 lines".
close_reason: Added allow_hyphen_values=true to the --lines arg in src/fs/read.rs so clap accepts -n -N. Added two clap round-trip tests pinning -n -20 (last 20) and confirming -n 1-50 / -n 100- still parse. Bumped version to 0.12.1.

Per CLAUDE.md: call out the hook update in the commit message — agents copying these hooks need to update their settings.json manually or the change is silently lost.

title: feat(linux): mounts command
description:
Parse `/proc/self/mountinfo` (richer than `/proc/mounts`). Output: TSV `mount_id<TAB>parent<TAB>device<TAB>root<TAB>mount_point<TAB>options<TAB>fs_type<TAB>source`.

Flags:
- `--type <fs>` — filter by filesystem type (`ext4`, `tmpfs`, ...)
- `--mounts` — fall back to the simpler `/proc/mounts` shape (`device<TAB>mount_point<TAB>fs_type<TAB>options`)
- `--format <tsv|json>`
- `--limit`

mountinfo has variable-length optional fields delimited by `-`; the parser splits on the `-` separator first, then field-by-field. Unit-test on a fixture that includes a bind mount and an overlay mount to cover the optional-field cases.
close_reason: Implemented in src/linux/mounts.rs: pure parse_mountinfo(&str) splits each /proc/self/mountinfo line on the lone '-' separator first so the variable-length optional propagation fields never disturb the six fixed pre-fields or the fs_type/source after. Default TSV mount_id/parent/device/root/mount_point/options/fs_type/source; --mounts falls back to /proc/mounts (device/mount_point/fs_type/options); --type filters by fs type; --format tsv|json (NDJSON); --limit. Path fields keep kernel octal escaping so spaces don't break TSV. Unit-tested on a fixture with a bind mount (non-/ root) and an overlay mount (zero optional fields); verified against real /proc/self/mountinfo.

title: feat(helm): lint command
description:
Wraps `helm lint <chart>`. Validates a chart's structure and syntax. Read-only — never writes the chart, just inspects it.

## Command

### `sak helm lint <chart>`

Flags:
- `--values <file>` — values to lint against (repeatable)
- `--set <k=v>` — inline overrides (repeatable)
- `--strict` — treat warnings as errors (passes through)
- `--with-subcharts` — recurse into dependencies (passes through)
- `--limit`

Output: TSV `severity<TAB>path<TAB>message` for each finding, plus a final summary line `result<TAB>passed|failed<TAB>chart_count<TAB>error_count`.

Exit codes follow sak's convention: 0 = no issues, 1 = at least one finding (matches grep/cert-expiring inversion). Document in `long_about` because helm's own exit code is 0=pass / 1=fail, which inverts naturally for sak's signal-on-finding convention.

Hook: redirect `helm lint` → sak.
close_reason: Implemented sak helm lint <chart>: validates a chart via helm lint. TSV severity/path/message per finding (header + rows) + final summary line result/passed|failed/chart_count/error_count. --values/-f, --set (repeatable), --strict, --with-subcharts, --limit. Parser handles helm reality: findings on stdout, summary on stdout when passing but stderr (Error: prefix) when failing — scans both; empty-path findings ([ERROR] :) and tab-indented continuation lines appended to prior message. Uses invoke (not invoke_ok) so a lint FAILURE (helm exit 1) maps to sak exit 1 not a tool error; absent summary => helm itself errored => exit 2. INVERTED exit convention (like cert expiring): pass=0, fail=1, documented in long_about. Pure parse_findings/parse_summary, fixture-tested on real-shape output incl empty path + continuation + Error:-prefixed summary. Hook: redirects helm lint; tests. README/quick-start/version 0.16.9. Live-verified: pass=exit0, pass --strict=exit0, fail=exit1 with 5 findings parsed correctly. fmt+clippy clean default+lean; 899/592 tests.

title: feat(k8s): can-i command
description:
## Context

Wraps `SelfSubjectAccessReview` — the Kubernetes RBAC self-check API. Equivalent to `kubectl auth can-i`. Critical when an LLM hits a `Forbidden` and needs to know whether to give up, switch context, or escalate to a human.

This command is **read-only with respect to cluster state**, even though the underlying API call is technically a `POST` (`create` on the `SelfSubjectAccessReview` virtual resource). RBAC self-checks are designed to be safe: they check permissions and return a verdict, never persisting anything.

## Command

### `sak k8s can-i <verb> <resource>`

Args:
- `<verb>` — e.g. `get`, `list`, `create`, `delete`, `patch`, `*`
- `<resource>` — e.g. `pods`, `deployments.apps`, `secrets`, `nodes`
- `-n, --namespace <ns>` — optional; omit for cluster-scoped check
- `--subresource <sub>` — e.g. `log`, `status`, `scale`
- `--name <name>` — check on a specific resource name (some authorizers care)

Output: `yes` or `no` on a single line. If denied, also write `reason: <reason>` to stderr (if the apiserver provides one).

Exit codes: 0 if allowed, 1 if denied, 2 on error contacting the apiserver.

## Implementation notes

- The grep test in `client.rs` currently forbids `.create(` on any kube `Api` outside `client.rs`. SelfSubjectAccessReview is the one legitimate read-only "create": the resource is virtual and never persisted. Two implementation options:
  1. Add a dedicated `pub async fn can_i(client, verb, resource_attrs) -> Result<bool>` helper to `client.rs` that wraps the typed `Api::<SelfSubjectAccessReview>::create` call. The helper lives only in `client.rs`, so the grep test still passes for everything else.
  2. Hand-build the POST via the existing `client::request_text` chokepoint (which would need to grow POST support). Cleaner separation but more code.

  **Recommended: option 1.** Add `can_i` to `client.rs` next to `pod_logs` (whenever that lands). Document that `SelfSubjectAccessReview` is the *only* legitimate use of `Api::create` in the entire domain, and that the helper is the chokepoint that contains it.
- The check needs `authorization.k8s.io/v1/SelfSubjectAccessReview` to be available, which it is on every modern apiserver — no graceful-degradation path needed.
- Keep the response shape simple: read `status.allowed` (bool) and `status.reason` (optional string).

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/can_i.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/client.rs` — add `can_i` helper and a small comment block explaining why the `.create(` mention is allowed here. Update the grep test forbidden-token list if necessary so the comment itself does not trip the test (the test already skips comment lines).
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `CanI` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (the chokepoint grep test must still pass — `.create(` only appears in `client.rs`).
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s can-i list pods -n default` — typically `yes`, exit 0.
  - `cargo run -- k8s can-i delete nodes` — typically `no`, exit 1.
  - `cargo run -- k8s can-i get pods/log -n default` — subresource check.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: json size: report size statistics for a document

title: fs grep: read from stdin via '-' path sentinel
description:
sak fs grep cannot read from stdin, so pipelines like 'some-cmd | sak fs grep pattern' silently search the cwd (paths defaults to '.') instead of the piped input. By contrast 'sak fs cut' reads stdin when no files are given.

Add stdin support using the '-' path sentinel (ripgrep/GNU grep convention), which avoids changing the existing default-to-'.' behavior. A path of '-' reads stdin; binary detection is skipped (can't seek stdin); display label is '(standard input)'. Works with -l/-c/-C/-U and mixed with real file paths.

Update --help examples, the fs quick-start in src/main.rs, README discovery list, and add tests.

Refactored search into reader-based cores (search_reader_lines/search_reader_multiline) with thin file wrappers; '-' reads stdin, skips binary detection, labels output '(standard input)'. Works with -l/-c/-C/-U and mixes with file paths. Default-to-'.' behavior unchanged. Updated --help, main.rs quick-start, README; added 3 tests. Patch bump 0.15.7->0.15.8.

title: fix(k8s): use '-' fallback for empty condition/event message column
description:
## Context

`sak k8s describe` and `sak k8s events` both render rows where the rightmost column is the event/condition `message`. When the apiserver returns no message (common for Pod conditions like `PodReadyToStartContainers`, `Initialized`, `ContainersReady` — the apiserver only sets `type` + `status`), the message column falls back to `""` while the other columns in the same row fall back to `-`. The result is a trailing tab and a visible asymmetry:

```
PodReadyToStartContainers<TAB>True<TAB>-<TAB>
Initialized<TAB>True<TAB>-<TAB>
```

## Why this matters

Two reasons, both small:

1. **Internal consistency** — the same section already uses `-` for missing `type`/`status`/`reason`. Mixing `""` and `-` for "no value" within one row is a small irregularity that costs an LLM a few tokens of confusion.
2. **Visible empty cells** — `-` is unambiguously "the field is here and empty," while a trailing tab is easy to miss when scanning output and looks like the row got truncated.

The tab-separated row contract (every row in a section has the same number of fields) is preserved either way — this is purely a fallback-string change.

## Fix

In `src/k8s/describe.rs` (status section, ~line 239) and `src/k8s/events.rs` (`extract_event_row`, ~line 107), change the message fallback from `unwrap_or("")` to `unwrap_or("-")`:

```rust
let message = collapse_newlines(c.get("message").and_then(Value::as_str).unwrap_or("-"));
```

Then update the affected unit tests in `src/k8s/events.rs` (`extract_missing_fields_use_dashes` already expects `""` — flip it to `"-"`) and any describe tests that exercise the path.

Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Changed the message-column fallback from "" to "-" in src/k8s/describe.rs (status conditions section) and src/k8s/events.rs (extract_event_row). Updated events.rs test extract_missing_fields_use_dashes to expect "-". No describe tests exercise write_status_section, so nothing to update there. Patch-level version bump 0.13.2 -> 0.13.3. cargo fmt + clippy + test (all-features and no-default-features) all clean.

title: Add line range exclusion to fs read (--exclude / --invert)
description:
Add the ability to emit a file with one or more line ranges removed -- the inverse of `fs read -n X-Y`.

## Motivation

Came up while editing README.md: needed to remove lines 75-357 from a file. Had to shell out to `awk 'NR<=74 || NR>=358'`. SAK should cover this case directly so LLMs don't reach for awk/sed.

This is read-only and stays within the existing "read a file, emit some of it" model -- the caller redirects stdout to write the result.

## Proposed Design

Extend `sak fs read` with one of:

- `--exclude <ranges>` -- drop the given line ranges, emit everything else
- `--invert` -- invert the meaning of `-n` (emit lines *outside* the specified range)

`--exclude` is more flexible (accepts its own ranges, composes with `-n`). `--invert` is simpler but only works with `-n`.

Recommendation: `--exclude N-M` (with the same range syntax as `-n`), supporting comma-separated multiple ranges like `--exclude 1-10,50-60`.

## Examples

\`\`\`
# Drop lines 75-357 from README.md
sak fs read README.md --exclude 75-357 > /tmp/new && mv /tmp/new README.md

# Drop multiple ranges
sak fs read data.txt --exclude 1-10,90-100
\`\`\`

## Implementation Notes

- Reuse the existing range parser used by `-n`
- `--exclude` and `-n` should be combinable (read range A, then drop sub-ranges)
- Line numbers in output should still reflect original file positions when `--line-numbers` is on
- Add tests covering single range, multiple ranges, and combination with `-n`

title: feat(helm): template command
description:
Wraps `helm template <chart>`. Renders a chart's templates locally without touching the cluster. Critical for LLM-driven chart debugging ("what manifests would this generate?").

## Command

### `sak helm template <chart> [--release-name <name>]`

Flags:
- `--release-name <name>` (positional optional, defaults to `release-name` like helm's own default)
- `--namespace <ns>`
- `--values <file>` — values overlay (repeatable, passes through `-f`)
- `--set <k=v>` — inline value override (repeatable, passes through)
- `--version <ver>`, `--repo <url>` — chart reference plumbing
- `--show-only <template>` — render only matching template (passes through `--show-only`, repeatable)
- `--limit`

Output: rendered YAML multi-document. Pipe through `sak config query` for selectors, or `sak fs grep` for text searches.

Note: `helm template` is intrinsically read-only — it never contacts the cluster (no `--validate` by default), so this is safe regardless of cluster state. Worth calling out in `long_about` because it's not always obvious to operators that `helm template` is offline.

Hook: redirect `helm template` → sak.

Text passthrough via emit_text_to_stdout. invoke_ok: template/chart errors -> exit 2; empty -> exit 1. long_about calls out that helm template is offline (no --validate). build_argv unit-tested (positional name-before-chart, repeatable flags).
