title: fix(cert): accept '-' as stdin alias for file arguments
description:
Follow-up to sak-llm-json-dash-stdin-alias-2gia (json now treats a file arg of exactly "-" as stdin, the cat/jq/grep convention). Apply the same to the cert domain.

Currently cert subcommands read stdin only when no file arg is given; "-" is treated as a literal filename and fails with "cannot read: -: No such file".

Chokepoint: read_cert_inputs in src/cert/mod.rs (used by inspect and expiring) — it already has an empty-files stdin branch that runs the bytes through extract_ders; extend the file loop so path == "-" reads stdin the same way (named <stdin>). Mirror the json is_stdin()/read_source() shape, but note cert reads *bytes* (read_to_end), not strings, because inputs may be raw DER — so the helper must be byte-oriented, not the string read_source json uses.

from-yaml and from-kubeconfig take a single path argument and have their own loaders; decide whether "-" should mean stdin there too (likely yes for from-yaml, which already parses YAML that could arrive on a pipe). expiring keeps its inverted exit-code convention regardless.

Add inline tests (cert has no chokepoint-grep test, so just is_stdin coverage + a named-file read).

title: fix(config): accept '-' as stdin alias for file arguments
description:
Follow-up to sak-llm-json-dash-stdin-alias-2gia, which taught the json domain to treat a file argument of exactly "-" as stdin (cat/jq/grep convention). Apply the same to the config domain for consistency.

Currently config subcommands read stdin only when no file arg is given; passing "-" is treated as a literal filename and fails with "cannot read: -: No such file".

Chokepoint: read_config_inputs in src/config/mod.rs (used by query/exists/keys/flatten/paths/grep/length/schema/type/validate). diff and convert have their own loaders — check and route them too. The json fix added is_stdin() + read_source() helpers; mirror that shape.

Wrinkle unique to config: format is auto-detected from the file *extension*, but stdin has none, so the empty-files branch already requires an explicit --format. A "-" argument has the same problem — when path == "-", require --format (or honor a global --format) rather than trying detect_format on "-". Document that '-' needs --format just like bare stdin does.

Add block/allow-style inline tests mirroring the json ones (is_stdin: "-" yes; a.toml/./-/-- no).

title: fix(csv): accept '-' as stdin alias for file arguments
description:
Follow-up to sak-llm-json-dash-stdin-alias-2gia (json now treats a file arg of exactly "-" as stdin, the cat/jq/grep convention). Apply the same to the csv domain.

Currently csv subcommands read stdin only when no file arg is given; "-" is treated as a literal filename and fails with "cannot read: -: No such file".

Unlike json/config/cert, csv has NO shared read chokepoint — each command opens files itself. headers/query (and stats/validate) build their reader inline with std::fs::File::open(path) and a separate stdin().lock() branch (see src/csv/headers.rs ~L180-186, src/csv/query.rs ~L283-296). Two options:
  (a) hoist a small shared helper into src/csv/mod.rs that yields a BufRead for a path-or-"-" (preferred — gives csv the chokepoint the other domains have), or
  (b) handle "-" per command at each File::open site.

Prefer (a). Keep the per-source display name as <stdin> when the arg is "-", matching json. Mind that csv delimiter handling is flag-driven (not extension-sniffed), so unlike config there's no --format/--delimiter requirement to add — "-" just routes to stdin.

Add inline tests next to the existing ones.

title: feat(linux): network command
description:
Parse `/proc/net/tcp` + `/proc/net/tcp6` + `/proc/net/udp` + `/proc/net/udp6`. Output: TSV `proto<TAB>state<TAB>local<TAB>remote<TAB>uid<TAB>inode`.

Hex addresses get decoded to dotted-quad (v4) or colon-hex (v6); state codes map to names (`0A` → `LISTEN`, `01` → `ESTABLISHED`, `06` → `TIME_WAIT`, ...). Byte order: the kernel writes the address in *host* byte order per 4-byte word, which on little-endian boxes means the dot notation comes out reversed unless we swap — test on a fixture with a known-good v4 + v6 entry.

Flags:
- `--proto <tcp|tcp6|udp|udp6|all>` (default all)
- `--state <LISTEN|ESTABLISHED|...>` — filter by state
- `--format <tsv|json>`
- `--limit`
close_reason: Implemented in src/linux/network.rs: decodes /proc/net/{tcp,tcp6,udp,udp6} into proto/state/local/remote/uid/inode TSV. decode_v4 reverses the kernel's host-byte-order 4 bytes (0100007F -> 127.0.0.1); decode_v6 byte-reverses each of the four 32-bit words before Ipv6Addr::from (00..01000000 -> ::1), rendered bracketed [::1]:port. Ports are hex u16; state hex codes mapped to names (0A->LISTEN, 01->ESTABLISHED, 06->TIME_WAIT, ... unknown passes through). --proto tcp|tcp6|udp|udp6|all (missing family files skipped), --state filter (case-insensitive), --format tsv|json (NDJSON), --limit; sorted by (proto,local,remote). 7 unit tests on known-good v4+v6 fixtures (byte-order, state map, header skip, full line parse). Verified against real /proc/net (sshd 0.0.0.0:22, cups [::1]:631).

title: feat(k8s): events command
description:
## Context

Second-most-used debug command after `logs`. When something is broken, the first question is always "what does the apiserver have to say about it" — and the answer lives in the `events` stream. `kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp` is what every SRE has aliased; sak should ship that out of the box, plus an `--for kind/name` filter so the LLM can ask "events about *this* object" without piping through `sak fs grep`.

## Command

### `sak k8s events`

Args:
- `-n, --namespace <ns>` / `-A, --all-namespaces` (mutually exclusive — same shape as `get`)
- `--for <kind/name>` — filter to events whose `involvedObject` matches this kind/name. Kind is resolved through `discovery::lookup_builtin` (no apiserver call) for canonicalization.
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `last<TAB>type<TAB>reason<TAB>kind/name<TAB>message`, sorted by `lastTimestamp` *descending* (newest first — opposite of sak default, but events are time-series and the LLM almost always wants the most recent first). If `lastTimestamp` is null, fall back to `eventTime`, then `firstTimestamp`.

Exit codes per sak convention: 0 found, 1 none, 2 error.

## Implementation notes

- Events live at `core/v1/Event`. Use the existing `list_dyn` chokepoint with the resolved `ApiResource`.
- `--for` is applied client-side after fetching: filter on `involvedObject.kind` (case-insensitive) and `involvedObject.name` (exact). Keep it client-side because field selectors on Event nesting are unreliable across apiserver versions.
- Multi-line `message` fields should be collapsed to a single line (replace `\n` with ` `) so each event stays one row.
- Timestamps: emit in RFC3339 in UTC for determinism. Do not localize.
- All output through `BoundedWriter`.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/events.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Events` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (add unit tests for the timestamp-fallback ordering on hand-built fixtures).
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s events -A --limit 20` — recent events cluster-wide.
  - `cargo run -- k8s events --for pod/some-pod -n some-ns` — events for one object.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented src/k8s/events.rs: lists cluster events sorted by lastTimestamp desc with eventTime/firstTimestamp fallback, --for KIND/NAME client-side filter (kind canonicalized via lookup_builtin), multi-line message collapse, --namespace/-A scoping, --limit. Helpers (EventRow, fetch_events_for, format_event_row, collapse_newlines) exposed pub(super) so describe can reuse them. Unit tests cover timestamp fallback ordering, message sanitization, sort tiebreaking, and case-insensitive kind matching.

title: feat(k8s): neat command to strip apiserver-injected fields
description:
`sak k8s get` returns objects with `managedFields`, `resourceVersion`, `uid`, `creationTimestamp`, `generation`, `selfLink`, `status` (sometimes), etc. — fine for live inspection, useless for diffing two clusters or comparing against a manifest.

`k8s neat` is the post-processing step that makes those diffs meaningful.

## Command

### `sak k8s neat`

Reads JSON or YAML from stdin (or `--file <path>`), strips apiserver-injected fields, emits the same format.

Default strip set:
- `metadata.managedFields`
- `metadata.resourceVersion`
- `metadata.uid`
- `metadata.creationTimestamp`
- `metadata.generation`
- `metadata.selfLink`
- `metadata.ownerReferences.*.uid`
- `metadata.annotations."kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration"`
- empty `metadata.annotations` / `metadata.labels` if they become empty after stripping

Flags:
- `--keep-status` — preserve `.status` (off by default)
- `--keep <jsonpath,...>` — keep specific fields that would otherwise be stripped
- `--strip <jsonpath,...>` — extra fields to remove
- `--format json|yaml` — output format (default: match input format)

## Implementation notes

- Pure function over `serde_json::Value` — no apiserver contact. Belongs in `src/k8s/neat.rs`, testable on hand-built fixtures (no cluster needed).
- Reuse `value::resolve_expression` for `--keep` / `--strip` path parsing.
- This is the prerequisite for any future `k8s diff` command and pairs naturally with `cargo run -- json diff` / `config diff`.
- Cross-domain composability: `sak k8s get deployment foo -o json | sak k8s neat | sak json diff - other.json`.

## No README hook needed

Not a kubectl read verb — `neat` is a sak-specific transform.

title: feat(hash): new domain for file/stdin hashing
description:
Universal need that currently requires shelling to `shasum` / `openssl dgst`. `cert` already pulled `sha2` into the unconditional dep set, so SHA-256 is essentially free.

## Commands

### `sak hash sha256 [files...]`
### `sak hash sha1 [files...]`
### `sak hash md5 [files...]`
### `sak hash blake3 [files...]`

Output: `<hex>  <path>` (two-space sep, matching `shasum`/`sha256sum` so output is parseable by existing tools). Reads stdin when no files given; in that case prints just the hash with no path.

Flags:
- `--binary` — print hex of raw digest only (when stdin)
- `--verify <sumfile>` — read a sumfile (`<hex>  <path>` lines) and check each file. Exit 0 if all match, 1 if any mismatch, 2 on error.

## Implementation notes

- `sha2` for sha256/sha1 (already in deps from `cert`).
- `md-5` for md5 (separate small crate).
- `blake3` for blake3 (one crate, very fast).
- Streaming: read in 64KB chunks via the `Digest` trait, never buffer whole file.
- No domain feature gate — `sha2` is already unconditional, the others are tiny and have no native deps.
- Output through `BoundedWriter`.

## Why a domain, not `fs hash`

Hashing isn't filesystem-specific (also wraps stdin pipes, base64 outputs from `cert`/`sqlite`, etc.). A top-level domain is more discoverable and parallels `cert`/`base64`/`hash` as "data inspection" siblings.

## Composes with future `base64` domain

```
sak k8s get secret foo -o json \
  | sak json query '.data."tls.crt"' \
  | sak base64 decode \
  | sak hash sha256
```
close_reason: Implemented sak hash domain (sha256/sha1/md5/blake3) in src/hash/mod.rs: streaming 64KB-chunk hashing of files/stdin, shasum-compatible '<hex>  <path>' output, --binary (hex only) and --verify <sumfile> (sha256sum-style OK/FAILED, exit 0/1/2). Always-on (sha1/md-5/blake3 are tiny pure-Rust deps; sha2 already present). Wired into main.rs + quick-start; extended the Claude Code hook to redirect sha256sum/sha1sum/md5sum/shasum/b3sum and 'openssl dgst'; updated CLAUDE.md + README. fmt/clippy/test clean on default and --no-default-features.

title: feat(json): table --where predicate filtering
description:
Follow-up to `sak json table` adding a predicate flag so callers can filter the array before projection. Depends on the table command landing first.

## Flag

`--where '<expr>'` — repeatable; multiple predicates AND together.

Predicate grammar (tight, deliberately not jq):

```
<path> <op> <literal>
```

where:
- `<path>` is a dot-path or JSON Pointer into the element (same syntax `sak json query` already accepts)
- `<op>` is one of `=`, `!=`, `~` (regex match against stringified value), `!~`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`
- `<literal>` is `"<str>"`, `<number>`, `true`, `false`, or `null`

## Examples

```
sak json table .issues -f id,title --where '.priority < 3'
sak json table .issues -f id,title --where '.title ~ "^feat\\(nix\\)"'
sak json table .issues -f id,title --where '.priority < 3' --where '.status = "open"'
```

## Why not full jq

`jq` re-implementation is out of scope. The grammar above covers the 80% case from triage scripts; anything more should pipe through real jq or `sak fs grep` on the TSV output.

## Implementation notes

- Tiny hand-rolled parser. Three-production grammar — path, op, literal.
- Pure function `eval(predicate, element) -> bool`, unit-testable on hand-built JSON fixtures.
- Document the stringification surprise: `~` on a non-string value stringifies it first (via `serde_json::Value::to_string()`), which means `1` matches `~ "^1$"`. Worth calling out in `long_about` so users don't expect type-strict regex.
- Reuse the path parser from `src/value.rs` so dot-notation and JSON Pointer both work in predicates.

## Out of scope

- Boolean operators (`AND`/`OR`/`NOT` in a single expression) — multiple `--where` flags AND together, and that's enough. Anything richer is jq's job.
- Subscript / array access inside the predicate path — keep path syntax identical to `sak json query`.
- `--where-not` — a `!=`/`!~` op already covers the common negation cases.

title: feat(k8s): not-ready command — condition-based health triage for any kind
description:
Generalize pod triage to any condition-bearing resource. `sak k8s failing|pending|restarts` only understand Pods, but the `.status.conditions[]` pattern (find the element with `type == X`, read its `status`/`reason`/`message`) is how Flux (Kustomization, HelmRelease), cert-manager (Certificate), Deployments, and most CRDs report health. Listing the ones that are not Ready is a constant GitOps triage need.

Today this needs a `python3 -c` fallback: `sak k8s get <kind> -A -o json` then iterate `.items[]`, dig into `.status.conditions[]`, pick `type==Ready`, and compare `.status`. Hit this checking "are all 100 Kustomizations Ready?" after a shared-component change.

## Why not json table --where (sak-llm-e8h)

`json table --where` filters the *top-level* array and projects field paths, but it explicitly puts "subscript / array access inside the predicate path" out of scope. Selecting the `conditions[]` element where `type==Ready` is exactly that nested-array predicate, so the conditions pattern stays out of reach. A small k8s-domain command that knows the conditions convention is the cleaner fit, and sits naturally next to the existing failing/pending/restarts family.

## Command

### `sak k8s not-ready <kind> [-A | -n <ns>]`

Lists objects of `<kind>` whose target condition is not at the wanted status (or is absent entirely).

- `<kind>` — any condition-bearing kind (`kustomization`, `helmrelease`, `certificate`, `deployment`, ...). Resolved through the same discovery path `sak k8s get` already uses.
- `-A, --all-namespaces` / `-n, --namespace` — same scoping flags as `get`.
- `--condition <type>` — condition type to inspect. Default `Ready` (covers Flux + cert-manager). Lets callers target `Available` for Deployments, etc.
- `--status <want>` — the healthy value. Default `True`; rows are emitted where actual status != want, including a missing condition.
- `--limit` — output truncation via BoundedWriter.

## Example

```
$ sak k8s not-ready kustomization -A
NAMESPACE     NAME          STATUS  REASON                 MESSAGE
self-hosted   tracefinity   False   ReconciliationFailed   ReplicationDestination ... must be of type string
```

(empty output + exit 0 when everything is at the wanted status)

## Implementation notes

- Reuse the dynamic list/get machinery behind `sak k8s get` (`k8s::client::get_dyn` / list); do not add a second client path. Read-only chokepoint stays in `src/k8s/client.rs`.
- Pure function `fn unmet(obj: &Value, cond_type: &str, want: &str) -> Option<Row>` operating on `.status.conditions[]` — unit-testable on hand-built JSON fixtures (Ready=True, Ready=False, condition absent, no status block at all).
- Sanitize tab/newline in `message` to preserve the column contract (same defense as `sak sqlite info`).
- Exit-code behavior: mirror whatever `failing`/`pending` already do (do not invert like `cert expiring`); pin it in tests.
- TSV/`--json` output shape consistent with the other k8s diagnostic commands.

## Out of scope

- Aggregating multiple condition types in one pass — one `--condition` per invocation.
- Pod-specific reasons (CrashLoopBackOff, etc.) — that is what `failing`/`restarts` already cover.
- Boolean logic across conditions.
close_reason: Implemented sak k8s not-ready <kind>: lists objects whose .status.conditions[] element of --condition (default Ready) is not at --status (default True), or is absent. Hybrid of get.rs (arbitrary-kind discovery + cluster/namespaced scope guard) and pending.rs (conditions walking). Pure unmet() fn unit-tested (Ready=True skip, Ready=False row, condition absent, no status block, custom --condition Available, newline collapse). Output: namespace/name<TAB>status<TAB>reason<TAB>message, no header, exit 0=matches/1=none (standard). No hook change (kubectl get already covered). Version bumped patch. fmt/clippy/test (default + --no-default-features) clean; live-verified against home-ops cluster.

title: Add config count command
description:
Add `sak config count <path> [files...]` — return the count of items at a path: array length for arrays, key count for objects, character count for strings, 1 for scalars, 0 for null.

## Motivation

LLMs often want to know "how many X are in this config?" before deciding how to process it. Doing this with `query` plus shell tools is awkward (need to parse JSON output, count elements). A dedicated command is cleaner.

## Output

A single integer on stdout. Exit code 0 if the path exists, 1 if missing.

## Relationship to existing work

Two json equivalents exist as separate issues: **sak-llm-keo** (`json length`) and **sak-llm-ym6** (`json count`). The naming overlap suggests these might want to be unified before implementing — `length` is cleaner for "how big is this thing?" while `count` more naturally suggests counting matches of a predicate. Worth resolving the json domain naming first, then adopting the same name in `config`.

## Implementation notes

- Once the json equivalent lands, the config version is a near-copy with `read_json_inputs` swapped for `read_config_inputs`
- The counting helper itself should live in `src/value.rs` so both domains share it

title: Add json exists command
description:
Add `sak json exists <path> [files...]` — boolean check whether a path exists in a JSON document. Exit code only (0 = exists, 1 = missing); produces no stdout output.

## Motivation

Exit-code-driven commands are valuable in agent loops and shell pipelines:

```sh
sak json exists .features.beta config.json && enable_beta_path
```

`query` already returns exit code 1 for missing paths, but it also writes the value to stdout when found, which is wasted output for a pure existence check. A dedicated `exists` command makes the intent clear in `--help` and avoids the spurious output.

## Scope

- Same path syntax as `query` (dot notation + JSON Pointer, via `value::resolve_expression`)
- Reads from stdin if no files are given
- Multiple files: exit 0 if **all** files contain the path; exit 1 if any are missing (or `--any` flag to flip the semantics?)
- No stdout output by default; `--verbose` could echo the value if found

## Relationship to other domains

A sibling `config exists` issue exists as **sak-llm-ozn**. Both should be implemented together since they both call `value::resolve_expression` and the only difference is the input reader (`read_json_inputs` vs `read_config_inputs`).

## Implementation notes

- Trivially small — `src/json/exists.rs` calls `resolve_expression` and returns the appropriate ExitCode
- Inline tests for present/absent paths, root path, JSON Pointer syntax
close_reason: Implemented in src/json/exists.rs. Same path syntax as query (dot + JSON Pointer via resolve_expression), stdin support, --any flag flips the default 'all files must contain' semantics to 'any file contains'. No stdout output; exit code only (0 = exists, 1 = missing).

title: feat(sqlite): tables command
description:
## Context

First read command in the new \`sqlite\` domain. Lists tables and views in a SQLite database. The "what is in this file" answer that every LLM session asking about a \`.db\` file starts with.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite tables <db>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`--views\` — also include views (off by default — listing only tables is the common case)
- \`--system\` — include internal \`sqlite_*\` tables (off by default)
- \`--limit <n>\`

Source query (when neither \`--views\` nor \`--system\` is set):

\`\`\`sql
SELECT name, type FROM sqlite_master
WHERE type = 'table' AND name NOT LIKE 'sqlite_%'
ORDER BY name
\`\`\`

With \`--views\`: include \`type = 'view'\`. With \`--system\`: drop the \`NOT LIKE 'sqlite_%'\` clause.

Output: \`name<TAB>type\`, sorted by name. Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

Exit codes: 0 if any rows, 1 if zero rows, 2 on error.

## Implementation notes

- Open the database via \`client::open_readonly\` from the foundation issue.
- Run the query through \`client::query_rows\` (do **not** import \`rusqlite::Connection\` directly — chokepoint test will fail).
- All output through \`BoundedWriter\`.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/tables.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Tables\` variant to \`SqliteCommand\` and dispatch.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — add unit tests over a temp database with at least one user table, one view, and one \`sqlite_*\` internal table, exercising the default and \`--views\` / \`--system\` filters.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite tables some.db\`.
- \`sak sqlite tables --help\` has \`long_about\` and \`after_help\` examples per sak conventions.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented sak sqlite tables. Added client::query_rows chokepoint helper (returns Vec<Vec<String>>) and a cfg(test) seed_for_tests helper so sibling tests can build fixtures without importing Connection. tables.rs supports --views, --system, --limit, emits name<TAB>type via BoundedWriter, exit 0/1/2. Unit tests cover default, --views, --system, success, empty (exit 1), missing-file (exit 2). cargo test passes with --all-features and --no-default-features; clippy + fmt clean; chokepoint grep test still green. Patch bump 0.7.0 -> 0.7.1.

title: feat(fs): glob accept multiple positional patterns
description:
Currently `sak fs glob` takes one pattern: `sak fs glob **/*.rs [path]`. Matching "any of N files" requires N invocations, e.g. to check for nix dev-shell hints in a repo I want:

```
sak fs glob flake.nix shell.nix default.nix .envrc* .
```

Today that has to be done as four separate calls (or, more dangerously, naive concatenation — I tried `sak fs glob flake.* shell.nix default.nix .envrc* .` and sak silently returned nothing because it parsed the space-joined string as one literal pattern).

## Proposed shape
- `sak fs glob <PATTERN>... [path]` — accept N patterns positionally, OR-semantics (any match qualifies).
- `globset::GlobSetBuilder` already supports adding multiple patterns and matching against the set, so the implementation is a small change to the call site.
- Existing single-pattern usage stays compatible (one positional arg).
- Path argument detection: if the last positional looks like an existing directory (not a glob with `*`/`?`/`[`/`{`), treat it as the path; otherwise default to `.`. Mirror current single-pattern behavior so `sak fs glob **/*.rs` still works without a path.

## Prior art
- `find -name X -o -name Y`
- `rg -g X -g Y`
- `fd X Y`

## Test
Inline test under `src/fs/glob.rs`: build a tempdir with `a.rs`, `b.toml`, `c.md`; assert `glob *.rs *.toml` returns `a.rs` + `b.toml` but not `c.md`, sorted.
close_reason: fs glob now accepts N positional patterns with OR-semantics via GlobSetBuilder. Trailing existing-directory arg (non-glob) is peeled off as the search root by split_patterns_path; lone positional stays a pattern so single-pattern usage is unchanged. Added split_patterns_path + multi-pattern inline tests; help/README/main.rs quick-start updated; version 0.17.0->0.17.1.

title: Add NixOS flake for packaging sak
description:
Add a flake.nix so sak is consumable on NixOS systems via flake input.

## Approach

- Use `rustPlatform.buildRustPackage` (standard nixpkgs pattern)
- No flake-utils — use `nixpkgs.lib.genAttrs` directly
- Bundled libgit2 (git2 crate default, no system libgit2 dependency)

## Details

- inputs: nixpkgs-unstable
- outputs: `packages.<system>.default` for x86_64-linux, aarch64-linux, x86_64-darwin, aarch64-darwin
- nativeBuildInputs: pkg-config (needed by git2 bundled build)
- buildInputs: openssl (Linux), Security + SystemConfiguration frameworks (macOS)
- cargoLock.lockFile = ./Cargo.lock
- Source filtering: exclude .git, target, .beads from Nix store copy
- Add `result` to .gitignore (Nix build symlink)
- Install-only — no devShell needed

## Consumer usage

```nix
# In a NixOS or home-manager flake:
inputs.sak.url = "github:patsoffice/sak";
environment.systemPackages = [ inputs.sak.packages.${system}.default ];
```

## Verification

```bash
nix build
./result/bin/sak --version
nix flake check
```
close_reason: Added flake.nix with rustPlatform.buildRustPackage, bundled libgit2, multi-platform support. Builds and passes nix flake check.

description: Umbrella for non-duplication refactoring opportunities from the 2026-05-22 source review (complexity, structure, naming, error handling, exit-code consistency). title: refactor: split value.rs into submodules + WalkOpts for recursive walkers
description: src/value.rs (~1132 lines) does three independent jobs: path resolution (PathSegment/parse_dot_path/resolve_path/resolve_expression, dot + JSON Pointer); the diff machinery (DiffKind/DiffEntry/diff/diff_walk/format_diff_entry); and recursive walkers (flatten_value/collect_keys/collect_schema/grep_walk). Split into src/value/{path,diff,walk}.rs re-exported from value/mod.rs so each subsystem and its tests live together. While there: the three walkers have noisy signatures mixing traversal state (current_depth, max_depth) with behavior flags (show_types, separator, ArrayMode) and disagree on depth type (collect_keys uses usize, collect_schema uses Option<usize>) -- introduce a WalkOpts struct and standardize depth. Also extract the copy-pasted prefix-join idiom (~10 sites) into a build_path helper. Keep the public surface consumed by json/ and config/ stable.
close_reason: Split src/value.rs (~1132 lines) into src/value/{path,diff,walk}.rs re-exported flat from value/mod.rs (globs, so crate::value::* stays stable); the small cross-cutting helpers (type_name/value_length/format_value) stay in mod.rs. Introduced WalkOpts { max_depth: Option<usize>, show_types, separator, arrays } with Default + with_max_depth(); collect_keys/flatten_value/collect_schema now take (value, &opts, out) with current_depth threaded by private *_inner fns instead of being in the public signature, and depth standardized to Option<usize> (collect_keys was bare usize). Extracted the ~10 prefix-join sites into path::build_path(prefix, segment, sep, leading_sep) — leading_sep=false for keys/flatten (bare root), true for schema/diff/grep (dotted root); used by all walkers + diff. Updated json/config callers (keys, flatten, paths, schema, incl. their test helpers). Behavior unchanged: full test suite (incl. 66 value tests) + clippy clean on default and --no-default-features.

title: fix: replace metadata .expect() in fs/glob with propagated error
description: src/fs/glob.rs:145 falls back to std::fs::metadata(...).expect(cannot read metadata) when entry.metadata() fails. A walkdir entry can fail to stat on a TOCTOU race or permission change between dir-read and stat, so this can panic on real input. Replace with ? + .with_context() so it surfaces as a normal sak: error (exit 2) instead of a panic. This is the one genuine production expect() found in the review; the others were test-only or serialize-and-str-to-JSON which cannot fail.
close_reason: src/fs/glob.rs: replaced the metadata fallback panic (entry.metadata().unwrap_or_else(|_| std::fs::metadata(path).expect("cannot read metadata"))) with a match that keeps the walkdir-cached → direct-stat fallback but propagates a final failure via std::fs::metadata(path).with_context(|| ...)? instead of expect(). A walkdir entry can fail to stat on a TOCTOU race / permission change between dir-read and stat, so this now surfaces as a normal 'sak: error: ...' (exit 2) rather than a panic. run() already returns Result<ExitCode> and anyhow::Context was already imported. Behavior unchanged on the happy path (entry.metadata() Ok). fmt + clippy clean on default and --no-default-features; full + lean test suites pass; verified glob still lists normally.

title: chore: audit exit-code (0/1/2) and error-prefix consistency across domains
description: Two verified consistency gaps vs documented conventions. (1) CSV commands (csv/query, csv/headers, csv/stats) always return ExitCode::SUCCESS even on zero results, unlike git/json/config/fs/docker which return exit 1 for no-results; either align csv with the 0/1/2 convention or document the exception the way cert expiring documents its inversion. (2) validate commands (json/validate, config/validate, csv/validate) print per-file diagnostics to stderr without the sak: error: prefix that main.rs applies to returned errors; decide whether this grep-style per-file output is intentional (document it) or should route through the standard error path. This is an audit + design decision, not a mechanical change.
close_reason: Resolved both gaps. (1) Aligned sak csv query to the 0/1 convention: process_source now sets a found_any out-param when a data row passes the filter (before the write, so a --limit-truncated match still counts, matching json/config query); run() returns ExitCode::from(1) when no row matched. The output header is decoration (write_decoration) and never counts. Added no_matching_rows_exits_one test; the two existing SUCCESS tests still match rows. csv query long_about updated to state the 0/1/2 convention. (2) Documented the rest as intentional rather than changing: csv headers/stats are not searches (always produce output for a valid file → always exit 0, no 'no results' state); the validate commands (json/config/csv) are deliberately grep/linter-style — per-file 'name: diagnostic' to stderr WITHOUT the sak: error: prefix, exit 1 = 'found invalid files' (a negative result), exit 2 reserved for tool failure. Both documented in CLAUDE.md gotchas; validate long_abouts already described the observable behavior. No behavior change to headers/stats/validate. fmt + clippy clean on default and --no-default-features; full + lean tests pass. (Safe to change csv query exit code — nothing consumes sak csv yet.)

title: feat(gh): api command (GET-only escape hatch)
description:
Wraps `gh api <endpoint>` as the catch-all GET escape hatch for any REST or GraphQL call sak doesn't otherwise cover. The foundation chokepoint enforces GET-only (rejects `-X / --method` with any value other than `GET`).

## Command

### `sak gh api <endpoint>`

Flags:
- `--repo <owner/name>` — substituted into endpoint placeholders (passes through `--repo`)
- `--header <K:V>` — request header, repeatable (passes through `-H`)
- `--field <K=V>` — typed parameter, repeatable (passes through `-F`)
- `--raw-field <K=V>` — string parameter, repeatable (passes through `-f`)
- `--paginate` — follow pagination (passes through; gh handles per-page chaining)
- `--jq <filter>` — server-side jq filter applied by `gh` (passes through `--jq`)
- `--cache <duration>` — local cache TTL (passes through; pure read optimization)
- `--limit`

Output: response body verbatim, streamed through `BoundedWriter` so `--limit` truncates cleanly.

Reject conditions (handled at the chokepoint):
- `-X POST` / `-X PUT` / `-X PATCH` / `-X DELETE` → hard error mentioning GET-only policy
- `--method <anything-non-GET>` → same

Allowed:
- bare `gh api <endpoint>` (defaults to GET)
- `-X GET` / `--method GET` (explicit GET — redundant but legal)
- GraphQL via `gh api graphql -f query=...` — uses POST under the hood, but `-X` is not present and the conventional invocation is canonical. The chokepoint *does not* special-case GraphQL — accepting `graphql` as the endpoint is fine because the `-X` guard only fires when `-X` is supplied.

That last point is subtle: GraphQL is *technically* POST but the read-only invariant holds for GraphQL queries (mutations require an explicit `mutation { ... }` keyword in the body, not a different HTTP method). The chokepoint cannot reliably parse GraphQL query strings; users wanting strict no-mutation discipline should pipe queries through a body validator before calling sak.

Hook: redirect `gh api` → sak when no `-X` flag is present, or when `-X GET` is present.
close_reason: Landed sak gh api — GET-only REST/GraphQL escape hatch. src/gh/api.rs: positional <endpoint> plus --repo (-> --repo), --header (-> -H, repeatable), --field (-> -F, repeatable), --raw-field (-> -f, repeatable), --paginate, --jq (-> --jq), --cache (-> --cache), --limit. No -X/--method flag is ever exposed, so every call is GET by construction; the foundation chokepoint also rejects any smuggled non-GET method as defense-in-depth. Response body streamed verbatim through BoundedWriter (line-split); empty body -> exit 1. Inline tests assert flag->gh-spelling mapping, bare-endpoint emits no -X, and repeatable-flag order. Wired Api into GhCommand dispatch and removed the temporary #![allow(dead_code)] from client.rs now that invoke_ok is consumed. Hook: added check_gh + gh_api_method_is_get to src/hook/claude_code.rs (routed via 'gh' in check()) — redirects 'gh api' to 'sak gh api' only for GET (no -X, or -X/--method GET); non-GET and mutating/unshadowed verbs pass through. Tests in src/hook/tests.rs (gh_api_get_blocks, gh_api_non_get_allows, gh_mutations_and_unshadowed_allow). Docs: main.rs QUICK_START example, README + CLAUDE.md dogfood bullets + hook-coverage lists. Version 0.14.0 -> 0.14.1 (patch: new command in existing domain). cargo fmt / clippy (default + --no-default-features) / test (default + --no-default-features) all clean. Note for older sak versions: hook now redirects 'gh api' GET, so upgrade to pick it up.

title: feat(talos): optional read-only Talos domain (certs, fan-out read)
description:
Add an optional `talos` domain to `sak` for read-only inspection of a Talos Linux cluster. Talos exposes a rich, structured COSI resource model (\`talosctl get …\`) plus an on-disk filesystem (\`talosctl read …\`), and a great deal of cluster-health triage requires fanning a command across every control-plane node and stitching the results. `sak talos` could absorb that fan-out and provide higher-level views that span nodes.

## Scope decision needed

This issue is partially out of scope — `sak` to date doesn't wrap operator-distribution-specific tooling. Two paths forward:

1. **In scope:** add a small \`talos\` subdomain. Justified because `talosctl` *is* read-only for the relevant operations, and Talos is a common Kubernetes distribution where these queries arise.
2. **Out of scope:** document the recommended `talosctl` invocations in the `sak` README's \"adjacent tools\" section and stop there.

Filed as P3 to surface the conversation. If the answer is (2), close with a documentation update.

## Real-world motivation

During home-ops cert-expiry triage, the same fan-out shell loop was written 5+ times:

```sh
for n in 192.168.1.10 192.168.1.11 192.168.1.12 192.168.1.13 192.168.1.14; do
  echo -n \"\$n: \"
  talosctl -n \$n read /system/secrets/kubernetes/kube-apiserver/apiserver-kubelet-client.crt \\
    | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates
done
```

…repeated for `front-proxy-client.crt`, `kube-controller-manager/kubeconfig`, `kube-scheduler/kubeconfig`, `/var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem`, etc. Every loop was identical except for the cert path.

## Commands (if in-scope)

### `sak talos certs [--node <ip|all>]`
Enumerate every static-pod and kubelet client cert across all nodes (or a single node), printing a table with node, role, path, subject, NotAfter, days remaining. Default: all control-plane nodes.

Internally uses the companion `sak cert` domain for parsing.

### `sak talos read <path> [--node <ip|all>]`
Wrapper for `talosctl read` with implicit fan-out across all nodes. Outputs per-node sections with `--all` or just the named node with `--node`.

### `sak talos get <type> [name] [--node <ip|all>]`
Wrapper for `talosctl get` that fans out, with the same `--path` extraction semantics as `sak k8s get`.

### `sak talos endpoints` / `sak talos members`
Show cluster membership (etcd members, control-plane node list) and endpoint health. Surfaces what `talosctl etcd members` and `talosctl health` show today, but composable.

## Connectivity

- Honor `\$TALOSCONFIG` (and document it)
- Auto-detect control-plane node IPs from the talosconfig endpoints
- `--node all` = parallelize across endpoints

## Implementation notes

- Don't reimplement the Talos COSI client — shell out to `talosctl` and parse stdout (it has stable `-o yaml` / `-o json` output)
- Strictly read-only — never accept any `talosctl` subcommand that mutates
- A small allowlist (\`get\`, \`read\`, \`list\`, \`version\`, \`health\`, \`etcd members\`) keeps the safety boundary tight

## Why now / leverage

- The fan-out loop is the worst-offending pattern in home-ops triage
- Reuses the proposed `sak cert` domain so the value compounds
- If the answer is \"not in scope,\" this issue still captures the loop pattern so the README can recommend a snippet
close_reason: Implemented sak talos domain with certs/read/get. Shells out to system talosctl rather than re-implementing the COSI client; no new Rust deps. Read-only enforcement is 3-layer: READ_ONLY_VERBS allowlist (get/read/version) refusing mutating verbs at runtime, chokepoint grep test banning Command::new + 'talosctl' outside src/talos/client.rs, and a runtime test exercising the rejection path. talos certs is the killer use case: replaces 'for n in <ips>; do talosctl -n $n read … | openssl x509 …; done' fan-out loops by piping each per-node read through cert::extract_ders + cert::parse_cert. Smoke-tested against home-ops cluster; surfaces a real finding (kubelet cert on one node has only ~118 days remaining). endpoints/members from the original spec deferred to follow-ups.

title: feat(docker): logs command
description:
Asymmetric gap: `sak k8s logs` exists but `sak docker logs` doesn't, even though docker has list/info/config/images.

## Command

### `sak docker logs <container>`

GET `/containers/<id-or-name>/logs?stdout=1&stderr=1&timestamps=...&tail=...&since=...`.

Flags:
- `--tail <N>` (default 100, `all` for everything)
- `--since <duration>` — last N (reuse the prom duration parser)
- `--timestamps` — include daemon timestamps
- `--stdout` / `--stderr` — only one stream
- `--grep <regex>` — filter lines (mirror `k8s logs --grep`)
- `--limit` — output truncation via BoundedWriter
- `--previous` — N/A for docker, exit with helpful error if passed

## Stream demux

Docker returns a multiplexed binary stream when both stdout+stderr are requested: 8-byte header (`stream_type:1, _:3, length:4 BE`) followed by `length` bytes of payload. When TTY is enabled on the container, the stream is raw (no headers). Detect TTY from a prior `info` call's `Config.Tty`, or use the response's `Content-Type` header.

`--all-streams` mode prefixes each line with `[stdout]` / `[stderr]` like `k8s logs --all-containers`.

## Implementation notes

- `DockerClient::get_json` returns a parsed JSON body — won't work for log streams. Add `DockerClient::get_stream(path) -> Result<impl Read>` to the chokepoint and demux in `logs.rs`.
- 404 (no such container) → exit 1.
- The 8-byte header demuxer is testable on a hand-built fixture; no daemon needed.
- Read-only enforcement test still passes — `get_stream` is GET only.
- Streaming line-by-line so `--limit` truncates cleanly without buffering whole logs.

## README hook update

Add `docker logs` to the docker hook regex in README.md alongside `ps|inspect|images`. Call out the hook change in the commit message — users running Claude Code need to update their own settings.json.

title: feat(helm): get command
description:
Wraps `helm get` (with subcommands `all`, `manifest`, `values`, `notes`, `hooks`). Selects which slice of a release to dump.

## Command

### `sak helm get <release>`

Flags:
- `--what <all|manifest|values|notes|hooks>` (default `all`) — which slice to fetch
- `--namespace <ns>`
- `--revision <n>` — specific revision (passes through `--revision`)
- `--limit`

Manifest output is YAML (helm's native shape); pipe through `sak config query` for structured access. Values output is YAML or JSON depending on what helm emits; same downstream story.

Implementation: chokepoint subverb is `None` (helm uses positional subcommands for get's slices, e.g. `helm get manifest <release>` — the chokepoint forwards them as positional args). Sak validates `--what` against the known slice list before invoking.

Hook: redirect `helm get all|manifest|values|notes|hooks` → sak.
close_reason: Implemented sak helm get <release>: dumps a release slice via helm get <what> <release>. --what all|manifest|values|notes|hooks (ValueEnum, clap-validated, default all), -n/--namespace via Conn, --revision passthrough, --limit. Output is helm native text streamed verbatim via new helm::emit_text_to_stdout (reuses stream_verbatim with empty marker). values slice forces -o yaml to strip helm table format USER-SUPPLIED VALUES: header so it pipes cleanly into sak config (other slices have no -o flag). Missing release -> exit 1 via new client::invoke_found (maps helm not-found stderr to Ok(None), mirrors k8s get_dyn); status.rs refactored to use it too (dropped bespoke detection + bail import). Hook: check_helm redirects helm get; tests. README/quick-start/version 0.16.3. Live-verified: manifest YAML, values header-free YAML piped into sak config query .k8sServiceHost (->127.0.0.1), notes, not-found=exit1, clap rejects bad --what. fmt+clippy clean default+lean; 860/553 tests.

title: feat(helm): client chokepoint + dispatch foundation
description:
Foundation for the helm domain. Blocks every other helm command issue.

## Deliverables

- `src/helm/mod.rs` — Clap subcommand dispatch, empty initially (commands wire themselves in as they land).
- `src/helm/client.rs` — sole chokepoint for `Command::new("helm")`:
  - `READ_ONLY_VERBS: &[(&str, Option<&str>)]` allowlist (see epic body).
  - `invoke(verb, subverb, args, ...)` returns `Output { stdout, stderr, status }`, mirroring `src/talos/client.rs::Output` and `src/nix/client.rs`.
  - `invoke_ok(...)` returns `Ok(stdout)` on success, `Err` on nonzero exit with trimmed stderr surfaced.
  - Inline `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` with:
    - `no_helm_invocations_outside_client_module` — copy of `src/talos/client.rs::no_talosctl_invocations_outside_client_module` with forbidden tokens `"helm"` (as a quoted literal) and `Command::new(`, exempting comment lines.
    - `rejects_mutating_verb` — assert that `invoke("install", None, ...)`, `invoke("upgrade", None, ...)`, `invoke("uninstall", None, ...)`, `invoke("rollback", None, ...)`, `invoke("repo", Some("add"), ...)`, `invoke("repo", Some("update"), ...)`, `invoke("dependency", Some("update"), ...)` all return errors mentioning the allowlist.
- `src/main.rs` — add `helm` to the domain dispatch.
- `Cargo.toml` — bump version per CLAUDE.md (minor: new domain).
- README.md — add `helm` to the domain list.

## Kubeconfig integration

`helm` reads `KUBECONFIG` / `~/.kube/config` directly, same as kubectl. The chokepoint passes `--kubeconfig`, `--namespace`, `--kube-context` through cleanly when supplied; otherwise it inherits the environment. No sak-side kubeconfig plumbing needed beyond the per-command flags.

## Out of scope

- Any individual command — they're their own child issues.
- Hook integration (`src/hook/claude_code.rs::check_helm`) — folded into the first command issue that introduces shadowing of a `helm` CLI verb, so the hook lands together with a working sak equivalent.
close_reason: Implemented helm domain foundation: src/helm/client.rs chokepoint (READ_ONLY_VERBS (verb,subverb) allowlist, invoke/invoke_ok, Conn for --kubeconfig/--namespace/--kube-context), empty HelmCommand dispatch in mod.rs, wired into main.rs, README + version bump 0.16.0. Grep chokepoint test + rejects_mutating_verb + admits_read_only_verbs all pass; fmt/clippy/test clean on default and --no-default-features. Hook integration deferred to first shadowing command per epic.

title: feat(k8s): contexts command
description:
## Context

Safety-first command. An LLM that does not know which cluster it is pointed at is the most dangerous thing in the entire k8s domain. `sak k8s contexts` lets the LLM see — and the user verify — which kubeconfig context is active before any read against the cluster. Should be the first command an LLM runs in a fresh k8s session.

This is the cheapest possible k8s command: it never contacts any apiserver. It only parses kubeconfig.

## Command

### `sak k8s contexts`

No required args. Lists every context in the merged kubeconfig, marks the current one, and exits.

Args:
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `current<TAB>name<TAB>cluster<TAB>user<TAB>namespace`, one line per context, sorted by name. The `current` column is `*` for the active context, empty otherwise. Goes through `BoundedWriter`.

Exit codes: 0 if any contexts found, 1 if the kubeconfig has none, 2 on parse error.

## Implementation notes

- Use `kube::config::Kubeconfig::read()` (or `read_from()` if `KUBECONFIG` is a colon-list) to merge the kubeconfig files exactly the way `kube::Client::try_default` does. Do **not** call `Client::try_default()` — that opens a real connection. We just want the parsed config.
- The current context comes from the loaded `Kubeconfig.current_context` field.
- Namespace per context is `context.namespace`, defaulting to empty (clients map empty → `default` at request time, but we should report what kubeconfig literally says).
- This command does not import `kube::Api` and does not need any chokepoint extension. The grep test still passes trivially.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/contexts.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Contexts` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual: `cargo run -- k8s contexts` against a kubeconfig with multiple entries shows the right `*` marker on the active one.
- `sak k8s contexts --help` has `long_about` and `after_help` examples per sak conventions.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add GitHub CI workflow for build, test, clippy, and fmt
description:
Add a GitHub Actions CI workflow that runs on push and PR to main.

## Checks

- `cargo fmt --check` — formatting
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` — lint (must be warning-free)
- `cargo test` — all tests pass
- `cargo build --release` — release build succeeds

## Notes

- Use a single workflow file: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- Target stable Rust toolchain
- Cache cargo registry and build artifacts for speed
- Run fmt and clippy as separate jobs so failures are clearly attributable
- Consider matrix for multiple OS (Linux at minimum, macOS nice-to-have)
- git2 crate requires libgit2 system deps — ensure CI installs them or uses the bundled feature
close_reason: GitHub Actions CI workflow added in .github/workflows/ci.yml with fmt, clippy, test, release build, and nix-build matrix jobs (Linux + macOS). Nix builds push to patsoffice Cachix cache so users can pull pre-built binaries.

title: Add config convert command
description:
Add `sak config convert --to <format> [files...]` to translate between TOML, YAML, plist, and JSON. Source files are not touched — output goes to stdout — so this stays within the read-only contract.

## Motivation

Agents frequently need to feed a config to a tool that wants a different format than the source (e.g., reading a binary plist as JSON to inspect it, or converting a YAML manifest to TOML for a Rust tool). Doing this externally is annoying because each pair has different tooling. Centralizing it lets LLMs say "give me this as YAML" without leaving sak.

## Scope

- Source formats: TOML, YAML, plist (XML or binary), JSON
- Target formats: TOML, YAML, plist (XML), JSON
- `--from` flag for explicit source format (required for stdin); auto-detect by extension otherwise
- `--to` flag is required and accepts toml|yaml|plist|json
- `--pretty` flag for JSON/YAML pretty-printing (default depends on target)

## Implementation notes

- All formats round-trip through `serde_json::Value`
- Reuse `read_config_inputs` for input
- For JSON output: `serde_json::to_string[_pretty]`
- For TOML output: `toml::to_string[_pretty]`
- For YAML output: `serde_yaml::to_string`
- For plist output: `plist::to_writer_xml` (binary plist output is probably out of scope — XML is the universal target)
- Document the lossy edges in `--long_about`: TOML datetimes round-trip as strings, plist Data fields lose their type, etc.

## Open questions

- Where does this command live? It's logically a `config` operation but JSON is also a target. Probably keep it under `config` since the matrix of formats is wider there; the `json` domain stays focused on json-specific operations.
close_reason: Added sak config convert <FILES...> --to FORMAT [--from FORMAT] [--pretty|--compact]. Targets/sources: toml, yaml, plist, json (XML plist only on output; binary and XML supported on input). All formats round-trip through serde_json::Value so cross-format diffs/convert come for free, including JSON which is now a first-class Format::Json variant — .json files are auto-detected by every config command. Multi-file YAML output gets --- separators. Tests cover each pair plus the TOML top-level-array error path and stdin --from requirement.

title: feat(k8s): schema command
description:
## Context

Fourth and last issue adding the `k8s` domain. Adds a single command, `sak k8s schema <kind>`, that fetches the OpenAPI v3 schema for a kind from the connected cluster and prints it as JSON.

This is the smallest and most independent of the four issues — it does **not** use the dynamic resource pipeline (\`Api<DynamicObject>\` / \`discovery.rs\`) at all. It uses the \`request_text\` chokepoint helper added in the foundation issue. Cleanly isolated, good \"spare cycles\" issue.

**Depends on sak-llm-k7e (foundation).** Can be developed in parallel with sak-llm-ovb and sak-llm-m78.

## Command

### \`sak k8s schema <kind>\`

Args:
- \`<kind>\` — e.g. \`Deployment\`, \`Pod\`, \`Service\`, or fully qualified \`apps/v1/Deployment\`
- \`--limit\`
- (optional) \`--group <g>\` and \`--version <v>\` if the user needs to disambiguate kinds that exist in multiple groups (e.g. \`Ingress\` in \`networking.k8s.io\` vs the deprecated \`extensions\`)

Output: pretty JSON of the schema for that kind, through \`BoundedWriter\`.

## Implementation

The Kubernetes OpenAPI v3 endpoint is a **two-hop** fetch:

1. \`GET /openapi/v3\` — returns an index of paths, e.g. \`{ \"paths\": { \"apis/apps/v1\": { \"serverRelativeURL\": \"/openapi/v3/apis/apps/v1?hash=...\" }, ... } }\`
2. Resolve the user's kind to a \`(group, version)\` pair (use the same shortname/builtin map from \`discovery.rs\`, or accept fully qualified input).
3. \`GET /openapi/v3/apis/<group>/<version>\` (using the \`serverRelativeURL\` from step 1 to pick up the hash query string).
4. Parse the returned OpenAPI document, find the schema for the requested kind under \`components.schemas\`, and print it.

Both fetches go through \`client::request_text\` from the foundation chokepoint.

**No fallback to \`/openapi/v2\`.** Older clusters that only expose v2 get a clear error. Keeping the implementation small.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/schema.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\` — add \`Schema\` variant, wire dispatch.
- (Possibly) \`src/k8s/discovery.rs\` — if the kind→(group, version) mapping needs to be reused, expose it as a public helper. The shortname map already has this info; just add an accessor.

## Acceptance criteria

- \`cargo test --features k8s\` passes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes (\`schema.rs\` must use \`client::request_text\`, not \`kube::Api\` directly).
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual integration tests against a real cluster:
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s schema Deployment | sak json query .properties.spec.type\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s schema Pod | sak json keys . --depth 2\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s schema NotARealKind\` → clear error
- Patch-level version bump.

## Known limitations

- \`/openapi/v2\` not supported. Old clusters error out cleanly.
- Schemas for CRDs work as long as the CRD's group/version is registered in the OpenAPI index, which it is by default in modern k8s.
close_reason:
Landed sak k8s schema as the fourth and final command in the k8s domain. The k8s domain is now complete (kinds, get, images, env, schema).

Implementation:
- src/k8s/schema.rs: two-hop OpenAPI v3 fetch via the chokepoint. Hop 1 GETs /openapi/v3 to find the serverRelativeURL (with hash query string) for the requested group/version. Hop 2 GETs that URL and looks up the schema in components.schemas by walking each entry's x-kubernetes-group-version-kind annotation — the package-style key (io.k8s.api.apps.v1.Deployment) is *not* used for matching, so CRDs and aliased schemas work the same way as builtins.
- Kind resolution is purely client-side: explicit --group/--version (must be supplied together) > fully qualified 'group/version/Kind' or 'version/Kind' > builtin shortname lookup via discovery::lookup_builtin. No cluster contact for resolution itself, so 'sak k8s schema NotARealKind' fails fast with a clear error and never burns a round trip.
- No /openapi/v2 fallback by design — old apiservers fail with a clean error message rather than carrying duplicate schema-walking code.

Chokepoint extension:
- src/k8s/client.rs gained pub async fn request_text(client, path), wrapping kube::Client::request_text. Required adding 'http = { version = "1", optional = true }' to Cargo.toml gated on the k8s feature — kube does not re-export the http::Request types we need to construct raw GETs. The chokepoint grep test still passes (schema.rs only calls client::request_text, never touches kube::Api).

Tests:
- 10 unit tests in schema.rs cover parse_qualified (3-part, 2-part core, plain, empty-segment rejection), openapi_path_key (core api/<v> vs apis/<g>/<v>), and schema_matches_gvk (positive, kind-case-insensitive, wrong-group rejection, missing annotation, multi-tagged array). All pure — no cluster needed.

Verified end-to-end against KUBECONFIG=../home-ops/kubeconfig:
- k8s schema Deployment -> pretty JSON with full descriptions
- ... | sak json query .properties.kind.type -> 'string' (full pipe round-trip)
- ... | sak json keys . --depth 2 -> shows x-kubernetes-group-version-kind, properties, etc.
- k8s schema Ingress --group networking.k8s.io --version v1 -> disambiguation flags work
- k8s schema apps/v1/Deployment -> fully-qualified form works
- k8s schema NotARealKind -> clear error, exit 2

cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, cargo fmt --check all clean. Cargo.toml bumped 0.5.2 -> 0.5.3 (patch bump for new command within an existing domain).

title: Add toml/yaml domain with query, keys, flatten, validate commands
description:
Add a new `config` domain to sak for reading TOML and YAML files, following the `fs` domain pattern.

## Commands

### `sak config query <expression> [files...]`
Extract values by dot-path from TOML or YAML files.
- Auto-detect format by extension (.toml, .yaml, .yml) or `--format toml|yaml`
- Dot-path syntax: `.server.port`, `.dependencies.serde.version`
- Options: `--raw` (output raw strings without quotes), `--format <toml|yaml>`
- Read from stdin with explicit `--format`

### `sak config keys [path] [files...]`
List keys at a given level or path.
- Default: list top-level keys
- Options: `--depth <n>` (recurse N levels), `--types` (show value types), `--format <toml|yaml>`
- Sorted output for determinism

### `sak config flatten [files...]`
Flatten nested config into `path<TAB>value` lines.
- Same output format as `json flatten` for consistency
- Options: `--separator <char>` (default `.`), `--max-depth <n>`, `--format <toml|yaml>`
- Sorted by path for determinism

### `sak config validate [files...]`
Check whether files contain valid TOML/YAML.
- Auto-detect format by extension
- Exit 0 if valid, exit 1 if invalid
- Report filename, line, column, error message
- Options: `--quiet`, `--format <toml|yaml>`

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `ConfigCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/config/`
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Parse to a common `serde_json::Value` or custom enum internally so command logic is format-agnostic
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Read from stdin when no files given (requires explicit `--format`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results/invalid, 2 = error

## Dependencies

- `toml` crate
- `serde_yaml` or `yaml-rust2` crate
- `serde_json` (shared with json domain for internal representation)
close_reason: Added config domain with query/keys/flatten/validate commands. Supports TOML, YAML, and plist (XML and binary) — plist included beyond original scope because its rich type system maps cleanly to the same internal serde_json::Value representation. INI deliberately excluded as a poor fit for the generic command shape. Shared value-walking utilities extracted to src/value.rs so json and config domains share traversal/path-resolution code.

title: feat(sqlite): dump command
description:
## Context

Sample rows from a table. Friendlier-flag wrapper around \`sak sqlite query\`'s \`SELECT * FROM <table> [ORDER BY ...] LIMIT N\` that handles table-name quoting and gives the LLM ergonomic flags. The "show me what is in this table" command.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue, and shares the type-mapping helper from the \`query\` issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite dump <db> <table>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`<table>\` — table or view name
- \`--limit <n>\` — row cap (default 10 — small enough to be safe by default)
- \`--order-by <col>\` — column to order by; sak quotes it
- \`--desc\` — order descending instead of ascending (no-op without \`--order-by\`)
- \`--format <json|tsv>\` — same as \`sak sqlite query\` (default \`json\`)

Effective query (built internally — never exposed to the user as raw SQL):

\`\`\`sql
SELECT * FROM "<table>" [ORDER BY "<col>" [DESC]] LIMIT <n>
\`\`\`

## Validation

- Quote \`<table>\` and \`<col>\` with double-quote identifier escaping (double internal quotes).
- Reject either argument containing a NUL byte.
- \`--limit\` must be a positive integer (clap should enforce).

## Output / type mapping

Reuse the type-mapping helper from the \`sak sqlite query\` command (extracted to a small \`mod\` shared by both files — \`src/sqlite/format.rs\` is fine, or pub-in-crate from \`query.rs\`). Same JSON-lines / TSV behavior, same NULL/INTEGER/REAL/TEXT/BLOB rules, same BoundedWriter output.

## Exit codes

- 0 if at least one row was emitted
- 1 if the table exists but has zero rows (or fewer than the limit AND the limit was respected — same condition: zero rows emitted means exit 1)
- 2 on error (missing table, malformed column, etc.)

## Implementation notes

- Open via \`client::open_readonly\` and run via the same \`query_rows_dyn\` helper from the foundation / query issue.
- This file should be small — most of the work is shared with \`query.rs\`.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/dump.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Dump\` variant and dispatch.
- \`src/sqlite/query.rs\` (or \`src/sqlite/format.rs\`) — make the row → JSON / TSV helpers \`pub(crate)\` so \`dump.rs\` can call them.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit tests over a temp database covering: ascending order, descending order, default limit, explicit limit, missing column → exit 2, table with zero rows → exit 1.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite dump some.db users --limit 5 --order-by id --desc\`.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented sak sqlite dump: SELECT * with quoted table/order-by, --limit/--desc/--format json|tsv reusing query.rs row helpers. Pre-validates --order-by against PRAGMA table_info to defeat sqlite's DQ-as-string-literal quirk.

title: fix(json): accept '-' as stdin alias (currently treated as a filename)
close_reason: json commands now treat a file argument of exactly '-' as stdin (cat/jq/grep convention). Added is_stdin() + read_source() chokepoint helpers in src/json/mod.rs; routed read_json_inputs, read_json_inputs_lines (NDJSON), diff::load, and validate through them. query/select/keys/flatten/paths/grep/schema/exists/length/type/validate/diff all honor '-'. Note: keys/length/type take an optional positional path first, so 'sak json keys -' parses '-' as the path (pre-existing ambiguity) — pass an explicit path ('sak json keys . -') to use '-' as stdin. Updated --help field docs + long_about across the domain and added a piped-stdin example to query. config/csv/cert deferred (separate readers) — fs grep already supports '-'. Patch bump 0.17.3 -> 0.17.4.

title: feat(json): table command — array iteration with multi-field projection to TSV
description:
The classic `jq -r '.[] | [.id, .priority, .title] | @tsv'` shape — iterate an array of objects at a path, project N named fields per element, emit one TSV row per element. Today this needs a `python3 -c "import json,sys; ..."` fallback because `sak json query` is single-value-at-a-path and `sak json select` operates on a single object, not on each element of an array.

Hit this three times in one session inspecting `br list --json` output — recurring papercut, not an edge case.

## Command

### `sak json table <array-path> [files...]`

- `<array-path>` is a dot-path or JSON Pointer into the input, pointing at a JSON array of objects. Default if omitted: `.` (root must be an array).
- `-f, --fields <name,name,...>` — required. Comma-separated field names (or dot-paths / JSON Pointers for nested values) to project from each element.
- `--header` — emit a header row (field names tab-separated). Default off to match `jq @tsv` semantics; LLMs can opt in.
- `--missing <token>` — substitute for missing/null fields. Default `-` matching the cert/sysctl convention elsewhere in sak.
- `--lines` — input is NDJSON (one JSON value per line), not a wrapped array. Matches the existing `--lines` flag on `json query/keys/flatten/validate`.
- `--limit` — output truncation via BoundedWriter.

## Example

```
$ br list --json | sak json table .issues --fields id,priority,title
sak-llm-myu     2       feat(nix): new domain for read-only Nix store / flake / profile inspection
sak-llm-wyn     2       feat(nix): client chokepoint + dispatch foundation
sak-llm-out     3       feat(nix): flake-show command
...
```

## Implementation notes

- Reuse `value::resolve_path` from `src/value.rs` for both the array path and per-element field paths.
- Reuse `BoundedWriter` from `src/output.rs`.
- Sanitize tab/newline characters in projected string values — same defense `sak sqlite info` uses for multi-line PRAGMA values — to keep the `field<TAB>field<TAB>...` contract.
- Pure function `project(arr: &Value, fields: &[Path]) -> Vec<Row>` unit-testable on hand-built JSON fixtures.
- Non-object array elements (scalars) — pin behavior in the tests. Suggested: render the scalar in the first field, `--missing` token in the rest. Hard error if any field path can't resolve in *any* element (caller meant a path that doesn't exist).

## Out of scope (file separately)

- Predicate filtering (`--where '.title ~ "feat(nix)"'`) — sibling issue. Filtering is a slippery slope toward a query language; users can pipe this TSV output through `sak fs grep` until that follow-up lands.
- Multi-array unioning (jq's `+`).
- Numeric formatting / column widths — TSV is for downstream tools, not human reading.

title: feat(talos): logs command for per-service node logs
description:
After `talos services` shows a failing service, the next question is "what does it say in the logs?" Today: manual `talosctl -n <node> logs <service>` per node.

## Command

### `sak talos logs <service>`

Reads service logs from one or more nodes.

Flags:
- `--nodes <ip1,ip2>` — node selection (default: context's full node set)
- `--tail <N>` — last N lines per node (default 100)
- `--since <duration>` — last N seconds/minutes/hours (reuse `prom::duration::parse_duration`)
- `--grep <regex>` — filter lines client-side (mirror `k8s logs --grep`)
- `--all-nodes` — prefix each line with `[<node>]` like `k8s logs --all-containers`
- `--limit` — output truncation via BoundedWriter

## Implementation notes

- Shell out via `talosctl logs <service>`. Add `logs` to `READ_ONLY_VERBS` if not already present.
- Streaming line-by-line (don't buffer full output) so `--limit` truncates cleanly.
- Multi-node fan-out: prefix each line with `[<node>]` and interleave; deterministic line order doesn't matter much since each line is timestamped at the source.
- For service names, accept the same set as `talos services` (kubelet, etcd, machined, apid, trustd, ...). Don't pre-validate — pass through to talosctl so future services don't require a sak release.
- Per-node errors: surface as a marker line (`[<node>] error: <msg>`) rather than aborting the whole command.

## README hook

Add `talosctl logs` to the talos hook regex in README.md. Call out the hook update in the commit message.

title: feat(k8s): foundation — cargo feature, tokio bridge, kube client chokepoint
description:
## Context

First of four issues adding a `k8s` domain to sak. This issue lands the scaffolding only — no user-facing commands. The point is to absorb the high-risk pieces (dep footprint, build size, async bridge) on their own so they can be reviewed and shaken out before any command code piles on top.

The k8s domain talks read-only to a live Kubernetes cluster via kubeconfig, using the `kube` crate (no shelling out — matches the git domain's `git2` precedent). To preserve sak's small-binary ethos for users who don't need k8s, the **entire domain is gated behind a cargo feature `k8s` that is off by default**. Default builds are unaffected in size and compile time.

## Scope

### Cargo.toml

- Add optional deps: `kube`, `k8s-openapi`, `tokio`
- Add `[features] k8s = ["dep:kube", "dep:k8s-openapi", "dep:tokio"]` (default = [])
- `kube`: `default-features = false`, features `["client", "rustls-tls"]` only. **Do not** enable `runtime`, `derive`, `ws`, `oauth`, `oidc`, `gzip`, `admission`, `jsonpatch` — none are needed for get/list and they significantly inflate build time.
- `k8s-openapi`: pin a single concrete API-version feature (e.g. `v1_31`). **Never** use `latest` — it's a moving target and breaks reproducible builds.
- `tokio`: features `["rt", "macros"]` only. Current-thread runtime is sufficient; do not enable `rt-multi-thread`.
- Verify the latest stable `kube` and matching `k8s-openapi` versions on docs.rs at implementation time before pinning.
- Bump sak's version per CLAUDE.md (minor bump for new domain).

### src/main.rs

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "k8s")]
mod k8s;

#[derive(Subcommand)]
enum Command {
    // ... existing variants ...
    #[cfg(feature = "k8s")]
    #[command(subcommand)]
    K8s(k8s::K8sCommand),
}
```

Add the matching gated dispatch arm in `main()`.

### src/k8s/mod.rs

- `K8sCommand` enum (empty for now — will be populated by issues #2/#3/#4)
- `pub fn run(cmd: &K8sCommand) -> Result<ExitCode>` that builds a current-thread tokio runtime locally and `block_on`s the async dispatch:

```rust
pub fn run(cmd: &K8sCommand) -> Result<ExitCode> {
    let rt = tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()
        .enable_all()
        .build()?;
    rt.block_on(async { dispatch(cmd).await })
}
```

The rest of sak stays sync.

### src/k8s/client.rs — sole `kube::Api` chokepoint

This module is the **only** file in `src/k8s/` permitted to import `kube::Api` or any of its mutation methods. It exposes a small async surface:

- `pub async fn build_client() -> Result<kube::Client>` — wraps `Client::try_default()`
- `pub async fn list_dyn(client, ar, namespace, list_params) -> Result<ObjectList<DynamicObject>>`
- `pub async fn get_dyn(client, ar, namespace, name) -> Result<DynamicObject>`
- `pub async fn request_text(client, path) -> Result<String>` — for `/openapi/v3` fetches

Read-only enforcement is **convention + a grep test** (kube has no read-only client variant). Add a unit test in `client.rs`:

```rust
#[test]
fn no_mutation_methods_outside_client_module() {
    // Walk src/k8s/*.rs excluding client.rs; assert none contain
    // \".create(\", \".delete(\", \".delete_collection(\", \".patch(\",
    // \".replace(\", \".patch_scale(\", or \"DeleteParams\"/\"PatchParams\".
}
```

### src/k8s/discovery.rs — shortname + GVK resolver

- Hardcoded map of common builtins: `po`/`pod`/`pods` → core/v1/Pod, `deploy`/`deployment`/`deployments` → apps/v1/Deployment, `svc`/`service`/`services` → core/v1/Service, etc. (~30 entries covering the common kinds).
- `pub async fn resolve(client, kind: &str) -> Result<ApiResource>`:
  1. Fast path: lookup in shortname map → call `kube::discovery::oneshot::pinned_kind(&client, &gvk)` (single-GVK, cheap).
  2. Slow path on miss: fall back to full `Discovery::new(client).run().await` and search by name. Document that this is multi-second on big clusters.
- Unit tests on the shortname map (no cluster needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo build` succeeds and is byte-identical to before this change (default features, k8s off).
- `cargo build --features k8s` succeeds. Record the binary-size delta and cold-build-time delta in the commit message — this is the data point that justifies (or doesn't) the rest of the work.
- `cargo test` passes (default features).
- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (adds the chokepoint grep test + discovery shortname tests).
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` passes with no warnings.
- `cargo fmt` clean.
- No user-facing command exists yet — `sak k8s --help` (with the feature on) should show an empty subcommand list, that's expected.

## Files

**Modify:**
- \`Cargo.toml\`
- \`src/main.rs\`

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/client.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/discovery.rs\`

## Blocks

This issue blocks issues #2 (kinds + get), #3 (images + env), and #4 (schema). Land this first.

## Known trade-offs

- **Binary size**: \`--features k8s\` builds will be ~15–25 MB larger and ~60–120s slower to cold-build than the default. This is the price of \`k8s-openapi\`'s generated code. Default builds are unaffected.
- **Read-only enforcement is convention + grep test**, not type-level. \`kube::Api\` exposes mutation methods on the same type used for reads. The chokepoint + grep test is the cheapest credible defense.
close_reason: Foundation landed: k8s cargo feature (off by default), tokio current-thread bridge, kube::Api chokepoint in src/k8s/client.rs (with grep test enforcing no mutation methods or Api imports outside that file), and src/k8s/discovery.rs with a 32-entry builtin shortname table + fast/slow path resolver. Default build unchanged; --features k8s adds 7 unit tests (all green) and a #[ignore]d live_smoke test verified end-to-end against a real cluster (build_client + fast-path resolve(pod) + list_dyn 241 pods + slow-path resolve(Lease) -> coordination.k8s.io/v1). cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, and cargo fmt all clean. Real binary-size delta deferred until sak-llm-ovb wires the first command — LTO strips the dead kube tree in foundation alone.

title: fix(k8s): get emits NDJSON but --path '.items[...]' fails silently
description:
sak k8s get <kind> emits NDJSON (one resource object per line), NOT a kubectl-style List wrapper {"items":[...]}. Users with kubectl/jq muscle memory naturally write --path ".items[0].metadata.name" (or feed the output to `sak json`), which never matches.

Two distinct papercuts:

1. SILENT FAILURE: `sak k8s get pods -n NS --path ".items[0].metadata.name"` exits 1 with NO output. There is no hint that .items does not exist or that the stream is NDJSON. The correct form is per-object: `--path ".metadata.name"` (applied to each line). A no-match path against every record should emit a diagnostic (e.g. "path matched 0 of N records; note: `get` output is NDJSON, one object per line — paths are relative to each object, not a List") instead of a bare exit 1.

2. DISCOVERABILITY: the NDJSON shape and the per-object --path semantics are not obvious. Feeding `sak k8s get` straight into `sak json query` fails with "trailing characters at line 2" because json expects a single document. CLAUDE.md / --help should state the output is NDJSON and recommend per-line consumption.

Repro:
  sak k8s get pods -n self-hosted --path ".items[0].metadata.name"   # exit 1, empty
  sak k8s get pods -n self-hosted --path ".metadata.name"            # works, one name per line

Suggested fixes (any subset):
- Emit a helpful stderr diagnostic when a --path matches zero records.
- Document NDJSON output + per-object path semantics in --help and CLAUDE.md.
- Optionally add a flag/format (e.g. --as-list or `sak json` --ndjson/--slurp) to consume the stream.
