title: feat(k8s): endpoints command
description:
## Context

`Service has no endpoints` is one of the top three k8s footguns. The current debugging path (`kubectl describe svc`, then `kubectl get endpoints`, then `kubectl get pods -l <selector>`) is awful. A focused command beats all of them.

## Command

### `sak k8s endpoints <service>`

Args:
- `<service>` — service name
- `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `address<TAB>port<TAB>node<TAB>ready<TAB>target` per backing endpoint, sorted by `(address, port)`.

- `address` — IP from `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].addresses[0]`
- `port`    — combined as `name=port/protocol` from `EndpointSlice.ports[*]`; one row per `(endpoint × port)` combo
- `node`    — `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].nodeName` (or `-`)
- `ready`   — `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].conditions.ready` (`true`/`false`/`unknown`)
- `target`  — `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].targetRef.kind/name` (or `-`)

## Implementation notes

- Modern clusters (1.21+) use `discovery.k8s.io/v1/EndpointSlice`; the legacy `core/v1/Endpoints` is deprecated. Use EndpointSlice only.
- Discovery: hardcode `discovery.k8s.io/v1/EndpointSlice` (no need to call `discovery::resolve`) — saves a round trip and EndpointSlice is stable enough to inline.
- Filter EndpointSlices by label `kubernetes.io/service-name=<service>`. List via `client::list_dyn` with `ListParams::default().labels(...)`.
- A service can have multiple EndpointSlices (large services are sharded). Walk all of them and emit one row per `(endpoint, port)` combination.
- If zero EndpointSlices come back, exit 1 (the sak "no results" code) — a service with no endpoints *is* the diagnostic we are reporting.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/endpoints.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Endpoints` variant.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (unit-test the EndpointSlice → row flattening on a fixture with two endpoints × two ports).
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s endpoints kubernetes -n default` — should always have endpoints on every cluster.
  - `cargo run -- k8s endpoints <some-service> -n <ns>`
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add INI file support to config domain
description:
Extend the `config` domain to handle INI files alongside the existing TOML/YAML/plist formats. INI was deliberately excluded from the initial implementation (sak-llm-iw0) because its flat, untyped structure is a poor fit for the generic command shape, but it remains a real-world format LLMs encounter and is worth supporting at a reduced fidelity.

## Why this is lower priority

Unlike TOML/YAML/plist, INI doesn't fit cleanly into the shared `serde_json::Value` model:

- **No standard spec** — dialects vary across `rust-ini`, `configparser`, and others (comment characters, multiline values, key=value vs key:value, case sensitivity, etc.)
- **Shallow by design** — structure is always exactly two levels: `section.key = value`. Depth-related flags (`keys --depth`, `flatten --max-depth`) become trivial or meaningless.
- **All values are untyped strings** — there's no distinction between `port = 8080` (number) and `name = alice` (string). The `--types` flag on `keys` returns `string` for everything; `schema` would be uniformly `string`.

These mismatches mean INI gets less value out of each command than the typed formats.

## What still works

Even with the caveats, the four core commands have meaningful INI semantics:

- `query .section.key file.ini` — returns the string value (always)
- `keys file.ini` — top-level section names; `keys .section file.ini` — keys within a section
- `flatten file.ini` — `section.key<TAB>value` lines, sorted
- `validate file.ini` — parse-only check; useful because INI files break in subtle ways
- `exists .section.key file.ini` — section/key existence check

## Implementation notes

- Add a Rust INI crate dependency. `rust-ini` (`ini = "1"`) is the most popular and supports the common dialects. Verify it can produce a structure that converts cleanly into `serde_json::Value` (a two-level object: section → key → string-value).
- Add `Format::Ini` to the `Format` enum in `src/config/mod.rs`
- Extend `detect_format()` to recognize `.ini`, `.cfg`, and `.conf` (the latter two are debatable — they're heavily overloaded; consider `.ini` only at first)
- Extend `parse_one()` with an INI branch that parses to `Ini` and converts to `Value::Object<section, Object<key, String>>`
- Update `--long_about` text on each command to call out the INI-specific limitations (everything-is-a-string, depth flags become trivial)
- Add inline tests covering: top-level keys (= section names), section keys, flatten output, validate on a malformed file
- Decide on dialect handling: probably accept the most permissive defaults and document them; do not expose dialect tuning flags initially

## Open questions

- **Comment styles**: `;` and `#` are both common; pick the parser default and document it
- **Sectionless keys**: some INI dialects allow keys before any `[section]` header. Map them to a synthetic top-level key (e.g., `""` or `_global`)? Reject them? The parser's default behavior is the safe call.
- **Duplicate keys**: INI allows them in some dialects. Last wins, first wins, or error? Probably last wins to match merge semantics elsewhere.
- **`.cfg`/`.conf` extensions**: too overloaded to safely auto-detect. Start with `.ini` only and require `--format ini` for the others.

## Background

Was discussed during scoping for sak-llm-iw0 and intentionally deferred. See that issue's close reason for the original rationale.

title: Add csv domain with query, headers, stats, validate commands
description:
Add a new `csv` domain to sak following the same pattern as the `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak csv query <columns> [files...]`
Select and filter CSV rows/columns.
- Column selection by name or index: `--columns name,age` or `--columns 1,3`
- Options: `--filter <column>=<value>` (exact match), `--filter-regex <column>=<pattern>`, `--delimiter <char>` (default `,`), `--no-header` (treat first row as data), `--limit`
- Output as CSV with header row
- Read from stdin when no files given

### `sak csv headers [files...]`
List column names and their indices.
- Output: `index<TAB>name` for each column
- Options: `--delimiter <char>`, `--types` (infer column types by sampling rows: string, integer, float, bool, empty)

### `sak csv stats [files...]`
Show summary statistics for CSV columns.
- Row count, column count, null/empty counts per column
- For numeric columns: min, max, mean
- Options: `--columns <names>` (limit to specific columns), `--delimiter <char>`, `--sample <n>` (sample N rows for large files)

### `sak csv validate [files...]`
Check CSV structure and report errors.
- Detect: inconsistent column counts, unclosed quotes, encoding issues
- Exit 0 if valid, exit 1 if errors found
- Report line number and error description
- Options: `--delimiter <char>`, `--strict` (reject rows with mismatched column counts), `--quiet`

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `CsvCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/csv/`
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Use `csv` crate for parsing
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Read from stdin when no files given (like `fs cut`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results/invalid, 2 = error
- Errors to stderr prefixed with `sak: error:`

## Dependencies

- `csv` crate
close_reason: Implemented as csv domain (always-on; pure-Rust csv crate). Commands: headers (column index/name + optional --types inference), query (column projection + --filter / --filter-regex, --no-header, RFC-4180 CSV output), stats (rows/columns + per-column nonempty/empty counts + min/max/mean for numeric columns, with --sample N), validate (parse-error reporting with --strict for column-count mismatch). Inline tests pin behavior; default + lean (--no-default-features) builds both clean. CLAUDE.md and README updated; no hook redirect added since CSV has no canonical read-only CLI to shadow.

title: Add fs enhancements: tree, stat, head, tail, wc commands
description:
Add new commands to the existing `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak fs tree [path]`
Display directory structure as an indented tree.
- Options: `--max-depth <n>`, `--hidden` (include dotfiles), `--dirs-only`, `--limit`
- Skip default ignored directories (.git, target, node_modules, etc.)
- Show file counts at the bottom: "N directories, M files"
- Deterministic: sorted alphabetically at each level

### `sak fs stat <path...>`
Show file/directory metadata.
- Output: size, permissions, modified time, type (file/dir/symlink)
- For directories: include entry count
- Options: `--format <human|json>` (human-readable sizes vs raw bytes)
- Tab-separated fields for parseability

### `sak fs head <file> [n]`
Show first N lines of a file (default 10).
- Essentially `sak fs read` with `--lines 1-N` but more ergonomic
- Options: `--bytes <n>` (first N bytes instead of lines), `--no-line-numbers`

### `sak fs tail <file> [n]`
Show last N lines of a file (default 10).
- Options: `--bytes <n>` (last N bytes), `--no-line-numbers`

### `sak fs wc [files...]`
Count lines, words, bytes in files.
- Output: `lines<TAB>words<TAB>bytes<TAB>filename`
- With multiple files: show totals row
- Options: `--lines` / `--words` / `--bytes` (show only selected counts)
- Read from stdin when no files given

## Implementation Notes

- Add new command files under `src/fs/`
- Update `src/fs/mod.rs` to add new variants to `FsCommand` enum
- Reuse existing `output.rs` utilities (BoundedWriter, format_line_number, relative_path)
- Reuse directory-skipping logic from glob/grep
- head/tail could potentially be thin wrappers around read logic
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Lower priority (3) since core fs commands already exist
close_reason: Added fs tree/stat/head/tail/wc commands. tree: indented tree with ├──/└── connectors, --max-depth/--dirs-only/--hidden, summary footer; reuses should_skip pruning. stat: per-path type/perms/size/mtime(ISO UTC)/entries as TSV rows or --format json (raw bytes); symlink_metadata so symlinks report as themselves; civil_from_days for chrono-free ISO. head/tail: thin wrappers delegating the line path to read (1-N / -N), plus --bytes raw byte modes and --no-line-numbers. wc: lines/words/bytes TSV with total row, --lines/--words/--bytes column selection, stdin when no files. Hook extended: head/tail now suggest dedicated commands; new check_tree/check_stat/check_wc with block/allow tests. Docs (main.rs quick-start, README discovery + hook list, CLAUDE.md dogfood list) updated. Version 0.17.2->0.17.3. fmt/clippy/test clean on default and --no-default-features.

title: feat(sqlite): add sqlite domain foundation behind opt-in cargo feature
description:
## Context

SAK currently has data-peek domains for `json` and `config` (TOML/YAML/plist) but no way to look inside SQLite databases. SQLite `.db` files show up constantly in app data, browser history, analytics dumps, and CI fixtures, and LLMs often need to peek into one read-only.

SQLite is a strong fit for SAK because read-only enforcement is OS-enforced via `SQLITE_OPEN_READONLY` plus `PRAGMA query_only=ON` — *cleaner* than the convention-plus-grep approach used by k8s. `rusqlite` maps cleanly to `serde_json::Value` so output flows through `BoundedWriter` the same way every other domain does. The dep can be cargo-feature-gated (`sqlite`) the same way `k8s` is.

This issue is the **foundation** for the sqlite domain. Six command issues depend on it (tables, schema, query, count, dump, info) and should be picked up only after this lands.

## Cargo feature

Add a new optional feature `sqlite` in `Cargo.toml`. **Recommendation: opt-in, not in `default = [...]`** until size and build-time impact are measured. The k8s feature (which is on by default) roughly doubles the release binary; we should not stack a second large feature into the default build without measuring first.

Use `rusqlite` with the `bundled` feature (compiles libsqlite3 from source, no system dep). Mirror the gating shape from `Cargo.toml:24-25` and `Cargo.toml:43-49`:

\`\`\`toml
[features]
default = [\"k8s\"]
k8s = [\"dep:kube\", \"dep:k8s-openapi\", \"dep:tokio\", \"dep:http\"]
sqlite = [\"dep:rusqlite\"]

[dependencies]
rusqlite = { version = \"0.32\", default-features = false, features = [\"bundled\"], optional = true }
\`\`\`

(Pin a specific 0.x — don't track latest.)

## Domain dispatch

Mirror the shape of `src/k8s/mod.rs`:

- `pub mod client;`
- `pub enum SqliteCommand` (empty for now — commands are added by dependent issues)
- `pub fn run(cmd: &SqliteCommand) -> Result<ExitCode>` — sync, no tokio runtime needed (sqlite is sync)

Wire it into `src/main.rs` next to the existing domain dispatches, gated on `#[cfg(feature = \"sqlite\")]`.

## Read-only enforcement (chokepoint)

`src/sqlite/client.rs` is the chokepoint that owns every `rusqlite::Connection`. All connection opening must go through:

\`\`\`rust
pub fn open_readonly(path: &Path) -> Result<Connection>
\`\`\`

which calls:

\`\`\`rust
Connection::open_with_flags(
    path,
    OpenFlags::SQLITE_OPEN_READ_ONLY | OpenFlags::SQLITE_OPEN_URI,
)
\`\`\`

and then immediately runs \`PRAGMA query_only = ON;\` as defense in depth.

Add a small \`query_rows\` helper in \`client.rs\` that returns rows as \`Vec<Vec<rusqlite::types::Value>>\` (or similar) so other modules under \`src/sqlite/\` never need to touch \`Connection\` directly.

## Chokepoint grep test

Mirror the k8s pattern from \`src/k8s/client.rs\`. Add a unit test in \`src/sqlite/client.rs\` that walks every other file under \`src/sqlite/\` and asserts none of them mention:

- \`rusqlite::Connection\`
- \`.execute(\` or \`.execute_batch(\` (mutation methods)
- \`Connection::open\` in any form

Only \`client.rs\` may mention these.

## Read-only behavior test

Add a unit test that:

1. Creates a temp sqlite file with a table and a row using a *separate* writable connection.
2. Opens it through \`open_readonly\`.
3. Asserts that an attempted \`INSERT\` / \`UPDATE\` / \`DELETE\` / \`CREATE TABLE\` through that connection returns an error.

(The mutation attempt has to go through \`client.rs\` itself, since other files cannot touch \`Connection\` per the grep test.)

## Documentation

Update \`CLAUDE.md\`:

- **Architecture** section: mention the \`sqlite\` domain alongside json/config and the read-only chokepoint enforcement story. Note that sqlite's enforcement is stronger than k8s's (OS flag + PRAGMA, not just convention).
- **File Layout**: add \`src/sqlite/\` to the example layout.
- **Build & Test**: add \`cargo build --features sqlite\` to the example commands.
- **Gotchas**: note the \`bundled\` rusqlite feature compiles libsqlite3 from source (longer first build, no system dep needed).

Update \`README.md\` if it lists domains.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\`
- \`src/sqlite/client.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`Cargo.toml\` — add \`sqlite\` feature and \`rusqlite\` optional dep
- \`src/main.rs\` — wire up the new domain (gated on feature)
- \`CLAUDE.md\` — Architecture, File Layout, Build & Test, Gotchas
- \`README.md\` — if it lists domains

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test\` (default features) passes.
- \`cargo test --no-default-features\` passes.
- \`cargo test --no-default-features --features sqlite\` passes.
- \`cargo test --all-features\` passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- \`cargo fmt\` clean.
- Read-only behavior test passes.
- Chokepoint grep test passes.
- \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite --help\` dispatches (lists no commands yet — that is fine, dependent issues add them).
- **Minor** version bump in \`Cargo.toml\` (new domain).

title: feat(k8s): images and env commands
description:
## Context

Third of four issues adding the `k8s` domain. Adds two targeted extractors for the most common LLM-useful queries against pod-bearing resources: \"what images are running\" and \"what env vars / secret refs does this thing have.\"

Both commands walk container specs, so they share a single walker module (\`src/k8s/containers.rs\`) which is **pure** and unit-testable on \`serde_json::Value\` fixtures with no cluster needed. This is the issue with the most testable logic in the whole k8s domain.

**Depends on sak-llm-k7e (foundation).** Can be developed in parallel with sak-llm-ovb (kinds + get).

## Shared walker — \`src/k8s/containers.rs\`

A single function used by both commands:

\`\`\`rust
pub struct ContainerView<'a> {
    pub namespace: Option<&'a str>,
    pub name: &'a str,            // resource name
    pub container: &'a str,       // container name
    pub image: &'a str,
    pub env: &'a [serde_json::Value],
}

pub fn walk_containers(value: &serde_json::Value) -> impl Iterator<Item = ContainerView<'_>>;
\`\`\`

The walker handles two shapes:
- Pod: \`spec.containers[*]\`
- Pod-template owners (Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet): \`spec.template.spec.containers[*]\`
- CronJob has an extra hop: \`spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers[*]\`

Walker only — no networking. **Unit tests on hand-built JSON fixtures live alongside the walker** and run on every \`cargo test --features k8s\` invocation.

## Commands

### \`sak k8s images [kind]\`

Walks container specs across resources of \`kind\` (default \`pods\`) and emits one line per container.

Args:
- \`[kind]\` — defaults to \`pods\`; resolved via \`discovery.rs\`
- \`-n, --namespace\` / \`-A, --all-namespaces\`
- \`-l, --selector\`
- \`--limit\`

Output:
- Format: \`namespace/name<TAB>container<TAB>image\`, one line per container, sorted by \`(namespace, name, container)\`.
- Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

### \`sak k8s env <kind> <name>\`

Walks env vars on a single named resource. Restricted to kinds that have a pod template:

Allowed kinds: Pod, Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet. Any other kind → clear error message at the front of the command (no silent no-op).

Args:
- \`<kind>\`, \`<name>\`
- \`-n, --namespace\`
- \`--limit\`

Output: \`container<TAB>name<TAB>value-or-ref\` per env var, where \`value-or-ref\` is:
- For literal values: the string value
- For \`configMapKeyRef\`: \`cm:<configmap-name>/<key>\`
- For \`secretKeyRef\`: \`secret:<secret-name>/<key>\`
- For \`fieldRef\`: \`field:<fieldPath>\`
- For \`resourceFieldRef\`: \`resource:<resource>\`

**Read-only**: \`env\` does **not** dereference secret/configmap refs to fetch their actual values — it only reports the references. This keeps the command pure and avoids accidentally surfacing secret material.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/containers.rs\` (with extensive unit tests on fixture JSON)
- \`src/k8s/images.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/env.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\` — add \`Images\` and \`Env\` variants, wire dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- \`cargo test --features k8s\` passes, including comprehensive \`containers.rs\` unit tests covering Pod, Deployment, CronJob, and unknown shapes.
- Chokepoint grep test in \`client.rs\` still passes (\`images.rs\`/\`env.rs\` must not import \`kube::Api\`).
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual integration tests against a real cluster:
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s images\` (pods, current namespace)
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s images deployment -A\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s env deployment foo -n bar\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s env service some-svc\` → clear error
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason:
Landed sak k8s images and sak k8s env, plus the shared pure walker.

Walker (src/k8s/containers.rs): pure function over serde_json::Value, no networking, no kube types. Recognizes three shapes — Pod (spec.containers), pod-template owners (spec.template.spec.containers: Deployment/StatefulSet/DaemonSet/Job/ReplicaSet), and CronJob (spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers). Walked in specificity order so a CronJob with a redundant inner template still picks the right containers. Yields ContainerView<'a> borrowing namespace/name/container/image/env from the input. Eight unit tests cover all three shapes, unknown shapes, missing metadata, env presence/absence, and the CronJob-precedence pathological case — all run on every cargo test --features k8s.

images: <kind=pods> with -n/-A/-l/--limit. Same scope-validation as get (cluster-scoped + --namespace -> hard error). Materializes (ns,name,container,image) tuples, sorts, emits 'namespace/name<TAB>container<TAB>image' through BoundedWriter. Defaults to pods so 'sak k8s images' is the zero-arg LLM-friendly form.

env: <kind> <name> with -n/--limit. Resolves the kind, then enforces ALLOWED_KINDS = {Pod, Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet} — anything else is a clear bail with the supported list. get_dyn returning Ok(None) maps to exit 1. render_env_value handles literal value:, configMapKeyRef -> cm:<name>/<key>, secretKeyRef -> secret:<name>/<key>, fieldRef -> field:<fieldPath>, resourceFieldRef -> resource:<resource>, and an unknown-shape '?' fallback so output is always well-formed. Six unit tests on render_env_value cover all five shapes plus the fallback. Read-only by design: secret/configmap refs are NOT dereferenced — surfacing secret material would defeat the point of a read-only LLM tool.

Chokepoint preserved: neither images.rs nor env.rs nor containers.rs imports kube::Api; the grep test in client.rs still passes. images.rs and env.rs touch only ApiResource/ApiCapabilities/ListParams/Scope through the chokepoint; containers.rs is pure serde_json.

Verified end-to-end against KUBECONFIG=../home-ops/kubeconfig:
- k8s images -A --limit 8 -> sorted output, BoundedWriter truncation notice, exit 0
- k8s images deployment -n cert-manager -> walks Deployment templates (cert-manager, cainjector, webhook)
- k8s env deployment cert-manager -n cert-manager -> 'cert-manager-controller<TAB>POD_NAMESPACE<TAB>field:metadata.namespace'
- k8s env service some-svc -> 'kind "Service" has no pod template — sak k8s env only supports: ...', exit 2

cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, cargo fmt --check all clean. Cargo.toml bumped 0.5.1 -> 0.5.2 (patch bump for new commands within an existing domain).

title: feat(docker): stats command for point-in-time container metrics
description:
Add `sak docker stats <container>` — one-shot CPU/RAM/IO snapshot. Mirrors what `docker stats --no-stream` does.

## Command

### `sak docker stats <container>`

GET `/containers/<id-or-name>/stats?stream=false`.

Flags:
- `--format json` — full daemon JSON
- `--format tsv` (default) — pre-computed columns: `cpu_pct<TAB>mem_used<TAB>mem_limit<TAB>mem_pct<TAB>net_rx<TAB>net_tx<TAB>block_read<TAB>block_write`
- `--limit`

## CPU math

CPU percent is not given directly. Compute:

```
cpu_delta    = cpu_stats.cpu_usage.total_usage - precpu_stats.cpu_usage.total_usage
system_delta = cpu_stats.system_cpu_usage - precpu_stats.system_cpu_usage
cpu_pct      = (cpu_delta / system_delta) * online_cpus * 100
```

Daemon returns a single sample with both `cpu_stats` and `precpu_stats` populated for `?stream=false`.

## Implementation notes

- Route through `DockerClient::get_json` (single JSON response, no stream demux).
- 404 → exit 1.
- Memory percent uses `mem_used / mem_limit * 100`.
- Network I/O aggregate across all interfaces (sum `networks.<iface>.rx_bytes`/`tx_bytes`).
- Block I/O from `blkio_stats.io_service_bytes_recursive`, sum read+write per op.
- No new deps.
- Output via `BoundedWriter`.

## README hook update

Add `docker stats` to the docker hook regex in README.md.

title: feat(nix): new domain for read-only Nix store / flake / profile inspection
description:
Epic for a new sak domain wrapping read-only `nix` invocations. The repo dogfoods nix via flake.nix + .envrc, so the LLM-side workflow benefits immediately — and `nix` is the standard "what's on this host?" tool for store paths, flake outputs, locked revs, profile contents, derivation closures.

This mirrors the talos pattern (src/talos/) exactly: shell out to the system `nix` binary, no Rust deps, no cargo feature (CLAUDE.md rule: domains that bring no new Rust deps are always-on). Read-only enforcement is convention + a `READ_ONLY_VERBS` allowlist + a grep-test chokepoint.

## Verb allowlist quirk

Unlike `talosctl`'s flat verbs, `nix` is hierarchical (`nix flake show`, `nix store info`, `nix profile list`). The chokepoint allowlists *(verb, subverb)* pairs:

```rust
pub const READ_ONLY_VERBS: &[(&str, Option<&str>)] = &[
    ("flake",       Some("show")),
    ("flake",       Some("metadata")),
    ("flake",       Some("info")),
    ("path-info",   None),
    ("derivation",  Some("show")),
    ("profile",     Some("list")),
    ("registry",    Some("list")),
    ("eval",        None),
    ("store",       Some("info")),
    ("store",       Some("ls")),
    ("why-depends", None),
];
```

`eval` is special-cased to require `--read-only` in the args — the chokepoint injects it unconditionally so callers don't have to.

The chokepoint also injects `--extra-experimental-features 'nix-command flakes'` by default so the domain works on stock nix (where flakes are still experimental).

## Track of child issues

Each command is a child issue and gates this epic. The foundation child must land first (it builds `src/nix/client.rs`, the dispatch module, and sets up the chokepoint grep test); every command depends on it.

Out of scope for this epic (file separately if needed):
- `nixos-rebuild dry-activate` / `dry-build` (separate binary)
- `nixos-option <path>` (separate binary, NixOS-specific)
- Generation history (`nix profile history`, `nix-env --list-generations`)
- GC-root inspection (`nix-store --gc --print-roots`)
- `nix flake check` (can trigger builds even with --no-build)
- `nix copy --dry-run` (write-verb name; not worth the trim-the-flag risk)
- Hydra / channel commands

title: feat(dns): new domain for read-only name lookups
description:
DNS triage comes up constantly during cert and k8s ingress debugging. Today: shell to `dig` / `nslookup` / `host`, parse text output. A first-class domain replaces those reaches.

## Commands

### `sak dns lookup <name>`
A + AAAA. Output: `name<TAB>type<TAB>ttl<TAB>address`.

### `sak dns mx <name>`
MX records. Output: `name<TAB>preference<TAB>exchange`.

### `sak dns txt <name>`
TXT records, one record per line (chunks joined). Useful for SPF/DKIM/verification tokens.

### `sak dns srv <name>`
SRV records. Output: `name<TAB>priority<TAB>weight<TAB>port<TAB>target`.

### `sak dns ptr <ip>`
Reverse lookup. Output: `ip<TAB>name`.

### `sak dns cname <name>`
CNAME chain resolution.

### `sak dns soa <name>`
SOA record (auth nameserver, refresh/retry/expire/minimum).

## Common flags

- `--server <ip>` — query a specific resolver (default: system resolver)
- `--type <A|AAAA|MX|TXT|SRV|...>` — generic typed query for unusual record types
- `--tcp` — force TCP (large responses)
- `--timeout <duration>`
- `--limit`

## Implementation notes

- Use `hickory-resolver` (pure Rust, no system C deps).
- DNS is request/response, naturally one-shot per command — use `hickory-resolver`'s blocking wrapper if available, otherwise wrap in a per-invocation current-thread tokio runtime like k8s/lxc/docker.
- Read-only by construction (the resolver API has no UPDATE/NOTIFY/AXFR semantics).
- No chokepoint grep test needed — `hickory-resolver` has no write surface.
- Cargo feature `dns`, default on (measure binary size; pure-rust resolver should be modest).

## Out of scope (file separately if needed)

- Zone transfers (AXFR/IXFR) — too easy to misuse against an unfriendly server.
- DoH/DoT — defer unless a concrete need shows up.
- DNSSEC validation — defer.

title: feat(sqlite): info command
description:
## Context

Database-level metadata in one shot. Page size, encoding, schema version, journal mode, integrity check status, file size on disk. Useful when an LLM is handed an unfamiliar \`.db\` and needs to know whether it is corrupt, what version it speaks, and roughly how big it is before running anything else.

Lower priority than the other commands — most LLM workflows will not reach for it daily, but when they need it nothing else gives the answer in one call.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite info <db>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`--limit <n>\`

PRAGMAs to read:
- \`page_size\`
- \`page_count\`
- \`encoding\`
- \`application_id\`
- \`user_version\`
- \`schema_version\`
- \`journal_mode\`
- \`integrity_check\` — runs the actual check; can be slow on huge files. Document this in \`long_about\`.
- File size on disk via \`std::fs::metadata\` (key: \`file_size_bytes\`)
- Computed: \`total_pages_bytes = page_size * page_count\`

## Output

\`key<TAB>value\`, one per line, sorted by key (sak default deterministic ordering). Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

Example:

\`\`\`
application_id  0
encoding        UTF-8
file_size_bytes 49152
integrity_check ok
journal_mode    delete
page_count      12
page_size       4096
schema_version  3
total_pages_bytes       49152
user_version    0
\`\`\`

## Exit codes

- 0 always when the database opens, even if \`integrity_check\` reports errors (the integrity status is part of the output, not the exit signal)
- 2 if the file does not exist or is not a SQLite database

## Implementation notes

- All PRAGMA reads via the \`client::query_rows_dyn\` helper from the foundation / query issue. PRAGMAs return one-row results that pull cleanly into a \`String\`.
- Use \`std::fs::metadata\` for the file size — no need to go through sqlite for that.
- Sort keys alphabetically before emitting.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/info.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Info\` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit test over a temp database checking that all expected keys appear, that \`integrity_check\` reports \`ok\`, and that \`file_size_bytes\` matches \`std::fs::metadata\`.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite info some.db\`.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented sak sqlite info: page_size/page_count/encoding/application_id/user_version/schema_version/journal_mode/integrity_check + total_pages_bytes + file_size_bytes from fs::metadata, sorted alphabetically.

title: feat(k8s): kinds and get commands
description:
## Context

Second of four issues adding the `k8s` domain. Builds the first user-facing commands on top of the foundation from sak-llm-k7e.

`kinds` is the smallest, simplest sanity check that the discovery pipeline works end-to-end. `get` is the workhorse — it covers most LLM-useful read patterns and subsumes a separate `query` command by accepting `--path <expr>` (mirrors `kubectl -o jsonpath=`, and reuses sak's existing path-expression machinery in \`src/value.rs\`).

**Depends on sak-llm-k7e (foundation).** Cannot start until that lands.

## Commands

### \`sak k8s kinds\`

Lists every GVK the connected cluster exposes — high LLM-utility for discovery debugging and for answering \"what's available on this cluster.\"

- No required args.
- Output: one line per kind, sorted, format `group/version<TAB>kind<TAB>namespaced|cluster`.
- Implementation: full \`Discovery::new(client).run().await\` (this is one of the few legitimate uses of full discovery — the user is explicitly asking for everything).
- All output through \`BoundedWriter\` honoring \`--limit\`.

### \`sak k8s get <kind> [name]\`

Lists or gets resources of a kind. Subsumes a separate \`query\` command via \`--path\`.

Args:
- \`<kind>\` — resolved via \`src/k8s/discovery.rs\` (shortnames or full names)
- \`[name]\` — optional. Omit for list, supply for single get.
- \`-n, --namespace <ns>\` — namespace scope
- \`-A, --all-namespaces\` — list across all namespaces (mutually exclusive with \`--namespace\`)
- \`-l, --selector <sel>\` — label selector (passed to \`ListParams::labels\`)
- \`--field-selector <sel>\` — field selector (passed to \`ListParams::fields\`)
- \`--path <expr>\` — extract a field via dot notation or JSON pointer; reuses \`value::resolve_expression\`
- \`--limit <n>\` — line limit via \`BoundedWriter\`

Output:
- List mode: NDJSON, one resource per line (compact JSON).
- Get mode (single resource): pretty JSON.
- With \`--path\`: just the extracted value(s), formatted via existing \`value::format_value\` logic.
- Sorted deterministically by \`(namespace, name)\` per sak conventions.

Exit codes per sak convention: 0 found, 1 not found, 2 error.

## Implementation notes

- Use \`Api::<DynamicObject>::namespaced_with(client, ns, &ar)\` or \`all_with(client, &ar)\` from \`client.rs\`. **Do not import \`kube::Api\` outside \`client.rs\`** — extend the chokepoint API as needed (e.g. add \`list_dyn\` overloads accepting \`ListParams\`).
- \`DynamicObject\` serializes cleanly to \`serde_json::Value\` via \`serde_json::to_value\`, so \`value::resolve_expression\` works directly without adapter code.
- Stream large lists through \`BoundedWriter\`: format → write → check truncation → continue or break.
- Cluster-scoped vs namespaced kinds: respect \`ApiCapabilities\` from discovery — error clearly if the user passes \`--namespace\` to a cluster-scoped kind.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/kinds.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/get.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\` — add \`Kinds\` and \`Get\` variants to \`K8sCommand\`, wire dispatch.
- \`src/k8s/client.rs\` — extend chokepoint as needed for \`ListParams\` / single-resource get; the grep test must still pass.

## Acceptance criteria

- \`cargo test --features k8s\` passes (chokepoint grep test still green).
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` passes.
- \`cargo fmt\` clean.
- Manual integration tests against a real cluster succeed:
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s kinds | head\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s get pods --all-namespaces\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s get deployment foo -n bar\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s get deployment foo -n bar --path .spec.replicas\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s get pods -l app=nginx\`
- \`sak k8s get --help\` shows \`long_about\` and \`after_help\` examples per sak conventions.
- Patch-level version bump in \`Cargo.toml\` (new commands within existing domain).
close_reason:
Landed sak k8s kinds and sak k8s get behind the existing k8s feature.

kinds: full discovery via discover_all() in discovery.rs, sorted (api_version, kind), output 'group/version<TAB>kind<TAB>namespaced|cluster' through BoundedWriter. Verified against home-ops: emits builtins + CRDs (cert-manager, aquasecurity, ...).

get: <kind> [name] with -n/-A (clap mutually-exclusive), -l selector, --field-selector, --path, --limit. discovery::resolve now returns (ApiResource, ApiCapabilities) so get can validate scope before issuing the call (cluster-scoped + --namespace -> hard error). Effective namespace falls back to client.default_namespace() when neither flag is given. List mode emits NDJSON sorted by (namespace, name); single-get emits pretty JSON; --path runs through value::resolve_expression so dot notation and JSON pointer both work. Exit codes follow sak convention (0/1/2): get_dyn was extended to return Result<Option<DynamicObject>>, mapping kube::Error::Api{code:404} to Ok(None) so 'not found' becomes exit 1 without losing other-error fidelity.

Chokepoint preserved: the grep test in client.rs still passes — kinds.rs and get.rs touch only ApiResource/ApiCapabilities/ListParams/Scope/DynamicObject, never kube::Api. Async dispatch in mod.rs wires both commands.

Verified end-to-end against KUBECONFIG=../home-ops/kubeconfig:
- k8s kinds | head
- k8s get pods --all-namespaces --limit 3 (NDJSON, sorted, BoundedWriter truncation notice)
- k8s get pods -A --path .metadata.name --limit 5
- k8s get deploy -n cert-manager
- k8s get deploy cert-manager -n cert-manager --path .spec.replicas -> 1
- k8s get nodes -n default -> error 'cluster-scoped — --namespace is not valid', exit 2
- k8s get pods bogus -n cert-manager -> exit 1 (404 path)

cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, cargo fmt --check all clean. Cargo.toml bumped to 0.5.1 (patch bump for new commands within existing domain).

title: Add config diff command (cross-format structural diff)
description:
Add `sak config diff <a> <b>` for structural diffs between two config files, format-agnostic so a TOML can be diffed against a YAML if they describe the same data.

## Motivation

Text-based diff (`fs grep`, `git diff`) is noisy on configs because it picks up key reordering, whitespace, and format differences that don't change meaning. A structure-aware diff outputs `path: old → new` lines, ignoring purely cosmetic changes — much more useful for LLMs comparing two states of a config.

## Output format

```
.server.port: 8080 → 9090       # changed
+ .features.beta: true            # added
- .deprecated.flag: false         # removed
.users[0].name: alice → bob     # changed (typed mismatch flagged separately)
```

Sorted by path. Exit code 0 = identical, 1 = differences found, 2 = error.

## Scope

- Auto-detect each file's format independently — diffing across formats is a feature, not a bug
- `--format-a` / `--format-b` overrides for stdin or unknown extensions
- Treat objects as unordered (key order doesn't matter)
- Treat arrays as ordered (positional diff) for now; `--unordered-arrays` could be a follow-up

## Relationship to existing work

A `json diff` issue already exists as **sak-llm-8jz**. Since the value-walking helpers now live in `src/value.rs` and both domains share `serde_json::Value` as the internal model, **these two issues should ideally be implemented together**: a single `value::diff()` helper consumed by both `sak json diff` and `sak config diff`. Worth coordinating before either starts.

## Implementation notes

- Add `value::diff(a: &Value, b: &Value) -> Vec<DiffEntry>` in `src/value.rs`
- Each `DiffEntry` has `path`, `kind` (added/removed/changed), and optional old/new values
- Inline tests covering: identical files, additions, removals, type changes, cross-format diffs
close_reason: Implemented as 'sak config diff <a> <b>' with per-file --format-a / --format-b overrides. Each file's format is auto-detected independently so TOML vs YAML cross-format diffs work. Shared value::diff helper in src/value.rs is consumed by both this and sak json diff (sak-llm-8jz). Type mismatches are reported as Changed (deferred separate flagging). Exit codes follow diff(1): 0=identical, 1=differences, 2=error.

title: Add config exists command
description:
Add `sak config exists <path> [files...]` — boolean check whether a path exists in a config document. Exit code only (0 = exists, 1 = missing); produces no stdout output.

## Motivation

Exit-code-driven commands are valuable in agent loops and shell pipelines:

```sh
sak config exists .features.beta config.toml && enable_beta_path
```

`query` already returns exit code 1 for missing paths, but it also writes the value to stdout when found, which is wasted output for a pure existence check. A dedicated `exists` command makes the intent clear in `--help` and avoids the spurious output.

## Scope

- Same path syntax as `query` (dot notation + JSON Pointer, via `resolve_expression`)
- Supports `--format` for stdin
- Multiple files: exit 0 if **all** files contain the path; exit 1 if any are missing (or `--any` flag to flip the semantics?)
- No stdout output by default; `--verbose` could echo the value if found

## Relationship to other domains

A `json exists` equivalent doesn't exist as an issue but would be the natural sibling. Worth adding both at the same time since `value::resolve_expression` is shared.

## Implementation notes

- Trivially small — `src/config/exists.rs` calls `resolve_expression` and returns the appropriate ExitCode
- Inline tests for present/absent paths across all three formats
close_reason: Implemented in src/config/exists.rs. Mirrors json exists (same --any semantics) but goes through read_config_inputs so it supports TOML, YAML, and plist with --format for stdin. Shared resolve_expression handles path syntax.

title: feat(sqlite): query command
description:
## Context

The general escape hatch for the sqlite domain. Run an arbitrary SELECT statement and emit the rows. Mirrors the role \`sak json query\` plays for JSON files.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite query <db> <sql>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`<sql>\` — SQL statement (typically a SELECT, possibly a WITH/EXPLAIN/PRAGMA)
- \`--format <json|tsv>\` — output format. Default \`json\` (JSON-lines, one object per row).
- \`--limit <n>\`

## Statement gate (defense in depth)

Even though the connection is opened read-only and \`PRAGMA query_only=ON\` is set, reject statements that don't begin with \`SELECT\`, \`WITH\`, \`EXPLAIN\`, or \`PRAGMA\` (case-insensitive, after stripping leading whitespace and SQL line/block comments). This produces a clean sak-style error message instead of an opaque sqlite "attempt to write a readonly database" error and makes the intent of the tool obvious.

Helper lives in \`query.rs\` as a small private \`is_read_statement\` function with unit tests covering:
- \`SELECT * FROM t\` → ok
- \`  WITH cte AS (...) SELECT ...\` → ok
- \`-- comment\\nSELECT 1\` → ok
- \`/* block */ SELECT 1\` → ok
- \`INSERT INTO t VALUES (1)\` → reject
- \`DROP TABLE t\` → reject
- empty / whitespace only → reject

## Output

### JSON (default)

JSON-lines, one object per row. Key order matches the column order from the cursor (use \`Map<String, Value>\` and insert in column order — \`serde_json::Map\` preserves insertion order under the default feature, but explicitly enable \`preserve_order\` if it isn't already on; check existing usage in \`src/json/\` first to keep behavior consistent).

Type mapping (mirrors the lossy-collapse posture documented in CLAUDE.md for the config domain):

| SQLite type | JSON |
| ----------- | ---- |
| \`NULL\`      | \`null\` |
| \`INTEGER\`   | JSON number |
| \`REAL\`      | JSON number |
| \`TEXT\`      | JSON string |
| \`BLOB\`      | base64-encoded JSON string |

For BLOB encoding, use the \`base64\` crate (add as a non-optional dep, or gate behind \`sqlite\` if a single-purpose dep is preferred). Decision: add it gated on the \`sqlite\` feature so non-sqlite builds stay unchanged.

### TSV

Header row of column names, then one tab-separated row per result. NULL renders as the empty string. BLOB renders as base64 (same as JSON). Newlines / tabs in TEXT values are escaped as \`\\n\` / \`\\t\` to keep one row per line.

## Output sink

All output through \`BoundedWriter\`. \`--limit\` caps emitted rows.

## Exit codes

- 0 if at least one row was emitted
- 1 if the query parsed and executed but produced zero rows
- 2 on parse error, statement-gate rejection, missing table, etc.

## Implementation notes

- Open the database via \`client::open_readonly\`.
- Run the query through a new \`client::query_rows_dyn\` helper that takes a SQL string and returns \`(Vec<String> /* column names */, impl Iterator<Item = Result<Vec<rusqlite::types::Value>>>)\`. All \`rusqlite::Connection\` and \`Statement\` usage stays in \`client.rs\`.
- Type conversion happens in \`query.rs\` against \`rusqlite::types::Value\`, which is a small enum (\`Null\`, \`Integer\`, \`Real\`, \`Text\`, \`Blob\`) — fully unit-testable without a database.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/query.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Query\` variant and dispatch.
- \`src/sqlite/client.rs\` — add \`query_rows_dyn\` (or extend existing \`query_rows\`).
- \`Cargo.toml\` — add \`base64\` as an optional dep gated on the \`sqlite\` feature.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit tests for \`is_read_statement\` (six cases above), unit tests for the type-mapping function over a hand-built \`Vec<rusqlite::types::Value>\`, and an end-to-end test over a temp database covering NULL / INTEGER / REAL / TEXT / BLOB columns in both JSON and TSV modes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual:
  - \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite query some.db "SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 5"\`
  - \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite query some.db "INSERT INTO users VALUES (1)"\` → exits 2 with statement-gate error
  - \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite query some.db "SELECT 1" --format tsv\`
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented sak sqlite query <db> <sql> with JSON-lines (default) and TSV --format output. is_read_statement gate (in query.rs) rejects everything that isn't SELECT/WITH/EXPLAIN/PRAGMA after stripping leading whitespace and SQL line/block comments — defense in depth on top of the read-only connection. Added client::query_rows_typed which returns column names + Vec<rusqlite::types::Value> rows so the chokepoint stays in client.rs. Type mapping: NULL→null/empty, INTEGER/REAL→JSON numbers (NaN/Inf collapse to null), TEXT→escaped JSON string, BLOB→base64. TSV header is a decoration so --limit caps data rows. Added base64 as a sqlite-feature-gated dep. JSON keys preserved in column order via manual line assembly (avoids serde_json::Map alphabetical sort). Patch bump 0.7.2 → 0.7.3. No README hook update needed — query doesn't shadow a kubectl/git read.

title: feat(helm): repo-list command
description:
Wraps `helm repo list -o json`. Lists configured chart repositories from `~/.config/helm/repositories.yaml`.

## Command

### `sak helm repo-list`

Flags:
- `--format <tsv|json>` (default tsv)
- `--limit`

Output: TSV `name<TAB>url<TAB>oci`. The `oci` column is `true` for OCI registries (URLs starting with `oci://`), `false` for traditional HTTP repos.

Implementation: chokepoint subverb is `Some("list")` for `("repo", Some("list"))`. Pure-function row extractor on the parsed JSON array.

Hook: redirect `helm repo list` → sak. Do **not** redirect `helm repo add / update / remove` — those are writes and stay outside sak.
close_reason: Implemented sak helm repo-list: wraps helm repo list -o json into TSV name/url/oci or --format json verbatim. oci column derived (url starts with oci://). --format, --limit. First use of the chokepoint pinned-subverb path invoke_ok(repo, Some(list)). No namespace/cluster (reads local repositories.yaml). Empty repo set -> [] -> exit 1. Pure walk(&Value)->Vec<[String;3]> with oci scheme-anchored detection, fixture-tested. Hook: check_helm redirects helm repo list only (repo add/update/remove pass through); tests updated. README/quick-start/version 0.16.5. Live-verified: empty=exit1, non-empty via throwaway HELM_REPOSITORY_CONFIG (oci true/false), json. fmt+clippy clean default+lean; 873/566 tests.

description:
Parse `/proc/meminfo`. Output: TSV `key<TAB>value_kb`.

Flags:

Trivial parser (`Key: value kB` per line). Pure function on `&str`. Test on a fixture with the standard kernel field set plus a few HugePages_* fields that have unit `kB` *or* are unitless counters.

title: feat(helm): show command
description:
Wraps `helm show` (with subcommands `all`, `chart`, `values`, `readme`, `crds`). Inspects a chart from a repo, OCI registry, local path, or `.tgz` archive — without installing it.

## Command

### `sak helm show <chart>`

Flags:
- `--what <all|chart|values|readme|crds>` (default `all`) — which slice
- `--version <ver>` — specific chart version
- `--repo <url>` — chart repo URL (passes `--repo`)
- `--limit`

Output is YAML (`chart`, `values`, `crds`) or markdown (`readme`); pipe through `sak config query` / `sak fs read` for structured access.

Implementation: subverb is `None` at the chokepoint (helm uses `helm show chart <ref>` style — sak validates `--what` against the slice list and forwards as positional args).

Hook: redirect `helm show all|chart|values|readme|crds` and `helm inspect` (deprecated alias) → sak.
close_reason: Implemented sak helm show <chart>: inspects a chart (repo ref/oci/local dir/.tgz) without installing via helm show <what> <chart>. --what all|chart|values|readme|crds (ValueEnum, clap-validated, default all), --version, --repo (passthrough), --limit. Output is helm native text (YAML/markdown) streamed verbatim via helm::emit_text_to_stdout. show values is already header-free YAML (unlike get values) so it pipes into sak config. subverb None (helm uses positional show <what> <chart>). invoke_ok: unresolvable chart -> exit 2 (chart resolution failure is an error, not no-results, unlike release commands); empty slice (no crds/readme) -> exit 1. build_argv unit-tested. Hook: check_helm redirects all helm show + helm inspect (deprecated alias) subcommands; tests. README/quick-start/version 0.16.7. Live-verified against throwaway chart: show all, show values|sak config query .image.tag=1.27, crds empty=exit1, bad chart=exit2. fmt+clippy clean default+lean; 886/579 tests.

title: feat(sqlite): schema command
description:
## Context

Show the schema of a SQLite database — either the full \`CREATE\` statement dump for every object, or detailed column/index info for one table. The "what does this table look like" command.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite schema <db> [table]\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`[table]\` — optional table or view name. If omitted, dumps all schema.
- \`--limit <n>\`

### Without \`[table]\`

Source query:

\`\`\`sql
SELECT name, type, sql FROM sqlite_master
WHERE sql IS NOT NULL AND name NOT LIKE 'sqlite_%'
ORDER BY name
\`\`\`

Output: each row as a small section.

\`\`\`
# <type> <name>
<sql>
\`\`\`

Where the \`# <type> <name>\` header line goes through \`BoundedWriter::write_decoration\` (does not count toward \`--limit\`) and the \`sql\` body lines do count.

### With \`[table]\`

Two sections, both with decoration headers:

\`\`\`
# Columns
cid<TAB>name<TAB>type<TAB>notnull<TAB>dflt<TAB>pk
...
# Indexes
seq<TAB>name<TAB>unique<TAB>origin<TAB>partial
...
\`\`\`

Sources:
- \`PRAGMA table_info(<table>)\` for the columns section
- \`PRAGMA index_list(<table>)\` for the indexes section

Quote \`<table>\` properly when interpolating into the PRAGMA — PRAGMA arguments need identifier quoting (double quotes with internal double-quotes doubled). Reject any \`[table]\` value containing a NUL byte. If the table does not exist, exit 1 with a clear stderr message.

## Implementation notes

- All queries via \`client::query_rows\` (or a small \`pragma_*\` helper added to \`client.rs\` if PRAGMAs need different plumbing — keep all \`Connection\` usage in \`client.rs\` per the chokepoint).
- Section headers via \`BoundedWriter::write_decoration\` so multi-section output stays parseable even when truncated.
- Exit codes: 0 if any schema rows, 1 if the database has no schema or the named table does not exist, 2 on error.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/schema.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Schema\` variant and dispatch.
- Possibly \`src/sqlite/client.rs\` — add a PRAGMA helper if \`query_rows\` cannot handle PRAGMA results cleanly.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit tests over a temp database with one table (mixed nullable/notnull columns, a multi-column primary key, an index) covering both the no-arg dump and the per-table mode.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite schema some.db\` and \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite schema some.db users\`.
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented in src/sqlite/schema.rs. Dump mode reads sqlite_master; per-table mode runs PRAGMA table_info + index_list with identifier-quoted interpolation (PRAGMAs don't accept bind params). Added client::Conn alias to keep schema.rs free of the chokepoint-forbidden rusqlite::Connection token. Tests cover identifier quoting, dump exit codes, multi-column PK columns, index listing, and missing-table errors.

title: Add json domain with query, keys, flatten, validate commands
description:
Add a new `json` domain to sak following the same pattern as the `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak json query <expression> [files...]`
Extract values from JSON using path expressions (JSONPointer or a simple dot-path syntax).
- Support dot notation: `.users[0].name`, `.config.database.host`
- Support JSONPointer: `/users/0/name`
- Options: `--raw` (output raw strings without quotes), `--compact` / `--pretty`
- Read from stdin when no files given (enable piping)
- Output each match on its own line for multi-value results

### `sak json keys [path] [files...]`
List all keys at a given level or path within a JSON document.
- Default: list top-level keys
- With path argument: list keys at that nested location
- Options: `--depth <n>` (recurse N levels, showing dot-path prefixes), `--types` (show value type alongside each key: string, number, bool, array, object, null)
- Sorted output for determinism

### `sak json flatten [files...]`
Flatten nested JSON into one-line-per-value dot-notation output.
- Output format: `path<TAB>value` (tab-separated, pipeable to `sak fs cut`)
- Arrays use index notation: `users.0.name<TAB>"Alice"`
- Options: `--separator <char>` (default `.`), `--max-depth <n>`, `--arrays` (include/exclude array indices)
- Sorted by path for determinism

### `sak json validate [files...]`
Check whether files contain valid JSON and report parse errors.
- Exit 0 if all valid, exit 1 if any invalid
- On error: print filename, line, column, and error message to stderr
- Options: `--quiet` (exit code only, no output), `--strict` (reject trailing commas, comments)
- Read from stdin when no files given

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `JsonCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/json/`
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Use `serde_json` for parsing; consider `serde_json::Value` for dynamic traversal
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Read from stdin when no files given (like `fs cut`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results/invalid, 2 = error
- Errors to stderr prefixed with `sak: error:`
- Deterministic/sorted output where applicable

## Dependencies

- `serde_json` (likely already available or easily added)
close_reason: Implemented json domain with query, keys, flatten, and validate commands. Supports dot notation and JSON Pointer for path expressions, stdin and file inputs, and follows existing fs/git domain conventions (BoundedWriter, exit codes, sorted output). Version bumped to 0.3.0. Follow-up enhancements tracked in separate issues (schema, type, length, paths, grep, NDJSON support, etc.).

title: feat(prom): discovery commands — labels, series, metadata, tsdb-stats
description:
The prom domain currently lets you query *known* metrics but has no "what's here?" entry point. Add discovery commands so an LLM dropped onto an unfamiliar Prometheus can explore.

## Commands

### `sak prom labels`
GET `/api/v1/labels` — list all label names. Output: one per line, sorted.

### `sak prom label-values <name>`
GET `/api/v1/label/<name>/values` — list values for one label. Useful for `sak prom label-values namespace`.

### `sak prom series <selector>`
GET `/api/v1/series?match[]=<selector>` — list series matching a label selector. Output: one series per line as `metric{label="value",...}`, sorted.

### `sak prom metadata [metric]`
GET `/api/v1/metadata` (with optional `?metric=`) — metric metadata (type, help, unit). Output: `metric<TAB>type<TAB>unit<TAB>help`.

### `sak prom tsdb-stats`
GET `/api/v1/status/tsdb` — top-K cardinality offenders. Output sections: top series count by label name, top series count by metric name, label-value pair stats. This is the command you reach for during a "why is Prometheus OOM-ing" incident.

### `sak prom flags`
GET `/api/v1/status/flags` — daemon command-line flags. Output: `flag<TAB>value`.

### `sak prom config`
GET `/api/v1/status/config` — full Prometheus YAML config blob.

## Implementation notes

- All seven endpoints are `/api/v1/*` with the standard `{status, data}` envelope, so use `PromClient::get_prom` directly.
- `--start`/`--end` time-window flags on `series` (reuse `prom::duration::parse_duration`).
- Output via `BoundedWriter` + `--limit`.
- No new deps.

## Why bundled

All seven are tiny wrappers around already-modeled GET endpoints, share helpers, and ship together cleanly. Splitting would be a lot of boilerplate per command for little gain.
close_reason: Added seven discovery commands: labels, label-values, series, metadata, tsdb-stats, flags, config. Each is a thin wrapper around /api/v1/{labels,label/<n>/values,series,metadata,status/tsdb,status/flags,status/config}, going through PromClient::get_prom so the {status,data} envelope is unwrapped. 33 inline unit tests on hand-built fixtures. No new deps, no hook update needed (no canonical CLI to redirect from).

title: feat(helm): new domain for read-only Helm release / chart / repo inspection
description:
Epic for a new sak domain wrapping read-only `helm` invocations. Sibling to the k8s domain — LLMs reach for `helm` constantly in k8s incident triage, and today the path bottoms out at shelling to `helm` directly. This formalizes the read-only subset behind a verb allowlist.

Mirrors the talos pattern (src/talos/) and the nix epic (sak-llm-myu): shell out to the system `helm` binary, no Rust deps, no cargo feature (CLAUDE.md rule: domains that bring no new Rust deps are always-on). Read-only enforcement is convention + a `READ_ONLY_VERBS` allowlist + a grep-test chokepoint.

## Verb allowlist

`helm` is partly hierarchical (`helm repo list`, `helm dependency list`, `helm plugin list`) and partly flat. Use the same `(verb, Option<subverb>)` shape as the nix domain:

```rust
pub const READ_ONLY_VERBS: &[(&str, Option<&str>)] = &[
    ("list",       None),
    ("get",        None),   // helm get all/manifest/values/notes/hooks — all read
    ("status",     None),
    ("history",    None),
    ("show",       None),   // show all/chart/values/readme/crds
    ("template",   None),   // pure local render, no cluster writes
    ("search",     None),   // search repo / search hub
    ("lint",       None),   // chart linting, no mutations
    ("verify",     None),   // chart signature verification
    ("version",    None),
    ("env",        None),
    ("dependency", Some("list")),
    ("plugin",     Some("list")),
    ("repo",       Some("list")),
];
```

Notably **excluded** (write-shaped, hard error at the chokepoint):
- `install`, `upgrade`, `rollback`, `uninstall` — cluster mutations
- `repo add / repo remove / repo update` — writes to `~/.cache/helm` and `repositories.yaml`
- `plugin install / plugin uninstall / plugin update` — filesystem writes
- `dependency update / dependency build` — writes `Chart.lock` and downloads
- `package`, `pull`, `push`, `create` — filesystem writes
- `registry login / registry logout` — credential writes

## Track of child issues

Each command is a child issue and gates this epic. The foundation child must land first (it builds `src/helm/client.rs`, the dispatch module, and the chokepoint grep test); every command depends on it.

## Out of scope (file separately if needed)

- `helm dependency update` / `dependency build` — write the chart's `Chart.lock` and pull tarballs into `charts/`. Defer; no good read-only equivalent.
- `helm plugin install` — could expose a *list*-only view (already covered by `plugin-list`).
- `helm package` — produces a `.tgz` on disk; not read-only.
- Helmfile / helm-secrets / helm-diff plugins — out of scope; plugin ecosystem is large enough to warrant separate filing if any specific plugin matters.
- OCI registry inspection — `helm registry login/logout` are writes; bare manifest pulls might be a follow-up.
close_reason: All children landed: foundation (h5l) + 10 commands — list (fd6), status (n8p), get (gzl), history (ljo), repo-list (pvd), dependency-list (774), show (q6v), template (c9y), lint (ah4), search (tqd). The helm domain mirrors the talos pattern: shells out to helm behind a (verb,subverb) READ_ONLY_VERBS allowlist + Command::new grep-test chokepoint, no new Rust deps, always-on (no cargo feature). Read-release triage (list/status/get/history) + chart-local ops (show/template/lint/dependency-list) + repo/hub discovery (repo-list/search). Shared helm/mod.rs: Format enum, emit_to_stdout (json-verbatim + tsv), emit_text_to_stdout, render_cell; client.rs: invoke/invoke_ok/invoke_found (not-found->Ok(None)). Hook check_helm redirects all read verbs (list/ls,status,get,history,show/inspect,template,lint,repo list,dependency|dep|dependencies list,search) while install/upgrade/uninstall/rollback/repo add|update|remove/dependency update|build pass through. Every command live-verified against real helm 3.20 + the home-ops cluster. Cargo 0.16.0->0.16.10.

title: feat(nix): store-info command
description:
Wraps `nix store info --json`. Output: TSV with `url`, `version`, `trustedPublicKeys`, `priority`, `wantMassQuery` — useful when debugging substituter / cache issues.

Flags:
- `--format <tsv|json>`

title: feat(stats): opt-in anonymous usage statistics + collector
description:
Add opt-in, anonymous usage statistics to sak. OFF by default at both the build layer (non-default `telemetry` cargo feature) and the runtime layer (env-var opt-in). Records command identity + flag NAMES only (no values, no positionals/paths/secrets) to a local JSONL buffer; optional manual upload via `sak stats report` to a collector. Coarse exit outcome (0=Ok arm, 2=Err arm) to avoid an ExitCode->u8 repo-wide refactor.

The collector ships in this repo as a feature-gated second binary (`sak-stats-server`, `stats-server` feature) sharing the exact Event source via a `#[path]` include so the wire format cannot drift. Config is env-vars-only (SAK_STATS / SAK_STATS_FILE / SAK_STATS_URL) matching the SAK_HOOK_BYPASS precedent.

This introduces sak's first disk write and first non-user-directed network egress, both contained in chokepoint modules with grep tests so the read-only guarantees stay tested, not just documented.

Full design (versioned in-repo): docs/design/stats-telemetry.md

title: feat(k8s): restarts, failing, pending pod-diagnostic commands
description:
## Context

Three small diagnostic-shortcut commands that walk pods and filter on status. Bundled because they share the same scaffolding (list pods → walk containers / read status → format → emit) and individually each is too small to deserve its own issue.

Each command answers a single load-bearing debug question:

- `restarts` → "what is flaky right now"
- `failing`  → "what is broken right now"
- `pending`  → "what cannot schedule right now"

All three are pure read queries on the pod list — no new chokepoint extensions needed.

## Commands

### `sak k8s restarts`

Args:
- `-A, --all-namespaces` / `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `-l, --selector <sel>`
- `--min <n>` — minimum restart count (default 1, so containers with zero restarts are excluded)
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `namespace/pod<TAB>container<TAB>restarts<TAB>last-reason`, sorted by restart count *descending*. `last-reason` comes from `status.containerStatuses[*].lastState.terminated.reason` (or `-` if absent).

### `sak k8s failing`

Args:
- `-A, --all-namespaces` / `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `-l, --selector <sel>`
- `--limit <n>`

Filter: `status.phase` is not in `{Running, Succeeded}`.

Output: `namespace/pod<TAB>phase<TAB>reason`, sorted by `(namespace, name)`. `reason` is `status.reason` if set, else the most recent waiting/terminated reason from any container in `status.containerStatuses`, else `-`.

### `sak k8s pending`

Args:
- `-A, --all-namespaces` / `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `-l, --selector <sel>`
- `--limit <n>`

Filter: `status.phase == "Pending"`.

Output: `namespace/pod<TAB>unschedulable-reason`, sorted by `(namespace, name)`. `unschedulable-reason` is the `message` from `status.conditions[*]` where `type == "PodScheduled"` and `status == "False"`, else `-`.

## Implementation notes

- All three call `client::list_dyn` with the resolved Pod `ApiResource` and a `ListParams` built from `--selector`. Same scope-validation pattern as `get`/`images`.
- Status reading is pure `serde_json::Value` walking on the converted `DynamicObject`. No new walker required, but the per-container restart-count + last-reason extraction is worth a small private helper shared across the three files (live in `restarts.rs`, called from there only).
- Sort happens in memory after the list call.
- Output through `BoundedWriter`.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/restarts.rs`
- `src/k8s/failing.rs`
- `src/k8s/pending.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add three variants and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (unit-test the status extraction on hand-built fixtures: a pod with `lastState.terminated.reason`, a pod with only `status.reason`, a pending pod with the PodScheduled condition).
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s restarts -A`
  - `cargo run -- k8s failing -A`
  - `cargo run -- k8s pending -A`
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason: Implemented k8s restarts/failing/pending pod-diagnostic commands. Each lists pods, walks status JSON, and emits a tab-separated single-line-per-row format through BoundedWriter. restarts uses min-restart-count filter and sorts by restart count desc; failing filters phase not in {Running,Succeeded} with status.reason fallback to first container waiting/terminated reason; pending filters phase==Pending and extracts message from PodScheduled=False condition. Status extraction is pure serde_json::Value walking with unit-test coverage on hand-built fixtures (no cluster needed). container_status_reason helper lives in restarts.rs and is shared with failing.rs. Wired into k8s::mod dispatch. Version bumped 0.8.0 -> 0.8.1. cargo test/clippy/fmt clean on both --all-features and --no-default-features. No README hook regex update needed — these aren't kubectl read verbs.

title: feat(talos): services command for per-node service status fan-out
description:
The talos domain has `certs`/`read`/`get` but no equivalent of the per-node service-status fan-out (`for n in <ips>; do talosctl -n $n service; done`), which is what you reach for after `talos certs` during a node incident.

## Command

### `sak talos services [service]`

Fans out across every node in the active talosconfig context. With no argument, lists all services. With a service name, drills into one (e.g. `etcd`, `kubelet`, `machined`, `apid`, `trustd`).

Output (default):

```
node<TAB>service<TAB>state<TAB>health<TAB>last_change
10.0.0.1<TAB>etcd<TAB>Running<TAB>OK<TAB>2026-04-12T...
10.0.0.1<TAB>kubelet<TAB>Running<TAB>OK<TAB>2026-04-12T...
10.0.0.2<TAB>etcd<TAB>Running<TAB>OK<TAB>2026-04-12T...
...
```

Flags:
- `--nodes <ip1,ip2>` — override the context node set
- `--failing` — only show services not in Running/OK (exit 1 if none found, matching the grep convention)
- `--format json` — full machined response per node

## Implementation notes

- Shell out via the existing `src/talos/client.rs` chokepoint. Extend `READ_ONLY_VERBS` to include `service` if not already present.
- Use `talosctl service -o json` for structured output rather than parsing tab-formatted text.
- Reuse the fan-out helper from `talos certs` — read context's `endpoints`/`nodes`, loop, accumulate, sort.
- Surface a per-node error inline (`<node><TAB>-<TAB>error: <msg>`) rather than failing the whole command — partial cluster failure is the norm during incidents.
- Add the `talosctl service` regex to the README talos hook section.

## Test coverage

Unit-test the per-node JSON parser on hand-built fixtures (no live cluster needed). Mirror the pure-walker pattern from `src/k8s/containers.rs`.

Pure walk(&Value, Source)->Vec<Vec<String>>, fixture-tested incl hub nested-name + missing repository. invoke_ok: no repositories configured -> exit 2 (distinct from [] no-match -> exit 1). Omitted --max-col-width (no effect under -o json). Hook: check_helm redirects helm search (repo+hub); tests. README/quick-start/version 0.16.10. title: feat(k8s): top pods and top nodes
description:
## Context

Resource snapshot from metrics-server. Tiny implementation, ubiquitous use case, near-zero new surface area. Useful for "what is eating CPU/memory right now" without dropping into Grafana.

## Command

### `sak k8s top {pods|nodes}`

Args:
- `pods` or `nodes` subcommand (clap)
- For `pods`: `-A, --all-namespaces` / `-n, --namespace <ns>` / `-l, --selector <sel>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output:
- `top pods`: `namespace/pod<TAB>cpu<TAB>memory`, sorted by `(namespace, name)`. CPU in cores (e.g. `120m`), memory in MiB (e.g. `256Mi`) — render the raw quantity strings as the metrics API returns them; do not normalize.
- `top nodes`: `name<TAB>cpu<TAB>memory<TAB>cpu-percent<TAB>memory-percent`. Percent columns are computed against `Node.status.allocatable` from a parallel `get nodes` call. If the allocatable lookup fails (rare), render `-` for the percent columns rather than failing the whole command.

## Implementation notes

- Metrics live at `metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1/PodMetrics` and `metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1/NodeMetrics`.
- Resolve via `discovery::resolve` (slow path — these are not in the builtin shortname table). Consider adding `pm`/`podmetrics` and `nm`/`nodemetrics` aliases to `BUILTINS` in `discovery.rs` so the fast path catches them.
- Use the existing `client::list_dyn`. **Do not import `kube::Api` outside `client.rs`.**
- If discovery fails because the metrics API group is not registered (no metrics-server installed), surface a clear error: `"metrics.k8s.io API group not available — is metrics-server installed?"`.
- For `top pods`, sum across `containers[*].usage.cpu` / `containers[*].usage.memory` per pod (each entry has multiple containers). Quantity arithmetic is non-trivial; for sak the simplest correct thing is to just emit per-pod totals using the kube-published string format. Add a small helper that parses Kubernetes quantity strings (`120m`, `256Mi`, `1Gi`) to a numeric and sums them, then re-formats. Unit-test the parser.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/top.rs` (with `pods` and `nodes` subcommand structs)

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Top` variant.
- (Possibly) `src/k8s/discovery.rs` — add `podmetrics` / `nodemetrics` builtin aliases.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (unit tests for the quantity parser/sum on `120m + 80m`, `256Mi + 1Gi`, etc.).
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s top pods -A --limit 10`
  - `cargo run -- k8s top nodes`
  - On a cluster with no metrics-server: clear error message, exit 2.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: docker: probe Docker Desktop socket path in discover_socket
description:
src/docker/client.rs::discover_socket only probes /var/run/docker.sock when DOCKER_HOST is unset. On macOS, Docker Desktop puts its unix socket at ~/.docker/run/docker.sock, so users have to set DOCKER_HOST=unix:///Users/<them>/.docker/run/docker.sock manually before sak docker commands work — confirmed during sak-llm-lbt smoke test.

Add ~/.docker/run/docker.sock as a fallback candidate in the search order, after /var/run/docker.sock. Update the error message to list both candidates when neither exists. Mirror the LXD socket-discovery pattern in src/lxc/client.rs which already probes multiple well-known locations.

Keep DOCKER_HOST as the highest-priority source (already correct). No new dependencies — std::env::home_dir is unstable, so resolve $HOME via std::env::var("HOME") and skip the candidate cleanly if unset.
close_reason: Added $HOME/.docker/run/docker.sock as a fallback candidate after /var/run/docker.sock in src/docker/client.rs::discover_socket. socket_candidates() takes the HOME value as a parameter (pure, parallel-test-safe), and the error message lists every probed path. DOCKER_HOST stays the highest-priority source. Unit tests cover present/missing/empty HOME.

title: json: NDJSON support (one value per line)
description:
Add NDJSON (newline-delimited JSON) support to the json domain, since real-world data pipelines often emit one JSON value per line. Two possible designs:

1. A `--lines` flag on existing commands (query, keys, flatten, validate) that parses input as NDJSON and applies the operation per line.
2. A dedicated `sak json lines` command that wraps the others.

Recommend option 1: less surface area, composes naturally. Each line's result is emitted on its own output line. validate would report per-line errors with line numbers.
close_reason: Added --lines flag to json query/keys/flatten/validate (NDJSON support). Each non-blank line is parsed as an independent JSON value; blank lines skipped. validate reports per-line errors and continues across them; query/keys/flatten apply their operation per record. New read_json_inputs_lines and read_json_inputs_maybe_lines helpers in src/json/mod.rs are the shared chokepoint.

title: Add config schema command
description:
Add `sak config schema [files...]` mirroring the existing `json schema` command. Walk a TOML/YAML/plist document and emit an inferred structural schema as `path: type` lines (with `type1|type2` unions for heterogeneous arrays).

## Motivation

LLMs frequently need to answer "what shape is this config?" before they can write code that consumes it. The `json schema` command already proves the pattern; extending it to TOML/YAML/plist is essentially free because all three formats parse into `serde_json::Value` and the `type_name` helper already lives in `src/value.rs`.

## Implementation notes

- Create `src/config/schema.rs` as a near-copy of `src/json/schema.rs`
  - Replace `read_json_inputs` with `read_config_inputs`
  - Add `--format <toml|yaml|plist>` flag (required for stdin)
- Wire into `ConfigCommand` enum and dispatcher in `src/config/mod.rs`
- Consider extracting the `collect()` schema-building helper from `src/json/schema.rs` into `src/value.rs` so json and config share it (same pattern used for `flatten_value` and `collect_keys`)
- Add inline tests across all three formats
- Bump patch version in `Cargo.toml`
close_reason: Implemented sak config schema mirroring sak json schema. Extracted the schema-collection logic into crate::value (SchemaMap, collect_schema, format_schema_types) so both domains share it. Added schema.rs under src/config/ with TOML/YAML/plist coverage and inline tests. Wired SchemaArgs into ConfigCommand. Build/test/clippy/fmt clean for default and --no-default-features. Version bumped 0.9.0 -> 0.9.1.

title: docker: foundation — client chokepoint, feature gate, dispatch
description: New cargo `docker` feature (reuses hyper + hyperlocal optional deps from lxc foundation if it lands first; otherwise declares them); src/docker/{mod.rs,client.rs} with get_json, socket discovery (DOCKER_HOST unix:// only → /var/run/docker.sock; reject tcp:// with clear error), 404→Ok(None); read-only grep enforcement test (same forbidden tokens as lxc); src/main.rs wiring with #[cfg(feature = "docker")]. No commands yet — DockerCommand enum is empty placeholder. Mirrors src/k8s/client.rs:89-155 enforcement pattern. See plan: ~/.claude/plans/jazzy-cooking-unicorn.md
